Sem,

I believe Lauren is 100% right on this. Please keep us posted as you start implementing this.

### Sem — 2012-06-18T05:25:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31884>

Thank you Lauren and Destiny. I actually have started implementing much of this. Both my boys have been 100% gluten free for the past two months. I've cut them way back on sugar and processed foods. I make them eat lots of protein and healthy fats like CO, pastured butter, ghee, etc. I even started them on sauerkraut this week (although I can only get them to choke down about one spoonful). I've made bone broth and my own yogurt. They take lots of supplements including probiotics, FCLO +butter oil, fish oil, cal/mag citrate and a multi almost every day. My biggest problem is that my older son has been on ADHD meds and when I try to take him off he gets really anxious so I am not sure what to do about that. The doctor is not at all helpful because he thinks he should be on them and wants to increase the dose! It's the same basic issue I am having trying to get off of anti depressants! Please let me know what else you guys think I can do. I SO appreciate your opinions and do not get offended at all. I'm here for help and know there's more I can do but just not exactly sure what it is. And it's hard to get real support in the outside world.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-18T05:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31890>

Sem, sounds like they have a good start. You're doing goood. Can they do any swimming? It's a fun way to CT.

### Sem — 2012-06-18T07:09:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31896>

Yes, we've been swimming a lot. However most of the pools are heated! The only pool that's not heated is at my mom's house because her heater blew out. I was so happy!  She lives across the street from me so we go there a lot. I might try face dunking with him too. He might get a kick out of that!

### Destiny — 2012-06-18T13:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31904>

> **Sem;31990 wrote:** Thank you Lauren and Destiny. I actually have started implementing much of this. Both my boys have been 100% gluten free for the past two months. I've cut them way back on sugar and processed foods. I make them eat lots of protein and healthy fats like CO, pastured butter, ghee, etc. I even started them on sauerkraut this week (although I can only get them to choke down about one spoonful). I've made bone broth and my own yogurt. They take lots of supplements including probiotics, FCLO +butter oil, fish oil, cal/mag citrate and a multi almost every day. My biggest problem is that my older son has been on ADHD meds and when I try to take him off he gets really anxious so I am not sure what to do about that. The doctor is not at all helpful because he thinks he should be on them and wants to increase the dose! It's the same basic issue I am having trying to get off of anti depressants! Please let me know what else you guys think I can do. I SO appreciate your opinions and do not get offended at all. I'm here for help and know there's more I can do but just not exactly sure what it is. And it's hard to get real support in the outside world.

You are so right that it is hard to get real support in the outside medical world. That is why we all flock to this site for help!

You already started and that is great. You need to patiently continue. Some days it will be two steps back but it is important to keep going. I got discouraged so many times but did not give up. This takes time, just keep going.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-19T00:17:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32015>

> **Sem;31990 wrote:** Thank you Lauren and Destiny. I actually have started implementing much of this. Both my boys have been 100% gluten free for the past two months. I've cut them way back on sugar and processed foods. I make them eat lots of protein and healthy fats like CO, pastured butter, ghee, etc. I even started them on sauerkraut this week (although I can only get them to choke down about one spoonful). I've made bone broth and my own yogurt. They take lots of supplements including probiotics, FCLO +butter oil, fish oil, cal/mag citrate and a multi almost every day. My biggest problem is that my older son has been on ADHD meds and when I try to take him off he gets really anxious so I am not sure what to do about that. The doctor is not at all helpful because he thinks he should be on them and wants to increase the dose! It's the same basic issue I am having trying to get off of anti depressants! Please let me know what else you guys think I can do. I SO appreciate your opinions and do not get offended at all. I'm here for help and know there's more I can do but just not exactly sure what it is. And it's hard to get real support in the outside world.

I'm with you totally on how hard it is to get real support. A couple of suggestions I'd be thinking of. One is obvious: have a consult with Dr K re: your son. He fixed his own kids and he clearly knows miles about this stuff that we're only beginning to learn about. And your son could be on the call, too, so it would be inclusive. I remember Cavemam saying how helpful Dr K was in explaining things to her son.

Another suggestion it to address the underlying anxiety. You say that 'he gets really anxious' when you 'try to take him off' his meds. When you say 'try' does that mean he comes off for a while and then anxiety goes up? Or he gets anxious at the thought of it? You say also that it's the same when you 'try to get off anti depressants." Julia Ross and Dan Kalish both talk a lot about using natural means to deal with mood dysfunction and I believe Julia talks specifically about starting things like 5HTP and Tryptophan while still on the meds. And I know that Kalish has said that kids can safely take low doses of this stuff. I work a lot with children (and my own son had learning/attention/mood issues). The problem with staying on the meds is that they are doing something major to the brain and not usually in a good way. There is really no way to see if your dietary changes are working while you still put stimulants like Ritalin into his body. But I'm not quite clear what is causing the anxiety - the thought of it or the actuality of it - so maybe you can give some more info there.

I've also noticed that when we supplement our kids like crazy it can create the same effect as when we put them on meds... they feel really 'damaged' and like something is majorly wrong. I think it's really critical that kids get most of what they need from food and that they learn to enjoy their meals (even if everything they eat isn't their favourite). We all know how it feels to feel 'broken' and constantly worried about all we need to fix. I don't think we want our kids to feel that way. (I'm not saying yours do, just thinking out lout about my own experience.)

Lauren

### Sem — 2012-06-19T00:36:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32020>

Hi Lauren,

Thanks for taking the time to respond. I hadn't thought of doing a consult for my son. That's a great idea. Would I need to do labs for him first? Not sure what kind of labs you need to get for kids.

As for the meds, he doesn't even really know what they are for. He just thinks they are another vitamin. What I was referring to is his tendency towards anxiety and obsessive thought patterns. This seems to go up rather dramatically when I have taken him off the meds. I have dramatically decreased them since school has gotten out. He is definitely hyper and kind of moody but I haven't seen a bug spike in the anxiety so we will see.

I have read a lot about the 5-htp and tryptophan and was thinking of trying those. Maybe I will try to get a consult with Dr. Kalish too?

As for getting most of our supplements from food, I couldn't agree more but I don't think my kids are there yet. LOL. The spoonful of sauerkraut is still a major ordeal! I made them eat some salmon the other day and you would have thought I was subjecting them to water boarding!  But I think we've made lots of progress!

Thanks again!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-19T04:11:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32063>

> **Sem;32129 wrote:** Hi Lauren,
>
>
>
> Thanks for taking the time to respond. I hadn't thought of doing a consult for my son. That's a great idea. Would I need to do labs for him first? Not sure what kind of labs you need to get for kids.
>
>
>
> As for the meds, he doesn't even really know what they are for. He just thinks they are another vitamin. What I was referring to is his tendency towards anxiety and obsessive thought patterns. This seems to go up rather dramatically when I have taken him off the meds. I have dramatically decreased them since school has gotten out. He is definitely hyper and kind of moody but I haven't seen a bug spike in the anxiety so we will see.
>
>
>
> I have read a lot about the 5-htp and tryptophan and was thinking of trying those. Maybe I will try to get a consult with Dr. Kalish too?
>
>
>
> As for getting most of our supplements from food, I couldn't agree more but I don't think my kids are there yet. LOL. The spoonful of sauerkraut is still a major ordeal! I made them eat some salmon the other day and you would have thought I was subjecting them to water boarding!  But I think we've made lots of progress!
>
>
>
> Thanks again!

I'd guess that in a consult with Dr K he could advise you about labs as well as offer some general thoughts and guidelines. Or you could first do one with Dr Kalish (who does an initial consult via phone/Skype, then tells you what labs to get, then follows up after the labs with recommendations).

I found that when my son had a really good breakfast (which, for him, is some sort of protein (usually a lamb sausage) and a lot of fat (I mix a tablespoon of melted butter with a tablespoon of melted CO, along with cinnamon and raw honey into plain yogurt), his mood stablised and his anxiety about school fell away. Since we started eating per Dr K's recommendations, my son has gone from wanting to stay home from school every day (and being told he is very behind in school as well as dyslexic, etc) to saying he loves school now and just being invited into the 'gifted and talented' programme! We also follow the light cycles religiously - candles only when sun goes down (which is currently 5:30pm!) - and his sleep is much improved too. Both my kids naturally wake just before sunrise and no longer say anything negative about going to bed. By 7:30 pm they are totally relaxed and tired and are sound asleep by 8.

Good luck. It is a LOT to worry about our children - I find it much more stressful than worrying about myself (which is stressful enough) and it takes a lot of effort to not operate out of a place of fear and anxiety. Hang in there!

### Sem — 2012-06-19T18:06:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32151>

Lauren,

That's such amazing success! You must be so happy and proud of all of your efforts. The light cycle thing is tricky and I honestly don't see that happening in our house. It's easy right now because it's summer for us so it doesn't even get dark until after 8. But I am certain my husband would never agree to walking around with candles after 5 in the winter. Also, my 85 year old mother in law lives with us and I don't think it would be too safe for her! How do your kids get their homework and activities done in time? I have to say that my boys are fairly good about going to bed and I'm sure we can implement parts of this - like no electronics, tv, etc after dark. I just fed them a bug far breakfast of bacon, eggs and some leftover steak!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-20T00:17:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32255>

> **Sem;32264 wrote:** The light cycle thing is tricky and I honestly don't see that happening in our house. It's easy right now because it's summer for us so it doesn't even get dark until after 8. But I am certain my husband would never agree to walking around with candles after 5 in the winter. Also, my 85 year old mother in law lives with us and I don't think it would be too safe for her! How do your kids get their homework and activities done in time?

Honestly? I just did it. My husband wasn't keen and my kids complained a lot. But - like with much of this - I explained a LOT and I insisted. Now, funnily enough, they don't bat an eye. We do homework by candlelight. We have candles set up in each room in safe holders so that when it gets dusk I just go and light them all. It isn't 100% because we don't have curtains on all our windows and the neighbours have lights on as well as street lights (bedrooms are totally blacked out). I can only speak from our experience, but you get used to it pretty fast. And your eyes adjust quickly. My kids are also excited about the fact that during summer they will get to stay up much later. I really believe the light cycles thing has had a huge effect on all of us - regulating moods, cortisol, melatonin, etc. - as well as eliminating nightly nightmares for my son. (It helped to get my husband on board by having him listen to the two TS Wiley interviews with Sean Croxton.) I think this is one area that makes a huge difference and is really only held back by our thoughts. We have just passed the shortest day of the year (sun up at 7:45am, sun down at 5:30) and we're handling it really well.

Good luck!

### nonchalant — 2012-06-20T00:52:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32267>

> **KiwiLauren;32368 wrote:** Honestly? I just did it. My husband wasn't keen and my kids complained a lot.

My husband wasn't keen either. His night vision was poor and he didn't like it dark. But since he has started cleaning up his diet, his night vision seems to be improving, like mine did. And he is getting sleepy earlier and sleeping through the night more often. He is also getting used to using the dimmer on the bathroom light in the morning. I'm lucky to have a DH who may not understand, but he does try to humor me. And he does notice benefits from respecting the light cycle. I wish someone would offer a TV with F.lux technology inside, because he does like his TV at night still.

### Sem — 2012-06-21T20:18:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32764>

This is all fairly easy to do in the summer so we will try to ease into it. My older son (the one who is always cold) also likes to sleep with his lights dimmed. Two of the light bulbs went out and I'm just not replacing them!

### NotAnother30 — 2012-06-16T17:43:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/post-31549>

I was just wondering if anyone with gluten intolerance has taken probiotics after being diagnosed and if there has been any significant changes. I would guess that they should help some but I'm on the wire about taking them.

Thanks

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-16T18:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/post-31555>

A blood test for allergies revealed that I was gluten intolerant. I avoid all gluten, if possible.

My doctor suggested VSL#3 probiotic. It is pharmacutical grade and needs to be refrigerated, but you do not need a prescription. Most pharmacies carry it but you have to ask for it. Interestingly, my first bottle was purchased from the doctor for $89 for 60 caps, but you can get online from VSL for less than $60 and I now purchase mine directly from my pharmacy for $53!

Yes, they made a world of difference for me. I have always have problems with constipation, but not anymore. This may be TMI, but on Thursday night, I went out for dinner and had a surf-n-turf dinner which was a steak and coconut shrimp. I think there was gluten in the breading. (duh!) Yesterday, I started having pains in my gut and a little gas. Last night it kept me awake somewhat. This morning I had a huge BM and feel great now. The probiotics definitely help getting the stuff out quick!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-17T16:37:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/post-31742>

Agree, was diagnosed via enterolab and despite being gluten free except for X-contamination I have had constipation problems until I added in a probiotic and digestive enzymes.

eta, as the above poster my probiotic has to be a pretty strong one though before I see results, I use a 50billion strain probiotic

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-06-22T20:52:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/post-32986>

Before I got on probiotics, a gluten experience would have me in bed for a week. Now, I can feel better in a day or two, without being completely fatigued. I've read you should switch your probiotic brands once in awhile, too, to keep your stomach guessing. I take 2-3 of the 50 billion strain myself, but I add in a different kind (only 14 billion) on occassion, just to switch things up. HCL and Super Digestive Enzymes also help with constipation for me.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-16T17:44:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31550>

So I bought my Flexi-freeze ice vest the one in the recommended section...and it gets my skin temp down...what I was curious of is has anyone had any success with it. What I'm looking for is people who weren't using it and than started and they had improvements in either weight or other things such as measurements etc...

I really want this to work...it wasn't really expensive but its still not cheap either.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-17T16:48:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31744>

[http://cooldownusa.com/index.php?cPath=1](http://http://cooldownusa.com/index.php?cPath=1)

If anyone happens to have used this Artic Heat vest I would be interested to know how that worked as well. Its substantially more expensive at 220.00...something to think about in the future if it has advantages.

[http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Ice-Vest-Navy/dp/B001P30358/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1332183843&sr=8-1](http://http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Ice-Vest-Navy/dp/B001P30358/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1332183843&sr=8-1)

Flexi Freeze is currently 64.99

155 dollar premium good for anything or just a waste?

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-17T19:00:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31769>

FWIW I'm not sold on either vest. For me, the flexi vest does not hold the panels close enough to my skin. Now I just wear the flexi panels next to my skin with a compression shirt over them to hold them tight to the skin. Its the only way I can get my skin pink. I think if I wore it once a day I would notice a difference. But life has been a little crazy lately.

I have been looking at these and if I were to do it all over, I think the only vest I would get is one that circulates water to maintain a certain temp. I see you are in TX - so you may get a much greater benefit than I do here in MN.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-18T00:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31824>

I find that several areas are getting pink and temp ranges from 45-65 some areas such as my lower back when I'm sitting getting the most pressure being the lower temps. I know below 50 is out of range by the way but I don't think it should be an issue because it never gets below 45 and things warm up decently fast it doesn't stay that way.

I think I might get a compression shirt does that work pretty well for you? Wondering if maybe the way mine fits on me is just better...since it does make my skin pink.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-18T19:27:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31959>

That is great you can get low skin temps using the vest!

I can't get that vest to fit me well enough to be hold the panels close to my skin. The compression shirt looks silly, but it works well. I just put the shirt on and then slip the panels underneath the shirt. Nothing between the panels and my skin. I would think any tight, stretchy shirt will do the trick. Next time I need more ice panels I'll probably go for something like

this: [http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Refreezable-Sheets-pack/dp/B001PTGO5A/ref=pd\_sim\_k\_3](http://http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Refreezable-Sheets-pack/dp/B001PTGO5A/ref=pd_sim_k_3)

or this:[http://www.amazon.com/Rubbermaid-Blue-Ice-Flexible-Blanket/dp/B004286IL6/ref=pd\_sim\_sg\_1](http://http://www.amazon.com/Rubbermaid-Blue-Ice-Flexible-Blanket/dp/B004286IL6/ref=pd_sim_sg_1)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-18T20:09:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31969>

I have the Flexi Freeze ice sheets and although I really like the concept of sheets and love how the sheets work they are not made of very good quality. I have had mine about 3 months and have already had at least 3-4 cubes per sheet break open (used only for CT) and spill out gelatinous goo. No big deal but now I don't have that spot cold!

I also have the Rubbermaid ones in the white not blue gel. Again I like the sheet idea better, but the regular white one doesn't stay cold very long. The blue may be different and may stay cold longer. I would definitely try the blue though since they have not leaked or I haven't had any problems with the Rubbermaid and I have had this for years and used it for a variety of things.

### Austin Runner — 2012-06-20T17:42:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-32417>

There is a company called CoolWear that makes cooling shirts (called an IceTee) that work. The shirts have gel packs that slide into pockets. The website is [www.theicetee.com](http://http://www.theicetee.com).

# Thread: Howdy from BIRDS AS ART :)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/howdy-from-birds-as-art.1902/>

### Arthur Morris — 2012-06-16T18:02:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/howdy-from-birds-as-art.1902/post-31552>

I am a 66 year old professional bird photographer living in Indian Lake Estates, FL but I travel much too much. My name is Arthur Morris. My health story is an interesting one. I tried killing myself with food for about 40 years--can you say 2/3 million oreo cookies?--and almost succeeded. At one point--in the late 1980s--I was having about a dozen episodes of atrial fibrillation a year, had had high blood pressure since I was 13, and weighed about 235. I weighed 264 when I was 18. At one point I was on every major heart medication including Lanoxin and Cardizem.

About 16 years ago I met a chiropractor/nutritionist from San Diego named Dr. Cliff Oliver. He got me eating well, exercising a bit, taking lots of the right supps as dictated by http://www.healthalternatives2000.com/nutri-spec.html for a Red-crowned Crane image from my Japan trip. Could not figure out how to get it to appear in the post.

NCH, Thanks. I am already seeing that when I am cold blood sugar goes down. I swim in a lap pool that I built in my backyard. I have been doing the breath hold face dunks every morning, getting better every time with the breath holds. Last week I got in a "cold" Florida tub: 80 degrees out of the tap. I have gotten that down to 74 degrees easily with just a big bucket of 40 degree water and some ice. I soak for 30 minutes before I swim. With lots of rain from Debby the pool is down to 80 degrees. When I swim I get close to shivering for the first half of the swim. Is that because the pool is still colder than my body?

### nonchalant — 2012-06-26T18:42:12+0300

But the doc said anything cooler than normal skin temp will work, just very slowly. Winter can't come soon enough!

# Thread: Why the high fat/high protein meal and ice cold water prior to CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-high-protein-meal-and-ice-cold-water-prior-to-ct.1905/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-06-16T19:04:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-high-protein-meal-and-ice-cold-water-prior-to-ct.1905/post-31567>

Sorry if this has been answered already. I've searched the blog, comments, and forum.

Why the high fat/high protein meal and the ice cold water prior to CT?

I've only CT'd for about a week, and I've followed all instructions well. Just prior to getting in, I eat a meal of fish, CO manna, and a huge glass of ice water. My CT sessions, all around 30 mins @ 50-55 degrees, have actually been pleasant. No shivering, etc.

However, I was in a hotel this week and brought all my CT supplies (thermometer, huge plastic bags to haul ice from the ice machine, ipod). I actually forgot (forgot!!!) to eat my evening meal. I just wasn't hungry! And I forgot about drinking the water. So I just hopped in, and I shivered like a maniac the whole time and afterwards for about an hour. This particular CT session really threw me for a loop, as it took so long to recover..

Is it because the body has to warm up the ice water, plus digesting the meal, so your body temp rises so you won't go into a hypothermic state?

**Labrix Cortisol Salivary Panel - Nov 2011

_Here I was doing lowish carb AI paleo and using GAPS early stage cooking methods (bone broths, soups, braises, only cooked peeled veggies, etc). I was exhausted all the time.

AM 6.97 nmol/L

Noon 3.39 nmol/L

Evening 2.2 nmol/L

Night 1.75 nmol/L

ZRT Cortisol Salivary Panel - May 2012_**_

Starting in Feb 2012 I started the leptin reset and began to pay more attention light/circadian cycles. I am still on a low carb AI paleo foods. Eating more fish. Using CT a couple times a week. And trying to eating carbs in accordance to the light cycles. I still use low temp cooking methods and eat my veggies cooked because my body says so._

AM 26.736 nmol/ML

Noon 5.57 nmol/ML

Evening 3.0635 nmol/ML

Night 2.228 nmol/ML

Too bad the tests were done by different labs, but even so that is a SIGNIFICANT improvement in my book. I still struggle with being tired in the morning, but I'm headed in the right direction. And even better this means no need to supplement with exogenous HC. That makes me super happy!! Thank you Dr K for thinking outside the box.

What do you think doc?

Water temps vary between 64-80 degrees depending on the weather. For the summer I plan to just roll with the outdoor pool. # Thread: Hepatic gene expression profiling reveals key pathways involved in leptin-mediated we
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hepatic-gene-expression-profiling-reveals-key-pathways-involved-in-leptin-mediated-we.1908/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-16T22:21:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hepatic-gene-expression-profiling-reveals-key-pathways-involved-in-leptin-mediated-we.1908/post-31611>

Hepatic gene expression profiling reveals key pathways involved in leptin-mediated weight loss in ob/ob mice.

Abstract

BACKGROUND:

Leptin, a cytokine-like protein, plays an important role in the regulation of body weight through inhibition of food intake and stimulation of energy expenditure. Leptin circulates in blood and acts on the brain, which sends downstream signals to regulate body weight. Leptin therapy has been successful in treating leptin deficient obese patients. However, high levels of leptin have been observed in more common forms of obesity indicating a state of leptin resistance which limits the application of leptin in the treatment of obesity. If the central effect of leptin could be by-passed and genes which respond to leptin treatment could be regulated directly, new therapeutic targets for the treatment of obesity may be possible. The purpose of this study was to identify genes and subsequent pathways correlated with leptin-mediated weight loss.

METHODOLOGY/PRINCIPAL FINDINGS:

WE UTILIZED MICROARRAY TECHNOLOGY TO COMPARE HEPATIC GENE EXPRESSION CHANGES AFTER TWO TYPES OF LEPTIN ADMINISTRATION: one involving a direct stimulatory effect when administered peripherally (subcutaneous: SQ) and another that is indirect, involving a hypothalamic relay that suppresses food intake when leptin is administered centrally (intracerebroventricular: ICV). **We identified 214 genes that correlate with leptin mediated weight loss. Several biological processes such as mitochondrial metabolic pathways, lipid metabolic and catabolic processes, lipid biosynthetic processes, carboxylic acid metabolic processes, iron ion binding and glutathione S-transferases were downregulated after leptin administration. In contrast, genes involved in the immune system inflammatory response and lysosomal activity were found to be upregulated. Among the cellular compartments mitochondrion (32 genes), endoplasmic reticulum (22 genes) and vacuole (8 genes) were significantly over represented.**

CONCLUSIONS/SIGNIFICANCE:

In this study we have identified key molecular pathways and downstream genes which respond to leptin treatment and are involved in leptin-mediated weight loss. Many of these genes have previously been shown to be associated with obesity; however, we have also identified a number of other novel target genes. Further investigation will be required to assess the possible use of these genes and their associated protein products as therapeutic targets for the treatment of obesity.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20808936](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20808936)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: copper chelation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-16T23:01:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31616>

[http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/fw08/copper.html](http://http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/fw08/copper.html)

Tetrathiomolybdate (TTM) is an anti-copper drug initially developed to treat Wilson's disease, a genetic disease characterized by excessive accumulation of copper in the liver and consequent liver and brain damage. The copper-chelating property of TTM was discovered decades ago in Australia and New Zealand, where ruminants developed copper deficiency due to naturally formed TTM in the sulfur-rich rumen.

### chocolate — 2012-06-16T23:07:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31617>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20693639](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20693639)

Abstract

There is increasing evidence for the crucial role of metals in the pathology of Alzheimer's disease. Both the aggregation and neurotoxicity of amyloid-β are dependent on the presence of copper. This study investigated the ability of the copper-complexing drug tetrathiomolybdate to reduce amyloid-β pathology and spatial memory impairment in both a prevention and a treatment paradigm in the Tg2576 mouse model of Alzheimer's disease. Tetrathiomolybdate treatment lowered brain copper and reduced amyloid-β levels in the prevention paradigm, but not in the treatment paradigm. Our data suggests that controlled lowering of systemic copper may achieve anti-amyloid effects if initiated early in the disease process.

### chocolate — 2012-06-16T23:15:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31620>

[http://www.news-medical.net/news/20091127/Copper-sequestering-drug-tetrathiomolybdate-shown-to-be-effective-in-treating-Wilson-disease.aspx](http://http://www.news-medical.net/news/20091127/Copper-sequestering-drug-tetrathiomolybdate-shown-to-be-effective-in-treating-Wilson-disease.aspx)

> TM also has been examined in recent studies where copper dysregulation is implicated in the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases such as familial amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis and Alzheimer's disease as well as primary pulmonary hypertension and left ventricular hypertrophy associated with type II diabetes. Copper modulating agents including TM have been shown to be active in animal models of these diseases providing a rationale for advancing tetrathiomolybdates into clinical evaluation in these areas.

### Lexi — 2012-06-17T22:57:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31814>

i assume this is only available by Rx?

### chocolate — 2012-06-17T23:47:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31820>

This article talks about three copper chelators that are otc . I like the molydenum chelates period.

[http://www.ehow.com/info\_10047966\_ways-chelate-copper.html](http://http://www.ehow.com/info_10047966_ways-chelate-copper.html)

The best things to stay away from are chocolate and shellfish, from what I read.

[http://candidapage.com/aldehyde.shtml](http://http://candidapage.com/aldehyde.shtml)

> And from another source comes another connection -- from Dr. Robert Atkins' newsletter: Dr. Atkins is writing about Pantethine which he prescribes to his Crohn's Disease and Colitis patients, with acknowledgement to Dr. Melvin Werbach for Dr. Werbach's study that demonstrated that people with colitis have markedly decreased Coenzyme A activity if the mucosal surface of their colons, even when the blood levels of pantothenic acid are normal. Dr. Atkins concluded, based on his success with these patients of his, that Pantethine bypasses the block in converting Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) to Coenzyme A. But also, that Pantethine is a growth factor for lactobacillus bulgaricus and bifidobacterium that we know help control yeast overgrowth (and Dr. Cooter also speaks of it in his book). Candida, according to antibody studies done at the Atkins Center, is involved in more than 80 percent of all cases of Crohn's and Colitis.
>
>
>
> And for autoimmune problems, Dr. Atkins states, " For all conditions that a doctor might prescribe prednisone -- allergies, asthma, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, lupus, and olther autoimmune diseases, pantethine can be safely, effectively substituted. I routinely use it for all of those conditions on hundreds of my patients, and it's valuable in weaning them off steroidal drugs, or certainly in allowing a lower dose....
>
>
>
> By upping body levels of a body enzyme, pantethine counteracts brain fog, certain allergic sensitivities, and some consequences of alcoholism. (And here it is --) ... In people with candidiasis, the enzyme fights off a toxic byproduct called acetaldehyde, which is thought to cause brain fog, often-suffered but rarely diagnosed.... Acetaldehyde also is suspected of being responsible for some symptoms of alcoholism, including alcoholic heart muscle disease. The pantethine-stimulated enzyme also detoxifies formaldehyde, an all too frequent offender for chemically sensitive individuals."
>
>
>
> **In summary, Dr. Atkins is saying that Pantethine, without toxic consequences, can reduce cholesterol, counuteract oxidation, stimulate the growth of friendly bacteria, and fight allergies, inflammation, autoimmune disruptions, and alcoholism.
>
>
>
> In case you wondered, Dr. Cooter and Dr. Schmtt suggest 300 micrograms of Molybdenum in three divided doses per day, and further suggests staying on it for at least 4 months.. Dr. Atkins suggests 450 to 900 miligrams daily of Pantethine with an equal amount of Pantethenic Acid.**

We have to get rid of surplus copper and add zinc to help with the candida. The iron will be used appropriately (allegedly) if the right balance is hit.

### Lexi — 2012-06-18T00:25:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31827>

thanks. more supplements to add.... its too overwhelming.

### chocolate — 2012-06-18T00:33:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31828>

I just alternate between sardines and pork and beef and still eat liver. If I don't have something greasy, I don't bother. I think minerals pretty much get carried by fat. I was eating the shrimp, but I can ditch it for a couple of months. No butter is proving disappointing and the dogs are higher on the pecking order for the bacon fat apparently.

### hazyjane — 2012-06-21T23:45:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-32808>

I had melasma (often due to high copper) that faded after lowering my intake of high copper foods (chocolate, nuts) and taking molybdenum and zinc.

woman, add LH & FSH to that list

man, add PSA & FreePSA, and LH, FSH.

I am not a doctor.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-19T20:09:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-labs.1914/post-32204>

Jasmine check out [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com) for thyroid info. I think you will find more information that you wanted on thyroids. Yes your T3 is really low. Can you get a Free T3 test? That is a better test to see how much T3 is getting into your cells. If not, just go by the T3 test, which is low. Your T4 is also low (more desirable test Free T4). It appears you are not getting enough T3 into you system/cells (meds). Although your TSH is really low that is typical of natural thyroid medication results. Mostly do you feel sluggish?

Also have you checked your adrenals via a cortisol saliva test? I don't know if you can get that either but a sluggish thyroid is often accompanied by sluggish adrenals. Just something else to check out.

Maybe altering the way you take it will help. I took my natural thyroid at 5am (got out of bed at 7) and then again around 2pm. I split my dose because it worked better for me. I would try adjusting how you take it before increasing the amount. I know some people go as high as five grains but that just sounds like an awful lot to me. Another possibility is going on T3 only but I don't know if you can get that in Denmark. There is a way to buy it online...PM me if you want the info. Some websites have that sell the thyroid meds have just disappeared so I try to protect the sources....don't know if it matters.

Is T3 ment to supplement Erfa Thyroid with both T3 and T4 or does it work on its own?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-20T18:36:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-labs.1914/post-32433>

Thyroid meds should be taken on an empty stomach. At least natural dessicated thyroid (NDT). It's best to wait at least 90 min after eating and 30 min before you eat. Also since you have the Erfa you can take it sublingually and dissolve it under your tongue. Synthroid doesn't work for most people so stay away from T4 only meds. I hadn't heard of any natural T3 only meds but please let us know if you find something. T3 is mostly used in people who don't tolerate T4 only or NDT and if there is a rT3 problem. I am currently on Cytomel T3 only, to clear some remaining rT3 right now.

Also what was your dose of Erfa? Oftentimes when starting out the dose is too small and really doesn't do that much. Read on the website I gave you on how to raise. Most folks take between 2.5-5 grains. Before I went on T3 I was at 2 1/4. You don't want to take too much to make you hyper but you want to be on enough to have it do it's job. I hope this helps!

### JanSz — 2012-06-20T22:04:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-labs.1914/post-32492>

> **Jasmin;32475 wrote:** I will study what is written in post #1
>
> Normally I eat 90-100g protein, 25-30g carbs and 90-110g fat in a day - is that like the picture if food composition or perhaps I chould go lower with the carbs? Am eating 2-3 meals a day.
>
>
>
> I thought that sunfloweroils was bad because it was from a plant - since I started LR I have only been eating coconutoil and animalfat - have I misundersood something?
>
> Since I started LR I havent eaten any fruit, no grains, diary and nightshades only twice, so that part I am following wery strict

There is variety of sunflowers.

Those specific oils that I mentioned have desirable Fatty Acids spectrum.

Another words, not all sunflowers are equal.

We are working toward certain goals, and where we are can be checked by doing numerous tests.

I would not worry to much that you are eating plant (sunflower for their ALA(O3) & LA(O6) content) but are supposed to eat ranch grazed beef.

If you trust you beef or you eat proghorn antelope meat, possibly you do not need sunflower.

Only blood test will tell you where you are.

### paton — 2013-06-18T09:24:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/late-night-weight-lifting.1916/post-88334>

In the morning, after a good night's sleep, our body is fresh to take on the activities of the day. Also, testosterone levels are the highest in the morning. Testosterone plays a major role in muscle growth. After any kind of exercise are body becomes to supply energy for the tasks. So for all these reasons it is best to exercise in the morning. At night our body is tired from the days work, our testosterone levels are get relatively low, and if we sleep immediately after exercise our body becomes stiff. Hence, it is best to work out in the morning.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-18T17:33:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/late-night-weight-lifting.1916/post-88349>

no its not.........not even close. That is your perception. From this blog long ago: [http://www.jackkruse.com/so-you-completed-the-leptin-rx-what-is-next/](http://www.jackkruse.com/so-you-completed-the-leptin-rx-what-is-next/)

5\. For Optimal results you must get most of your daily activity between 9AM and 4PM when light cycles are strong year round. This is another reason I strongly advocate high vitamin D levels year round. We evolved around the equator and equatorial sun has been shown to keep human vitamin D levels 50-150 ng/mL. Avoid sitting at all costs and consider walking to get lunch or a short run during your mid day break. The real goal here is to increase Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis (NEAT) during strong light hours. This has major effects when it is done consistently over time. For example, I run upstairs and avoid elevators and I park far away from my destinations to increase my NEAT daily. I also carry all my groceries out to the car and never use a shopping cart to make it easier. I look at every aspect of my actions to make sure I am maximizing it for NEAT.

7\. Critical point: The best time to work out biologically occurs when it is least likely to be convenient for you because of our neolithic lives wont allow it. I re-tooled my entire schedule as a surgeon to make this work optimally for me to lose weight and change my body. It is that important biologically to get to optimal. 1-5 PM is the ideal workout window. For best results, try to do the exercise in bright sunlight.

8\. Dinner should be eaten within 45 minutes to 1 hour of this late afternoon work out. During dinner you want to make sure to include a lot of protein (25-75 grams), the remainder of your carb allotment and the balance in fats. The type of fats at dinner are also critical. Try to concentrate on 10-18 carbon fats because these are best at stimulating Cholecystokinin (CCK) that destroys the night time appetite. I use coconut oil, ghee, pastured butter, and bacon lard to get this effect. I use the fat to cover the carbs and the protein most times in sauces.

10\. Sleep by 11PM in spring and summer months. I stay up longer June 10th to July 10th due to summer solstice on June 21. During this time of the year I tend to have higher body fat with longer light cycles. In autumn and winter I am in bed by 10 PM. I am in bed earlier when the clocks are set back on hour in fall and heading toward the winter solstice on Dec 21st. I have found I am leanest during this time of the year. The goal of sleep in any day of the year is an optimal 7.5- 8 hours of sleep a night no matter the season. You will know when you are doing well because you will no longer need an alarm clock and your sleep wake cycle will be automatic. I found after one year of using this protocol I no longer needed an alarm clock to wake up for surgery.

WHAT CHANGES SHOULD I CONSIDER AFTER THE LEPTIN RX RESET?

1\. If you are active, drop all of the top ten paleo supplements I mentioned in that blog. They were only meant for the transition from a sugar burning metabolism to a fat burning furnace as the permanent paleo template takes flight. If you are not active, I would strongly consider you remain on PQQ and vitamin D3. Try to optimize your vitamin D levels to 70-100 ng/mL.

2\. If you decide to Intermittent Fasting do not skip breakfast ever. It is the key to circadian congruity and optimal body composition. You will see below how this determines body comp and not the amount of exercise one does.

KEY POINTS TO KEEP IN MIND:

1\. Higher protein consumption occurs at night now, not at breakfast as it did in the Leptin Rx reset. The reason is because late afternoon is when the human body is normally programmed to undergo up regulation of protein synthesis biochemically. This is how our biology is designed by God/evolution. If you remember back earlier in the post, I told you the key point for breakfast was to use carbs and a small protein load to prime the gastric acid circadian cycle for maximal effect later in the day. This is precisely the reason why. Gastric pH should be highest when we are eating our biggest protein load of the day while simultaneously upregulating protein synthesis maximally in our body. This maneuver actually influences our body composition more than any exercise could if it was added to the equation at all. Doing this on time is akin to an orchestra playing in unison. It is a huge point to try to follow daily.

2\. Carb macro’s should parallel activity and light cycles. Too often I hear many in the “primal world” talk about carbs and activity. They always forget about the light cycle. If you are real active and work out more than 4 days a week in sunlight, you can handle 30% of your calories from carbs. If not consider 10-20% range. The closer I get to June 21st the higher my carb amounts tend to go, and the closer I am to Dec 21st I am at a zero carb diet. I give this to you as a baseline to work from. It is not meant for you to copy exactly as I do. This is precisely how I ate to lose 135 pounds the first 11 months I came up with this program.

3\. Exercise/activity is optimal for us between 9AM and 4PM biologically. I understand this might be hard to fit into your schedule but the payoff is massive. Let me explain why now. Remember that cortisol is highest in the AM to allow us to wake up. If you exercise before breakfas,t you are risking elevating your cortisol even higher. This will cause a pregnenolone steal syndrome and ruin your hormonal response that controls your lean muscle mass and fat ratios and eventually your body composition. If you continue to do this over time, it will slow your protein synthesis that occurs later in the afternoon and evening, that increases your body comp. Moreover, it will also ruin your sleep cycle (checked by having low DHEA levels) and you risk being in an overtrained situation. I think this is the biggest error I see in the paleo community because they are trying to fit their “neolithic life” into their day best from a time stand point. My advice is don’t even try it.

**4\. You get the best (optimal) protein synthesis benefit if exercise occurs 1-4PM. Make sure your lifting days occur on the days of the week you can accomplish this. Save sprinting days for days that this wont work for your schedule. Use common sense about building this into your own life. Optimize your schedule to benefit your body composition. If you fight this trend you can still get ripped up but you will exhaust your stem cells in doing so and you will lose years on the back end of your life. Timing is that important.**

5\. To show you how important meal timing to the light cycles are to us humans, consider these facts regarding exercise and meal timing. If you are able to yoke your workout to your evening protein dinner meal (within 30-45 minutes) you actually “**triple the amount of protein synthesis**” that occurs compared to those who do not. This is how a hunter gatherer attained their optimal body comp without having to do huge amounts of exercises. Moreover, if this is also yoked to the light cycle in winter when the temperatures are below 40 degrees, **you can increase protein synthesis to 400%** while inducing uncoupling proteins to burn fat at a tripled rate to baseline. This allows you to increase fat burning to shred body fat further faster while you are increasing lean muscle mass. NASA uses this technology for the astronauts that space walk. They found this data out ironically from the Sherpa’s who help foreigners scale the last 2000 feet of Mount Everest summit. This is also precisely why I am leanest around December 21st,. At this time, the light cycle its lowest and the temperature is usually low so exercising in this window is the easiest way I know to shred body fat and gain muscle mass quickly. I went from 44% BF to 19% BF in one year doing this. I would also like to point out something here that you might not have ever thought about in order to show you how incongruent humans really are with this timing issue. When do we see ads in the newspapers for weight loss aids? The answer is around New Years Day in January. Why? Because humans tend to get fat in winter so sports stores place ads when they are most likely to gain revenue. They are in tune to our behavior. Now change your mind set and think about wild animals you have seen on the Discovery channel. When are they leanest during the year? The answer is in winter, when food is scarce and they have to really forage for food in subzero temperatures. Most humans live a life that is biologically incongruent to how our biology is designed to live. Once I thought about the leptin receptor biology and how I could reset it using circadian rhythms I went back to my veterinarian friends and to the exercise literature (Frank Booth) to figure how to live the rest of my life optimally and regain my optimal body form and metabolism. This is precisely how I live daily today. Feel free to call my operating room team and ask them when was the last time they ever saw me operating between 2-5 PM? For the last 5 years the answer is never.

### JackFrake — 2013-06-18T18:18:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/late-night-weight-lifting.1916/post-88351>

What is your opinion of the following IF protocol:

BAB around 5/530am

Lunch/Dinner around 1130/noon (last meal)

2-3x/week: either heavy lift or circuit lift (30-35 mins max)

2-3x/week: light jujitsu or boxing

Try to save more strenuous workout days for the weekend. This is when I can eat post-workout.

Otherwise, I don't have a problem eating my last meal around noon and fasting until the BAB the next day. Even when I workout or do light jujitsu/boxing.

Just wondering if this is potentially problematic or too far from optimal.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-18T20:52:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/late-night-weight-lifting.1916/post-88363>

I like IFing after you are LS with a good panel.......does not have to be great just avg........But I still would pay attn to the light cycle........when doing it.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-18T23:17:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/late-night-weight-lifting.1916/post-88372>

In regards to IFing, I've thought up my own interesting protocol after reading much of the LeptinRX and Mastering Leptin. Pay attention to light cycles and in spring and summer eat when you awaken, 30-1hr after but ideally 30mins after, no snacking, and finish your last meal before the sun has set. You should be able to get in about 12hrs from last to first meal of the day which is more than enough for autophagic benefits of fasting to set in, not including the hormonal fat burning benefits. Then you increase this during fall and winter. You finish your last meal before the sun sets, therefore you would easily be able to get in a typical 16-18hr fast while still eating an early breakfast. To take it another notch, increase calories and carbs summer and winter/fall, not only eat ketogenic, but eat less. I don't know if that'd be beneficial or not but it seems like that'd be the most significant way to be mindful of light cycles while also fasting.

### Albert83BCN — 2013-06-23T10:38:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/late-night-weight-lifting.1916/post-88722>

Considering you cannot train on the golden range suggested by Jack that maximizes protein syntesis training around 1-4PM and you have to stick to one of those two 'less ideal' options... I've been training late on the day, ranging from 6PM to 23PM. I'm just theorizing that this messed up my circadian rythms a lot. I guess it will depend on the activity type. If you're going to do some strenght training for short and not push it too much, or a mild exercise form, I'd say it could be feasible to do it later on the day, I'd still try to make sure you end about 3-4h before going to sleep, but the longer the time in between, the better.

But... if you are going to train to exhaustion and raise a lot your cortisol levels, then I think it would be better to do in the morning. For me, after some intense interval workouts (like the 5x2000, 6x1000 or 10x400) or long runs at intense pace, once I ended them I felt too much wired, getting to sleep got very difficult to me. But as I also was into moderate carb, slighty undercaloric, I got up earlier on the morning also, so maybe got to bed at 2-3PM but get up at 8 or so... and feeling pretty washed up, getting short sleep... I really think that I altered my circadian patterns that way, that's why I now feel my best at night (typical fatigue sympthoms) and worst in the morning.

Well, since incorporing light cycle resync, looking at the morning sun, switching off lights at night and cutting back my training to allow for regeneration and going out for a walk first thing in the morning everyday, I really start to feel best on the morning again and a little bit sleepy at night by 23PM, not perfect yet but definetly improving.

I know Jack does not advocate to train first thing in the morning because it migh raise cortisol that it should be already high by then, but if I were to choose again, when I reincorpore some running I'll try to do it in the morning before work. This is something I could not did before when I woke up at 9AM drained but now I wake up at 7AM refreshed things are different. Then if I have to strenght train I can leave that for 5-6PM, my current work schedule allows for this at least 70% of the time. That way I can eat between 7-8PM and be ready to sleep around 23PM.

Just my 2 cents as a layperson, again an experiment of N = 1

### marcus — 2013-07-25T07:36:24+0300

### nonchalant — 2013-09-07T17:56:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/late-night-weight-lifting.1916/post-95138>

In addition to the circadian effects on exercise, it can't be good to exercise when your source of greatest power (the sun) is absent. Even daytime exercise outside while the sun is obscured in heavy clouds would not be optimal, unless you were barefoot or it were raining. At night you would have to be well grounded. Maybe ocean swimming or weightlifting on the grass? But it would still mess up your hormones. And you couldn't see that circling shark. ;-)

### Norbert — 2013-09-30T21:55:34+0300

# Thread: Pimples  the Vagus nerve
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pimples-the-vagus-nerve.1917/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-06-17T18:53:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pimples-the-vagus-nerve.1917/post-31768>

I eat wheat, usually about 3 times a year. The last time was for Mother's Day, when I ate quite a bit of homemade angel food cake. I took some DGL before I ate it, and was pleased that I didn't get an upset tummy after the stomach emptied into the small intestine. That stuff works!

A few days later, as expected, I got some pimples. Then more. I'm still seeing a few popping up, both fore and aft. Long ago I bought the spiel about dirty skin causing pimples. What a guilt trip! Now I see that it's more of a detox reaction, or inflammation. But what is the reason for the location of the pimples? They are near both ends of the alimentary canal.

I think the vagus nerve only extends from just below the nose to the upper parts of the colon. So would it have no role in pimples near the nose or eyebrow? Or on the bum?

Anyway, perhaps next time I won't eat so much of it. So my pimple population is a bit more reduced. ;-)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-17T20:17:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pimples-the-vagus-nerve.1917/post-31787>

You eat wheat once and you have forever altered yourself. It destroys the delicate balance of our leaky gut and turns it into a massive damn failure........We are designed to have a slow leak to get the nutrients we need in fast to help us.......but when you break the damn mechanism.......all hell breaks loose.

>
>
> I'm curious about this, not in the sense of eating wheat, I don't as I am diagnosed non-celiac gluten intolerant and extremely sensitive but from the perspective of at what level of exposure to wheat do you consider it to "forever alter(ed)" the body?
>
>
>
> for example, if asked you would probably say Jack that you are gluten free yet you have stated you eat up to 2 bars per week of the World Market 99% Cocoa bar which contains the warning that there is possible cross contamination as it is processed on lines that process wheat.
>
>
>
> If you were celiac, have dermatitis herpeteformis or non-celiac gluten intolerant then that chocolate would be an inedible product because of the cross contamination and if you did eat it you would experience/be aware of the damage being done to your body as the inflammatory reaction ensues.
>
>
>
> If you are not celiac, don't have DH or aren't a non-celiac gluten intolerant you can happily consume that chocolate and not feel any inflammatory reaction.
>
>
>
> and yet we know such an inflammatory reaction must exist because there is a section of the population that does react to the presence of the wheat in the chocolate that came as a by product of production.
>
>
>
> Are you looking at this much like the European Union and the Codex Alimentarius where gluten free foods:
>
> Gluten-free foods are dietary foods
>
> a) consisting of or made only from one or more ingredients which do not contain wheat (i.e., all
>
> Triticum species, such as durum wheat, spelt, and kamut), rye, barley, oats1 or their crossbred
>
> varieties, and the gluten level does not exceed 20 mg/kg in total, based on the food as sold or
>
> distributed to the consumer, and/or
>
>
>
> b) consisting of one or more ingredients from wheat (i.e., all Triticum species, such as
>
> durum wheat, spelt, and kamut), rye, barley, oats1 or their crossbred varieties, which have
>
> been specially processed to remove gluten, and the gluten level does not exceed 20 mg/kg
>
> in total, based on the food as sold or distributed to the consumer.

or is any amount of wheat exposure too much?

### Martha Ray — 2020-08-29T01:54:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pimples-the-vagus-nerve.1917/post-291807>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=31859)
>
> The truth usually does.

Re: **Truth**
I had a friend once who used to say, 'The truth will set you free, but first, it will piss you off'.

Truth may well be entwined in a lot of human knowledge but the Truth that sets free is the person of The Lord Jesus Christ. John 14:6 Jesus said to him, _“_**I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me.**_"_

I believe that with much learning comes much suffering. _Ecclesiastes 1:18_ but it is also true God's people perish from lack of knowledge. _Hosea 4:6_ And I do have faith that The Truth sets us free. _2 John 1:4-6_ and when He sets one free, one is free indeed. John 8:36.

With sound reason from what is true in the physical realm, John 8:12 "Again Jesus spoke to them, saying, “I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.”

**Ultimately when all the health dots are connected what really matters is that even when you die you will live.**
John 11:25-26 "Jesus said to her, “I am the resurrection and the life; he who believes in Me will live even if he dies, and everyone who lives and believes in Me will never die. **Do you believe this?**”

# Thread: The 30 Day Seafood Challenge thread!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 271
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### August — 2012-06-17T21:56:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31807>

Challenge: Eat one meal a day with Seafood as the protein source . . everyday for 30 days! (freshwater fish and shellfish also count . . . anything fake like faux does not count)

**Inspiration for the Challenge:

> **Glamazon;31899 wrote: I have noticed that with eating salmon twice a week there is a difference in my skin that even my mother noticed. My fat deposits on my belly are feeling squishy too since I started back in Feb.
>
> **
>
> I am going to attempt to eat seafood every day for at least one meal for 30 days.
>
>
>
> Whole foods is selling frozen sockeye with the skin on for $10.99 a pound. Can't beat that with a stick. Yummm...crispy salmon skin.
>
>
>
> I also bought a 150 gallon 2x2x6 poly stock watering tank yesterday and dragged it out to the patio. Not too heavy at 49 pounds. Only $120 bucks at the farm store. Time to get down and learn to chillax Kruse style.
>
>
>
> Susan****

****
Source: http://forum.jackkruse.com/search.php?searchid=140349

Other rules:** there are no other rules . . just Eat one meal a day with Seafood as the protein source,everyday for 30 days! Freshwater fish and shellfish also count . . . anything fake like faux does not count.

You may add your own bonus rules . . like if you are doing the Leptin Reset (for as long as you are on it) having Seafood as your BAB protein source . .

It would be appreciated if you posted your intentions to participate in this thread . . and then also post here about your Seafood meals . . . . and also your results!! (that probably ought to be a rule . . but I don't much like rules!)

Disclaimer:** This is my first time starting a "Challenge Thread" anywhere . . so I don't know what I am doing (really) . . I'm counting on you all to make this fun and interesting (or not) . . . also I am not responsible for anything bad that happen because of this challenge or your participation in it . . .um . . try not to eat too much of the kinds of fish that are highest in mercury, I guess . . . . . maybe also watch out for tainted clams (but that is not medical advice, as I am not a doctor) . . and any other appropriate Disclaimer type information!

Good Luck!!!**

### Glamazon — 2012-06-17T22:13:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31810>

I am in!  Starting tomorrow. Will be a challenge cause I go out of town this Thursday thru Sunday. Guess that means a canned salmon and an opener on those day. Oh and canned sardines. I love sardines and kippers.

Thanks August. You are doing great! I don't like rules much either. LOL

### nonchalant — 2012-06-17T22:34:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31812>

I'm in. I've been trying to eat at least a little seafood every day for a couple weeks anyway. Wild caught salmon, canned tuna, trout, shrimp, sea scallops, sardines, crawdads, whatever. I can't wait for autumn to dig into the oysters!

I don't think my skin can get any smoother since I started eating so much seafood, but it might because of the vitamin K2 Menatetrenone (5mg) that I started taking recently.

### LinD — 2012-06-17T22:58:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31815>

Had tilapia for lunch today with family at Boo Rays restaurant.

### AmyJ — 2012-06-18T02:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31847>

Great idea, I'm in. I was planning to do this anyway, all stocked up on seafood.

Last weekend I tried a BAB of 8 oz salmon cooked in CO & a can of sardines in olive oil. I was starving within 2 1/2 hours. I don't understand that. 8 oz of grass fed high fat ground beef holds me for 6 hours--that's my typical BAB. Thoughts, anyone? Seafood just doesn't seem to stick with me. And I don't feel particularly great after eating it. Not bad, just not good and not satiated like with beef. Think I'll try a mix of beef and fish in the morning.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-18T02:50:24+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-18T03:57:57+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-18T04:36:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31876>

I'm deep into my second month of eating seafood at least once a day, and my skin and hair are beautiful. Scars are diminishing. Fresh wild and canned salmon and sardines, canned mackerel, shrimp, oysters, and mussels make up the bulk of my fishy food. I love my diet and see no reason to stop now. Thanks for everything, Jack!

### Glamazon — 2012-06-18T05:43:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31888>

> **AmyJ;31953 wrote:** Great idea, I'm in. I was planning to do this anyway, all stocked up on seafood.
>
>
>
> Last weekend I tried a BAB of 8 oz salmon cooked in CO & a can of sardines in olive oil. I was starving within 2 1/2 hours. I don't understand that. 8 oz of grass fed high fat ground beef holds me for 6 hours--that's my typical BAB. Thoughts, anyone? Seafood just doesn't seem to stick with me. And I don't feel particularly great after eating it. Not bad, just not good and not satiated like with beef. Think I'll try a mix of beef and fish in the morning.

My stock answer is add more fat. I make sure the skin is crisped and I eat that too. I cook mine in ghee and then drench it in ghee at the table. Fish that way will hold me for 5-6 hrs sometimes longer. I think I will start adding MCT oil to it also. I have a hard time getting a half a pound in but that is my goal. I dont like the taste of coconut oil. Comes from being raised on the beaches in Florida and smelling that crappy suntan oil that smells like coconuts.

### Glamazon — 2012-06-18T05:47:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31889>

> **August;31917 wrote:** Please correctly me if I am wrong about this, but I have the impression that canned salmon is always/almost always wild caught salmon . . .
>
>
>
> I think I would likely opted for canned over farmed . . what do you think? I've heard that farmed salmon might be high in arachidonic acid (which might kind of defeat part of the purpose re: omega 6/omega 3 ratio and inflammation) . . I've also heard that it is a lot higher in environmental toxins (maybe like dioxin?) . . and also heavy metals . .
>
>
>
> Anyway thank you for the brilliant inspiration for this thread . . and for starting us off!
>
>
>
> Good Luck to you and wishes for excellent health benefits!

Yes, I think this too. I usually see wild caught on the can too. I think I read somewhere in the forum that Atlantic Salmon is all farm raised. Farm raised is also artificially colored pink because the farmed salmon can't get eat the pink shrimp like wild salmon can. Industrial salmon is supposed to be bad as industrial beef. I know the wild foods offers farmed sea food like mussles but with disclaimers that these were farmed responsibly. They are significantly cheaper than wild.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-18T13:14:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31902>

Count me in. My grocery began carrying Wild Caught Sockeye Salmon a few months ago so we have it at least 2 or 3 times a week. I can do it every day, not a problem at all. Love all seafood, except sea urchin (uni) - it's too strong.

Yesterday dinner: Tampa Bay blue crabs from Saturday's catch.

This morning: leftover Salmon & garlic.

I cook everything in lots of CO. I've read that using salt will cut down the coconut flavor, Glam, but haven't tried it because I love the flavor.

### SeaHorse — 2012-06-18T15:09:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31912>

I'm in too....I have to be particularly careful with seafood because of the histamine buildup that happens as soon as the fish/seafood dies. Canned stuff can be a problem, so fresh (within a day), fresh frozen or just out of the water is the only way I can eat these things now. I love sardines, etc. but they seem to bother me too much.

My husband stocked our pond with rainbow and brown trout last year, so we can eat REALLY fresh fish a few times a week during the summer. Best fried up with bacon and a squeeze of lemon!

I started last week trying to get one meal in a day....but this is a great way to keep on the path.

Kidneys for breakfast....Maine shrimp for dinner tonight.

I LOVE COD, on the grill, cooked in ghee and salt, cooked in ghee and seasoned w/taco seasoning and served over a bed of fresh romaine, covered in guacamole and fresh pico and jicama chips! as we get closer to summer solsitice I've noticed and increased craving for fish.... the smoked oysters and sardines cannot be kept in the house....

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-18T17:38:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31931>

Mahi mahi for BAB. I cannot stand cod...... it always cooks up into rubber or something no matter what I try.

### ealachan — 2012-06-18T17:54:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31935>

> **Souldanzer;32038 wrote:** Mahi mahi for BAB. I cannot stand cod...... it always cooks up into rubber or something no matter what I try.

You might be cooking it too long. Even with thick filets, I only cook cod for about 2.5 minutes per side. Carry-over heat will cook it the rest of the way through by the time you go to eat it. In the oven, I do 450 degrees for 15 minutes for thicker filets, 10 minutes for thinner ones.

### melrito — 2012-06-18T18:46:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31949>

I've actually been eating a meal (sometimes 2) of seafood a day for the past month and plan to continue that. I rotate between sockeye salmon with skin (my favorite), mackerel, cod, mahi mahi, tuna, scallops, shrimp, and on occasion swordfish - basically whatever I can get wild caught and will not eat anything farmed. I feel great and my hunger has decreased a lot (although that could be from CT, supplementation, or something else). I have not lost any weight, but I look better and clothes are getting looser. Skin is definitely improving and in addition to the fish I have been liberal with the coconut oil (eating as well as on the skin), although I have been using coconut oil for years and my skin has just improved the last few weeks - no longer as dry, very smooth and soft. I no longer supplement with krill oil since I'm getting so much seafood, but I do need to get my Omega 6:3 ratio tested to be sure it's good. I also no longer bruise as easily and if I do get a bruise, it heals very very quickly and is not as painful. Again, I don't know what of this is attributable to the seafood and what to the CT and other things I've been doing.

### BJK77 — 2012-06-18T18:47:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31950>

I've been doing this already, so I'll stick with it and join you. I always have beef and seafood for breakfast - sardines, salmon, shrimp, scallops, etc. It seems to randomly cause histamine reactions, but for now I'm just dealing with it and hoping the benefits outweigh any harm.

### Huck — 2012-06-18T18:53:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31952>

> **Souldanzer;32038 wrote:** Mahi mahi for BAB. I cannot stand cod...... it always cooks up into rubber or something no matter what I try.

The cod I buy at the grocery store is very flaky and tastes great. The cod I buy at the fish market cooks and tastes like rubber. There are different types of cod, plus other fish are often sold as cod, so maybe try cod from a different supplier. I cook it in butter and garlic and find a similarity to lobster in texture and taste.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-18T21:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31978>

My husband had a severe histamine reaction one night after eating tuna steak. From what I've read, histamines accumulate in fish as it ages and the longer the fish has been out of the water, the higher the concentrate of histamine. Tuna seems especially prone. Get your seafood as fresh as possible.

### SeaHorse — 2012-06-18T21:51:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-31988>

There is some good information about histamine and fish on the internet. There is a product called Histame which has Diamine Oxidase Enzyme in it. This will breakdown histamine in foods....it doesn't help with immune system allergic reactions (from what I understand).

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-32106>

I love this challenge!

I have been eating lots of wildcaught seafood last weeks and I love it. Often not only once / day. I love the taste, I love how it makes me feel. I guess it is great for the skin too. I do not get burnt, but get a nice color instead! I love how my skin looks these days, I am not the one blessed with great skin from birth, just sayin.. (my stomach has a little red color because I just had a icepack on it for 20 minutes)

..

I usually cook my fish just in a tiny bit water, add seasalt and curcumin and black pepper, and then as it is ready (I cook it about 1-2 minutes) I add a good amout of coconutoil if it is a non-fatty fish.

I eat a pound or more of it in one setting, and it keeps me satisfied for 5-6 hours. I just love it!!!

I eat my fish raw too, often. And I do the mackerel-head-cocktail if I have mackerels, maybe 1-5 times / month. My health-bomb...;

\+ water =

Taste is awful so I always close my nose though. But I figure it must be healthy.

Sometimes I get a little heart-palpations after drinking it, I wonder why.

Inger

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-23T18:11:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-33162>

> **Anna K.;33285 wrote:** I'm not sure if anybody else noticed that, but I naturally started craving more seafood after doing CT for 2 months. This was even before Dr. Kruse revealed factor X and how important seafood is.

Yes! I'm only at the end of Week 2 of CT (5x/wk @ 50F), and I've eaten seafood every single day. I was always a seafood hater; I don't think I ate it more than 3x/year (other than canned tuna).

I find I WANT it now! Yesterday, I made a huge salad with a whole avocado and fresh crabmeat, using CO as my dressing. OMG...yum!

I've been finding myself shopping in the seafood section which is actually foreign to me. I have NEVER (I'm 50!) shopped for seafood. So many choices.

### SeaHorse — 2012-06-25T22:04:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-30-day-seafood-challenge-thread.1920/post-33511>

"I always associated Scurvy with the sea/sailors . . also every since I learned that we were one of the few species that does not make our own . . "

August, the sailors got scurvy because they only ate salt pork and hard tack which was a flour and water biscuit on long voyages. I don't think they ate fish in the navy and it was the preserved meat and wheat diet that used to do them in (sounds familiar!) Even after people realized that citrus fruit would prevent scurvy, the navy didn't make it part of the sailors rations for many years.

some how I managed to read/assimilate BG1 & BG2 w/a 4yo and 2yo crawling over me here's a clip of the event:

4yo: mommy can I color?

me: yes here's the crayons - just don't color on the wall, daddy will kill mommy

4yo: why?

me: b/c daddy doesn't like messes

4yo: but the house is always messy

me: yes, do you know why?

4yo: b/c of our toys & mommy doesn't vacuum

me: you're so smart.

\*\*\*\*\* Continue reading BG1\*\*\*

2yo: mum, mum, mum sit eed

me: mommy's reading little dude

2yo: eed me mum

\*\*\*\* proceed to continue reading BG1 aloud to 2yo\*\*\*\*\*

\*\*\*\* 2yo climes down, gets coloring book, comes back to me\*\*\*\*

2yo: olor, eezze????

me: yes you can color - here's a box of crayons, sit in your chair

\*\*\*\* Finish BG1, start on BG2\*\*\*\*\*

4yo: mommy I need to go potty!!!!!!!!!

me: then go potty

4yo: mommy I'm done!

me: poopie or pee?

4yo: poopie!

me: Coming....

\*10min later sit back down to read BG2\*\*\*\*\*

2yo: atch eigo, atch eigo, eeezzeee, mum?

me: ok you can watch diego.

\*\*\*\*Back to reading BG2 and eventually finished it w/additional interruptions\*\*\*\*\*\*\*
