### Inger — 2013-01-02T01:52:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68963>

Nice fitness@home and Caroline! My aged heart is already about 3-4 months old and it is quite like ammonia..lol But I like it as I always liked those "ammonia" cheeses too! I am too sad when my jars are empty.. I need to order some new grassfed heart soon so I can make new batches.

If someone does not feel any difference eating their high meat it might be you need a larger amount! There is no upper limit AFAIK..
I had fresh oysters yesterday and then some high heart (about 4 "mouth large" chunks).. and my sleep was just AMAZING.

I woke up at 6 PM and felt like in heaven..huh!

### caroline — 2013-01-02T02:34:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68969>

I have gotten sick twice now from oysters ..... JK says to stick with it. It kind of gives me the creeps - but I will try again. The last time I had them on Christmas day - no one else got sick so it couldn't have been a bad batch I don't think.
I am thinking - Inger .... if you had oysters and high meat before going to bed ..... does that give you jungle mouth???? It might be good that you are sleeping alone for the moment anyway ...LOL

### Inger — 2013-01-02T03:23:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68975>

> **caroline said**
>
> I have gotten sick twice now from oysters ..... JK says to stick with it. It kind of gives me the creeps - but I will try again. The last time I had them on Christmas day - no one else got sick so it couldn't have been a bad batch I don't think.
> I am thinking - Inger .... if you had oysters and high meat before going to bed ..... does that give you jungle mouth???? It might be good that you are sleeping alone for the moment anyway ...LOL

Caroline...LOL no! I always rinse and take care my mouth does not smell like high meat..huh! I often brush my teeths after too.
I actually love how my breath never smell anymore. I recognize it too well when others breath smell.. It is weird because if one thinks about that I eat high meat and such it could make ones breath smell but it does not. So nothing to fear!

Just rinse your mouth well after eating it!

Ha. Guess what, I as not sleeping alone. My ex slept in my bed. He likes to stay by my place quite often and he is allowed too when he follows my rules, I cannot say no if someone wants the good things you see. My rules are quite harsh, early bedtime, cold and dark home, and some more.

About oysters. I wonder if it could be a sort of detox too, or adjustment to bacteria or such? If no others got sick it still might have been it is not you, because often it might be just one oyster from the batch that is not good. Try to always smell it first, if it smells like ocean and fresh it is good. If it smells not so good it could have died and oysters that are dead more than one hour start to secret a poison that makes us sick. It is nothing serious or deadly but you will throw up and get sick in your stomach.
Just be careful the oysters are fresh and smell them carefully before eating. If they are almost dried out they are older too. The more water they have still inside the fresher they are. So that is a good sign. I have been eating almost dried out oysters too without issues but it is riskier. I am just not scared at all my stomach digests almost stones me thinks..lol
I do once got nausea after eating oysters a few weeks ago and I throw them up. I was fine again immediately after.

Do not ever give up Caroline, it gets better and better!

### caroline — 2013-01-02T03:32:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68977>

Inger - the funny thing is .... The oysters are sitting on rock salt - and it is the rock salt \[or whatever it is\] that gives me the creeps!

### caroline — 2013-01-02T03:34:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68978>

I don't just throw up - I get severe bloating and the runs too - so it has to be something.

### Inger — 2013-01-02T04:02:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68982>

> **caroline said**
>
> I don't just throw up - I get severe bloating and the runs too - so it has to be something.

Oh Caroline.. I feel for you!
Hmmm.. on rock salt? Are you eating them in a restaurant? Next time can you ask to get them without the salt? In Europe the serve oysters on ice.. not on salt. Are you sure it is the salt? Because it should not touch the oyster if it is in the one half shell, like they use to be served.. still "swimming" in the seawater that are inside.

How are you exactly eating the oysters Caroline? Are they fresh shucked? It is very important you shuck them right before eating and that they are still alive.

About the runs and bloating.. I guess I did not got that because I threw up so fast after. I did it immediately after I felt the nausea coming. Might have saved me from more troubles..
I know people get serious "food poisoning" for days from shellfish and that is why I am cautious and if I feel bad after eating them I do not hesitate to get the foods up. I am not too willing to suffer for days. But it has only happened once so far.

Caroline if you are sensible I would be very careful that the oysters are fresh and better buy them yourself and shuck them right before eating! It is quite fun and a challenge to be great at shucking oysters! One can never be sure what is going on in restaurant kitchens..lol I say that as a waitress that have seen a lot...

### Inger — 2013-01-02T04:08:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68984>

Caroline do you always got into troubles eating oysters? Or are there times you have eaten them raw and felt fine after?
Not that you have a allergy or something?

### caroline — 2013-01-02T04:56:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68988>

I have been eating them for about a year now. I buy them from a very large and busy fish market. They are shucked and sitting on a bed of salt. Actually - the first time I got sick about 6 weeks ago - was the worst. The second time - I was at a friend's and she got them from the same fish market - already shucked. This time I knew to throw up right away and get rid of it so I was less sick. Jack says to keep trying - so I will. I don't think it is an allergy but the salt really did give me the creeps. funny. Remember that we had a restaurant for 35 years - LOL . However we had an open kitchen - you could always see everything that was going on!

### Inger — 2013-01-02T13:26:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69024>

Caroline, they were both times shucked? Hmmm.. how long time does it went from they beeing shucked unril you ate them? Should never be more than an hour max! In restaurants I worked in, where fresh oysters were on the menu, the chef shucked them only after a guest had ordered some and they was in front of the guests, on ice, as soon as possible.

Caroline, your restaurant with the open kitchen sound so cool! I was often in the most famous and high quality sushi restaurant in Hamburg, Henssler&Henssler (my ex lover, the most lovable man I ever met, always brought me there) and there was an open kitchen also! I loved it! The food was incredible.. Nice memories.

### kathylu — 2013-01-02T15:19:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69035>

I used to work on a science vessel where we had a small oyster dredge (on the Chesapeake Bay). One of my fond memories was taking the oysters we dredged back to the lab and then steaming them just until they opened (in the autoclave) and eating them with lemon, horseradish and hot sauce. Washed down with vodka we distilled ourselves...

Sometimes it's good to be a scientist, even though we are mostly geeks...lol!

### kathylu — 2013-01-02T15:24:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69038>

I've tried eating a few oysters raw from the pints of shucked oysters you get at the seafood counter in the grocery store. They taste fine and I haven't gotten sick, but it does make me a bit nervous. I know a lot more about what's IN oysters than I would like too...lol. It's a little hard psychologically to eat them (not knowing how long ago they were shucked), so I have just been swallowing them whole. No problems so far, knock on wood.

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-05T23:09:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69537>

I just wrote an article for the Vermont Sheep and Goat Assoc. newsletter on organ meats recipes. This was requested by my shearer, who is also a butcher. She hates to see so many good organ meats going to waste. When she found out I was a fan, I got this writing assignment...I used to free-lance, but never did any cooking articles! Next year I'll offer to write one on eating RAW organ meats....we'll see how it goes.

My new favourite oyster is the Thatch Island oysters from Mass. I really love their taste....they just happen to be the hardest to shuck. I'm now wearing gauntlets to prevent bloodshed.

### nonchalant — 2013-01-07T16:21:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69771>

Inger, over the Christmas holidays I didn't stir my jar of high meat for quite a while. I had left the jar lid on loosely, like you mentioned, but when I did check on it, I didn't care for the smell. So I stirred it again, and this time I put a few layers of cheesecloth between the jar and the lid, and again put the lid on loosely. Once again I went out of town, and neglected the jar for several days. But this time when I checked it, it smelled wonderful and inviting.
Leaving the lid off the jar is not an option for me, but the cloth seems to work quite well.

### fitness@home — 2013-01-28T00:40:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-72931>

> [Nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=69771)
>
> Inger, over the Christmas holidays I didn't stir my jar of high meat for quite a while. I had left the jar lid on loosely, like you mentioned, but when I did check on it, I didn't care for the smell. So I stirred it again, and this time I put a few layers of cheesecloth between the jar and the lid, and again put the lid on loosely. Once again I went out of town, and neglected the jar for several days. But this time when I checked it, it smelled wonderful and inviting.
> Leaving the lid off the jar is not an option for me, but the cloth seems to work quite well.

I also used Inger's advice and left my jar lid just a bit cracked when we had to go out of own for a week. I will admit to being scared to open the fridge when we got back. Was expecting a foul smell when I opened the door, but it was fine. The meat itself was definitely "riper".

It is now about two months old. My goal is to take a couple pieces out to eat each time I stir and air it (about every three days). Now, by eat I mean pop it into my mouth and wash it down quickly with a beverage...have to admire those of you that actually chew it.

Has anyone used free range pork heart for this purpose?

Nonchalant, I will try your cheesecloth method next time we are heading out of town for week or more.

### Inger — 2013-01-28T12:30:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-72982>

Cool idea with the cheesecloth! It looks to prevent the meat drying out because that is what will stop he ferment process me thinks. It needs to be moist..

Anna,
the high meat has no real time to be "ready" I guess.. because it just ferments more and more so it gets more and more effect.. I think after 1-2 months it already has a pretty good effect. But one needs to try out this. Might be the younger the meat the more you need to eat to get the benefits.

If it has gone bad.. I gues when it smells off. It should smell a bit like eggs.. or an smelly cheese or so. But not like a cow barn..hmm hard to explain. I did a batch with elk tongue but I ditched that. It smelled like a barn. I guess the tongue was dirty, I should have washed it well or take off the skin.. it made wrong bacterias grow.
People eat 1 year old high meat and older so there is not really any time when it goes bad in that sense me thinks.. if you only have the good bacteria in it. It just starts to taste pretty strong like ammonia. Like very old cheeses do too!

### Inger — 2013-01-28T17:32:19+0200

You know Diane.. I kind of suspect it is because it was prefrozen. I would personally not use prefrozen.. When freezing some enzymes die and we need them all to make nice high meat. You can make high meat from muscle meat too! It does not need to be heart if you can only get that prefrozen.
I think prefrozen hearts are superfood too but for high meat they are less ideal.. make a carpaccio out of them instead!

The last few days I have incorporated uncooked buffalo liver and mixed organ meats (ground) warmed in butter and olive oil but not cooked. I am not ready yet to incorporate high meat into my diet.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-18T03:36:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-80930>

> [GlenPDQ said:](/goto/post?id=80830)
>
> Let's say I cube up some raw heart for high meat and wanted to spike it with some probiotics the same way milk is innoculated to make yogurt. Any suggestions what one would use? I have a bottle of probiotics onhand which contains Lactobacillus, Leuconostoc, Kluyveromyces, Brettanomyces, Debaryomyces, Saccharomyces, and Torulospora strains.
> .

Glen, I think if the meat were cooked, then you would need to add probiotics. Pasteurized milk needs them to make yogurt, but raw milk does not. I have made yogurt from raw milk several times, often unintentionally while giving some to my chickens. What they don't drink becomes yogurt with the heat of the sun. They seem to love that yogurt even more than the milk. I also would use the whey from making raw yogurt in my pickles. (I don't heat the raw milk much, only to body temp.) But recently I have considered leaving it out, because perhaps dairy bacteria aren't necessarily the proper microbes for vegetables. Salt seems to work fine, along with the normal bacteria present in the cucumbers.

Raw meat already has the beneficial bacteria needed to get off to a good start. Making sure it has oxygen keeps the anaerobic bacteria at bay, thankfully.

> Sally Fallon's book 'Nourishing Traditions' has some good raw meat and fish recipes...I've tried most of them and love them! Have yet to try high meat...I think if I do I will be innoculating it with a bit of whey to ensure I grow good bacteria.

I love Sally's Kibbeh recipe. Instead of using bulghur, I use raw ground, pastured beef, cooked/cooled quinoa, shallot, parsley, ginger, chopped up kraut, capers, lemon juice, salt, pepper.

### caroline — 2014-02-10T00:10:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-109329>

That sounds delicious - I always loved Kibbeh and have forgotten about it! I wouldn't use the quinoa tho - for me anyway.

No, I don't have the sleep problems (well, I do but they are called Dylan and Elliot - two one year olds who don't sleep well.) I've recently convinced my mom to go low carb again and now she isn't sleeping well. She said she had this problem before when she ate low carb. I guess she is waking up a LOT in the middle of the night. Thoughts? Suggestions?

### janagram — 2012-06-25T23:28:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-and-slep-problems.2039/post-33532>

Have her eat nothing for 4 hrs before bed. Also perhaps have her take a good enzyme with her food.

I have had problems with sleep for a long time, cannot say if paleo eating has made it worse....but I am workin on it, currently getting labs done and will report anything I learn.

### chocolate — 2012-06-26T04:56:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-and-slep-problems.2039/post-33588>

I couldn't sleep until I added fat. And If I wake up, and don't doze back off, I eat a spoon of coconut fat. It works very quickly.

### Rick Martin — 2012-06-28T12:03:00+0300

Chocolate, how much fat do you eat every day? Can you guesstimate?

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-28T16:16:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-and-slep-problems.2039/post-34146>

### chocolate — 2012-06-28T16:28:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-and-slep-problems.2039/post-34151>

I probably eat too much. the no butter thing sent me reeling. I use it on coffee and tea.... probably about 4 tablespoons. In the night I put it on tea and sip it off the top. I also take melatonin if I think I need it... there is no such thing as too much. It heals your guts also.

# Thread: How did our paleo ancestors get salt?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-did-our-paleo-ancestors-get-salt.2041/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-06-25T23:33:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-did-our-paleo-ancestors-get-salt.2041/post-33533>

I just heard (or read) some paleo expert suggest that they no doubt used animal blood, which is very salty. Somewhere along the line that was considered not to be a good practise, possibly for religious reasons. (Old Testament prohibits. Says the spirit of the animal is in the blood.) And sometime long ago men went in search of salt. Have you ever seen the film "Saltmen of Tibet"? Very interesting.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-26T00:23:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-did-our-paleo-ancestors-get-salt.2041/post-33539>

From the sea? Either seawater or salt flats, I'd guess. I hadn't thought of blood. I know we have "sweat bees" that lick salt off of humans and other animals.

### Michael — 2012-06-26T09:18:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-did-our-paleo-ancestors-get-salt.2041/post-33619>

> **janagram;33670 wrote:** I just heard (or read) some paleo expert suggest that they no doubt used animal blood, which is very salty. Somewhere along the line that was considered not to be a good practise, possibly for religious reasons. (Old Testament prohibits. Says the spirit of the animal is in the blood.) And sometime long ago men went in search of salt. Have you ever seen the film "Saltmen of Tibet"? Very interesting.
>
>
> Certainly blood has had religious significance. It's been important in the theology of sacrifice, for example. In Old Testament times the altar in the temple at Jerusalem (destroyed by the Romans) would have run with blood.
>
>
>
> I wonder - perhaps the significance of blood in early religion is indicative of its dietary importance.
>
>
>
> There's a thought among some of the later prophets that what God really wants is not food in the form of sacrifices, but, in the religious language, a pure and contrite heart. The psalmist (50:12) has God say:
>
>
>
> If I be hungry, I will not tell thee
>
> for the whole world is mine, and all that is therein.
>
> Thinkest thou that I will eat bulls’ flesh
>
> and drink the blood of goats?

Why the Jewish law had animals be drained of blood when slaughtered I don't know. I don't even know if anyone knows. I haven't looked up the books of the law in the OT to see what's said. And, of course, what's said there may only be a rationalization of something quite simple - maybe it would make the meat keep longer in a hot climate, for example.

In any event, the Christian Church decided not to follow Jewish dietary law very early on. The story is told in _Acts. So the way blood is treated in kosher practice has been irrelevant to the West. It's also, of course, had no influence in places like the Far East.

I expect people probably made more use of mined salt when they had less fresh meat, hence blood, to go round and lived in settled civilizations that could, or were prepared to, undertake more organized activities like salt panning or mining.

Settled civilizations, living on high-carbohydrate diets, seem to have needed the salt. I understand that the enzymes used in carbohydrate metabolism are sodium-dependent. Hence, I think you can get in trouble if you eat a lot of plant material and eliminate salt as a "health" strategy. Archaeologists will sometimes point out the connection between settlement, diet, and the changing needs for salt, based upon diet. For example, Michael D. Coe in _The Maya_ says that Maya civilization was predicated on a particular salt deposit. Without it, it could not have existed - because settled populous society had to run on farmed maize, rather than caught game, and that required salt in the diet:

[http://www.amazon.com/Seventh-Edition-Ancient-Peoples-Places/dp/B0085S065I/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Seventh-Edition-Ancient-Peoples-Places/dp/B0085S065I/)

With low carb I guess you might get away with very little salt.

On very_ low carb you seem to run into a different problem - the kidneys dump water and sodium, so you need to replace what's lost.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-33557>

**Who sells them (at reasonable price)?

I see them sold freeze dry, meant for aquarium fish food.**

I think they can be eaten whole.

Some Philipino's that I used to work with, eat shrimps whole, shell and all.

[http://ecoscope.com/krill/index.htm](http://http://ecoscope.com/krill/index.htm)

[http://ecoscope.com/krill/krill5/index.htm](http://http://ecoscope.com/krill/krill5/index.htm)

> Special: some scientists describe Euphausia superba as the once most abundant multicellular species of the planet - some scientists describe Euphausia superba as one of the most flexible of our planet - some describe Euphausia superba as the most physiologically dynamic - some as a most interestingly designed - a famous scientist as "of almost extraterrestrial appearance, endowed with magical powers ", another: "The high importance of lowly krill"- and most of those few humans who were lucky to observe Euphausia superba alive, under water, lights still on, with the right optics, as utmost beautiful and fascinating
>
>
>
> estimation of biomass between 50 - 900 million tonnes (>900 000 000 000 000 individuals), some estimates are as high as 1.5 billion tonnes - estimation of annual production as high as a billion tonnes - central predator/prey organism in the antarctic ecosystem - key stone organism - current fishery ca. 80 000 tonnes per year.

### purplemonster — 2012-06-28T04:44:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-34098>

Why eat fresh, whole krill? Why not just take a krill oil supplement?

### JanSz — 2012-06-28T05:47:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-34108>

> **purplemonster;34253 wrote:** Why eat fresh, whole krill? Why not just take a krill oil supplement?

Whole krill is a sea food, like shrimp or fish (but likely much better).

When we eat shrimp or fish we have pollutants in mind.

Krill is on the bottom of food chain, I expect minimal pollutants.

That should make krill the best sea food there is, (not sure how it tastes but garlic, sea salt @ hot pepper can help a lot).

Fish oil contains EPA & DHA that we treasure. Fish does not make them, it gets them thru food chain from krill.

EPA & DHA are made by Krill.

Fish oil does not have phospholipids, krill oil does have phospholipids.

Not sure, I guess phospholipids also originate within krill.

We do not discuss phospholipids much.

Everybody but specially older people and sick, should supplement with phospholipids.

Bottle of PS from BodyBio (300 pills) costs $150 , PS=(PhosphatidylCholine + other phospholipids and ALA & LA in 4/1 ratio)

Either fish oil or krill oil have proportionally too much DHA for the amount of contained EPA.

Supposedly ideal ratio is EPA/DHA=3/1

Krill or fish oil are human products, EPA/DHA proportions are likely skewed due to manufacturing process.

Eating krill directly would likely overcome this. Krill is as natural food as it gets.

/////////////////

//

### JanSz — 2012-07-09T01:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-36260>

[http://www.rimfrostkrill.com/press-release-olympic-seafood-as-introduces-its-flagship-brand-the-rimfrost-krill-collection-at-vitafoods-2012/](http://http://www.rimfrostkrill.com/press-release-olympic-seafood-as-introduces-its-flagship-brand-the-rimfrost-krill-collection-at-vitafoods-2012/)

PRESS RELEASE: Olympic Seafood AS Introduces its Flagship Brand, the RIMFROST KRILL COLLECTION at Vitafoods 2012

on MAY 4, 2012 Â· in PRESS RELEASES

FosnavÃ¥g, Norway – April 26th, 2012 — Norwegian firm Olympic Seafood AS launches their flagship brand, the RIMFROST KRILL COLLECTION at the 2012 Vitafoods trade fair from May 22-24 in Geneva, Switzerland.

The RIMFROST KRILL COLLECTION is a new premium range of products from the shrimp-like marine crustacean **Euphausia Superba (Antarctic krill)** that includes krill meal, krill powder and krill oil that are high in omega-3 fatty acids in the bio-efficient phospholipid form. The RIMFROST COLLECTION maintains its intrinsic goodness by immediate and gentle two-step enzymatic processing on-board their specialised krill-collecting vessel ‘Juvel’.

─Olympic Seafood AS focuses exclusively on Antarctic krill ingredients for consumer applications and is deeply committed to sustainable fishing practices and eco-responsibility, explains sales and marketing director Even T. RemÃ¸y.

─We take great pride in ‘Juvel,’ the newest krill vessel on the ocean, which utilises industry-leading technology and aims for low emissions, low energy use and low climate impact.

Having their own vessel guarantees 100% traceability and is also certified by Friend of the Sea to ensure it reaches the highest possible standards in sustainability. In addition, Juvel is always carrying an international observer from the Convention on the Conservation of Antarctic Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR), which is reporting observations and scientific data to CCAMLR. It is important to note that many other krill vessels do not carry these observers, which can result in non-sustainable practices.

The Antarctic waters are the cleanest in the world and krill from these waters are naturally extremely low in pollutants, giving pure products where no extra purification is needed. Antarctic krill (Euphausia Superba) can tolerate the icy conditions due to their rich content of nourishing nutrients: phospholipid bound omega-3 fatty acids, other lipids, protein and antioxidants. In humans omega-3 phospholipids are more efficiently absorbed and utilised by the body as well as causing significantly less after taste than other forms of omega-3.

─Our exceptional onboard processing technology allows us to produce highly stable and unique raw material that is the best possible for SFE extraction, explains RemÃ¸y.

─This gives our distinct clear red minimally processed oil and powders with superior quality. No extra purification steps are necessary since the SFE extraction gives oil with high purity and favorable viscosity.

─We are proud of our products but we are also proud to provide a stable supply of goods with an established stock of raw materials as well as finished products, says RemÃ¸y.

─Our reserve of raw materials and ready products ensure all orders have a high delivery precision and security for customers, details RemÃ¸y.

Product Range

RIMFROST GENUINE is Antarctic krill meal where only the fluoride containing krill shells and water are removed before mild low-temperature drying and packaging of the product while still on board the Juvel. RIMFROST GENUINE has a characteristic reddish-brownish colour and fresh shrimp-like odour and taste. Just the way it should be!

RIMFROST PRISTINE is Antarctic krill powder that is not only high in omega-3 fatty acids in the bio-efficient phospholipid form but also lush with nourishing marine nutrients like peptides and antioxidants. RIMFROST PRISTINE also uses a rapid enzymatic processing on-board to derive its characteristic reddish colour and fresh shrimp-like odour.

RIMFROST SUBLIME is Antarctic krill oil high in omega-3 fatty acids in the bio-efficient phospholipid form, with a characteristic clear red colour and low odour with a hint of fresh shellfish. These traits are captured with a two-step process including gentle SFE extraction.

FRESH FROZEN KRILLS

Pacifica krill, ideal for small and medium sized marine and tropical fish

ITEM # DESCRIPTION SIZE & PACKAGING

FP200 Fresh frozen Pacifica Krill 10 kgs poly bag bulk in totes

FS300 Fresh frozen Superba Krill 24 kgs. 2x12 kgs poly boxed

### JanSz — 2012-07-14T22:09:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-37825>

> **Anna K.;37858 wrote:** Is Krill better then shrimp???

Speaking of food chain, the higher the more poluted.

Krill is on very bottom of food chain.

Krill eats micro-algae that contain (valuable) DHA

Krill have inside factory that produces EPA and phospholipids

cell membranes are made of phospholipids

phospholipids are mostly PhosphatidylCholine also little of PhosphatidylEtanolamine and PhoshadtidylInositol and minor Glycolipids.

\----------

asking for help

BodyBio.com sells PC (PhosphatidylCholine)

it contains not only above phospholipids

but also

ALA(O6) and LA(O3) at the ratio of 4:1

both are vegetable oils

I would like to figure if

phospholipids in krill contain any other Fatty Acids (other than EPA & DHA), specially if there is any ALA & LA

important, I got curious when learned that grass fed beef contains ALA & LA (vegetable oils)

(mistigi how are you doing, tx for this info)

# Thread: Diminished dermal distress
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diminished-dermal-distress.2046/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-06-26T06:38:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diminished-dermal-distress.2046/post-33604>

This weekend I walked into a Texas Bull Nettle plant, while wearing sandals. Instant pain!! I was able to wash that foot about 5 minutes later, but the pain had already subsided. I was stunned. It looked like I had a dozen mosquito bites. I was amazed at the lack of pain, because a couple years ago I learned the hard way to avoid this weed. I was hurting intensely for at least an hour after my first encounter.

I think I have CT to thank for this, though I haven't been taking ice baths for 6 weeks. I do take (relatively warm) tapwater showers, lay on top of an ice pack several times a week, and I've gone to the lake a few times. So maybe it \*is\* possible to stay semi cold adapted during the Texas summer?

Earlier this year I noticed the lack of pain (after the first few seconds) for a wasp sting, and also a grease splatter. But I was taking ice baths at that time. This is amazing, stuff Dr. K.

### Destiny — 2012-06-26T13:11:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diminished-dermal-distress.2046/post-33626>

> **Nonchalant;33741 wrote:** This weekend I walked into a Texas Bull Nettle plant, while wearing sandals. Instant pain!! I was able to wash that foot about 5 minutes later, but the pain had already subsided. I was stunned. It looked like I had a dozen mosquito bites. I was amazed at the lack of pain, because a couple years ago I learned the hard way to avoid this weed. I was hurting intensely for at least an hour after my first encounter.
>
>
>
> I think I have CT to thank for this, though I haven't been taking ice baths for 6 weeks. I do take (relatively warm) tapwater showers, lay on top of an ice pack several times a week, and I've gone to the lake a few times. So maybe it \*is\* possible to stay semi cold adapted during the Texas summer?
>
>
>
> Earlier this year I noticed the lack of pain (after the first few seconds) for a wasp sting, and also a grease splatter. But I was taking ice baths at that time. This is amazing, stuff Dr. K.

My grandma used to step intentionally into nettles. She believed it was helpful with her arthritis. She lived to be almost 97.

# Thread: Breakfast
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-06-26T11:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33622>

What did you have then?

Me, I was in a hurry and tripping over myself trying to do lots of different things. Took fish oil. I ate some cold bacon from the fridge. Then I got a big bunch of organic spinach, washed it, spun it nearly dry in a salad spinner, and wilted it in a covered pan over very low heat with an occasional stir. I then put it in a dish and added a tablespoon of ghee. Swimming in it.

Then I had to get out the house. So I ate two hardboiled duck eggs on the train and bought my coffee at the rail station.

About 680 kcal comprising 74% fat, 23% protein, 3% carbs.

My %age of carbs will go up over the day and protein down with salads and fruit. I'll probably still be fairly low carb. I guess I could be higher the season being what it is, but I've been experimenting with lower carb at the moment.

### Inger — 2012-06-26T12:05:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33625>

I have been eating pollock last days, as it is really cheap here and wildcaught.

I eat about 400 grams, lightly cooked with curcumin, black pepper and seasalt in some water or fishbroth, just a minute or so, and then I add a big spoon of coconut oil on the top. This is totally yummy and so easy, takes no time at all! It holds me satiated for 5 hours easily. A lot of protein and fat.

Then I have my bulletproofcoffee.. I make it with organic coffee, kardamom, 2 eggyolks and a big spoonful of coconut oil.

I blend it and it gets so very yummy and frothy! Boah.. I am so in love with it I want it every morning! A big cup.

Then I go and pick a few fresh wild edibles and herbs from the garden. I might have a few spoonful of wild berries too for dessert.

### fitness@home — 2012-06-26T13:15:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33627>

I just finished 5 oz of fresh wild caught gulf shrimp lightly sautÃ©d with ghee and turmeric and two slices of uncured bacon.

Had BP coffee with 1TBL of Kerrygold unsalted butter and MCT oil. I found a local supplier for single origin, washed coffee beans. He does not roast the beans until you place the order. Love trying the different coffee beans...just received a very good Colombian and have yet to grind some of the Ethiopian beans.

Inger, I adore my BP coffee also...

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T16:46:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33658>

Today I had about 8oz (weight before cooking) of home-made pork sausage, two eggs, and some BP coffee.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-26T21:59:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33747>

I had my usual, and it makes the best breakfast/lunch/dinner, whatever! Some kind of meat or fish sauteed in lots of coconut oil, and then sauteed ginger/garlic/green onions/and lots of coleslaw (really just grated cabbage and carrots). I really, really like it made with scallops. Often I'll do scallops and cod, or pollock. Salmon is delicious as well.

### vkiernan — 2012-06-27T03:30:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33835>

BAB was 2 pieces bacon, 2 sausage links, 1 venison brat and 3 pieces can. bacon all mixed up and fried in bacon grease. I also fried up 3 eggs along with all of this. Don't know where I put all this food but I am not really stuffed afterwards. I also add turmeric and pepper to my fried eggs. I really enjoy this BAB!

### janagram — 2012-06-27T23:41:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-34046>

today I had grassfed grnd beef with onions, cilantro, sheep cheese, salsa, and lactosefree organic sour cream. yum.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-06-28T02:45:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-34079>

This AM...grass fed burger, 2 eggs, and 4 large mushrooms cooked in CO and kerrygold.

### purplemonster — 2012-06-28T04:44:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-34099>

I ate hotdog and chocolate drink.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-30T23:34:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-34678>

I had sockeye salmon heads and raw oysters for breakfast with a garden greens salad and homemade dressing. I was full after the raw oysters and only ate one head.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/34838=287-salmon-heads-oysters.jpg)

If you are wondering how to eat a fish head start with the meaty top of the head. It is full of fat. I like to eat the skin too. I then eat the cheeks and tongue. I eat the eye balls and spit out the cornea and eat the back of the eye. Suck out and eat the brain for more fat, if you can stomach all the rich food. It is strange how the best part of the fish is now considered "garbage" for the modern eater.

# Thread: iron and CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-06-26T15:12:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/post-33636>

I've been donating whole blood every 8 weeks for the last 5+ years.

This is the first time I've ever failed the hematocrit test. (11.7 and the cutoff is 12.5)

So....I've been CTing since February and lost about 21 llbs.

That's the only change up I've done - is my body using up the iron to make more mitochondria?

### earlyriser — 2012-06-26T18:02:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/post-33686>

Here in Australia they only allow us to donate every 3 months. Maybe every 8 weeks is too soon?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-06-26T18:21:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/post-33690>

It could be. A big reason why I started them up to begin with is I was worried about having high iron levels - since I am a male and been eating low carb for decades. So I was looking for a way to shed excess iron and also help someone out. Looks like maybe I shed enough! But I'm curious if CT has somehow changed things for me.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-06-27T16:50:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/post-33932>

I found this guy: [http://www.ayubmed.edu.pk/JAMC/PAST/14-2/AhmedBadar.htm](http://http://www.ayubmed.edu.pk/JAMC/PAST/14-2/AhmedBadar.htm)

And since I was doing the maximum per year (6), and this study showed 4 times in 2 years, I'm chalking this up to I finally made a dent in my iron stores after all of these years.

# Thread: Out,  out damn light!

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 26

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-26T15:29:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33639>

I'm trying to make the bedroom as dark as humanly possible. I also want it stylish and attractive. I've got nice room darkening shades, but even then it still lets light in at the sides.

What products/types of window coverings are you using that work really well. If you are covering the whole opening with aluminum foil, I'd love to hear about it - but not exactly the direction I want to go in. I want my house to be attractive to future buyers.

I see lots of products in my line of work, but people tend to get weirded out if I ask if we can shut all the shades and turn out the lights so I can see how dark it is

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-26T15:34:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33642>

We got room darkening shades from blinds to go or next day blinds. they do an amazing job. the were really expensive though b/c our windows are close to 6ft in width, and 3ft in height in the bedrooms! the do let some light in from the side - but its far less than what we used to deal w/our old crappy world market shades, and the neighbors spotlight which is on all freaking night! not sure how to eclipse the side light - ok here's an idea - what about putting some decorative valances down the side - so that when the darkening shades are pulled - the valances bloc what little light might come through the side???? decorative and useful - especially if you get a heavy fabric w/light blocking paneling?

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-26T15:36:28+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-26T16:52:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33659>

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-26T17:12:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33666>

I ordered a covering that adheres via velcro strips to the window frame. It blocks all light and can be removed during the day if you want to. The velcro strips are either black or white (which have a sticky back, so that'll stick to the window frame.... not sure how it comes off).

[http://www.blackoutez.com/](http://http://www.blackoutez.com/)

There won't be any air circulation either... just so you know. A/C required at least in the summer if you use it. And if you decide to try it, order it big enough to go all the way to the corners of your window frame to ensure all light is blocked.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33670>

Someone with my last name was Mayor years ago. Once the polls closed I snagged a bunch of the signs that only said for Mayor. I can put one in the window to cast a shadow on my head if the neighbors turn on their too bright street lights. I have been thinking about framing some thick white PVC I have. Then setting it on the sill and using magnets attracted to the cornerbead in the window frame to hold it up right. The windows are large enough I may not have a good place to set the frames during the day. Maybe a genius idea will show up on this thread.

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T17:55:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33684>

> **Cavemam;33797 wrote:** I do have the rest of that blue painters tape left over after doing the tub drain. How shall I list this in the MLS:
>
>
>
> Features: Coveted decorative painters tape accents in master bed.

Painter's tape might not actually be a bad idea, since it's designed to come off of painted surfaces without damaging them. I don't know how it holds up to long-term use though, ie: whether or not it starts to leave a sticky residue like duct tape will after a while.

Are you actively showing the house now? If not, then just use the tape until you DO start to show the house?

Also, not sure if it's been mentioned already, but are drapes a possibility? You could pull them over enough that they blocked out the edges of the blinds where the light is showing through.

I need to get a blind for the window in my room. We live out in the country so it's fairly dark at night anyway, but in the winter time especially, if there's a bright moon it's pretty bright in our room. Full moons when there's snow on the ground make it almost daylight brightness in there. I tack a bath towel up over the window on nights like that (it's a casement window, so it's tall and narrow).

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-26T18:04:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33687>

> **ealachan;33822 wrote:**
>
>
>
> I need to get a blind for the window in my room. We live out in the country so it's fairly dark at night anyway, but in the winter time especially, if there's a bright moon it's pretty bright in our room. Full moons when there's snow on the ground make it almost daylight brightness in there. I tack a bath towel up over the window on nights like that (it's a casement window, so it's tall and narrow).

I don't think that's a mismatch.... if it doesn't come from artificial light. My body loved the natural light cycles when I lived in the mountains. Even my cycle was in tune with them. I purposely left the window covers open... and looked at the pretty moon while falling asleep.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-26T18:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33688>

Years ago when I made "warm window" curtains, I wanted a good tight seal. I put thin adhesive magnetic strips on the long edges of the curtains and the walls adjacent to the window. The magnets do a great job of holding the curtain tight against the wall and blocking light. The strips can be painted to match the wall and they blend in fairly well. That might not be acceptable for showing your house; if not, I'd remove them and repaint the walls (which you might do anyway before selling).

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T18:14:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33689>

> **Souldanzer;33825 wrote:** I don't think that's a mismatch.... if it doesn't come from artificial light. My body loved the natural light cycles when I lived in the mountains. Even my cycle was in tune with them. I purposely left the window covers open... and looked at the pretty moon while falling asleep.

It's pretty, but it keeps me awake. Do not want!

### nonchalant — 2012-06-26T18:29:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33694>

> **ealachan;33795 wrote:** Duct tape around the edges? Duct tape fixes everything...

Ealachan you're a scream!

### janagram — 2012-06-26T19:43:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33706>

about to order EZblackout curtain!! custom...dunno price yet....

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-26T20:48:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33728>

don't try to save on the custom size... I tried and ended up having some light shine through b/c it's a bit small. Order it so it will go all the way into the corners of the window ... not just the frame.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-26T21:24:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33737>

By eye masks.......cheaper and does not affect room.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-26T17:02:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/loaning-kindle-books.2051/post-33662>

my understanding is that you can only loan books, btwn kindles on the same account - I looked into this b/c my mom/dad have 2 kindles and some books I want to read - but we couldn't figure out how to transfer the books btwn the different accounts.... then I sent an email to amazon - and they informed me it was only btwn kindles on the same account.... I was quite bummed... If they've changed it...or there's a way to loan btwn different accounts I'm all in!!!

### thyme — 2012-06-26T17:11:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/loaning-kindle-books.2051/post-33664>

You can loan, for 14 days, books that are loan-able to another kindle account. [Lending Kindle Books](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html?nodeId=200549320).

Shijin, you can also put books from your father's account onto your mother's kindle, and vice-versa, for keeps. It involves one-time password sharing, so is best reserved for people who know each other and possibly share a breakfast or dinner table. . PM me if you want details.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-26T17:19:23+0300

I was quite bummed... If they've changed it...or there's a way to loan btwn different accounts I'm all in!!!

My understanding is same as Thyme's. There is a 14 day limit then it goes away. When you are looking in your library, on the right is a pull down options menu. On that it will say "lend" if that books publisher allows it.

Here's a tip on loaning books via your Kindle.

If you have borrowed a book and need more time to read it simply TURN OFF THE WIRELESS. If Amazon can't contact your Kindle it cannot "erase" it from your reading list.

# Thread: Exercising resulted in adrenalin/panic chaos
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-06-26T16:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-33651>

I stopped exercising for about a year, while doing the lepton rx. I just arrived at my lake house where I am going to stay for a month. I wanted to return to exercise and enjoy myself. For two days I swam for hours in the lake and played hide and seek with some kids....sprinting many times. I also went for walks in the woods, probably totaling 2 miles. I love doing these things. But, I am now having complete and awfu panic attack. Started last night and are really bad this morning. I hardly ever get them in the morning. My hands are tingly which makes me think I have neuropathy or something very bad. I feel so broken and just want to be able to be active and play with my children. How do I stop this adrenalin....or my hyperresponsiveness to it?

### AKMan — 2012-06-26T18:56:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-33699>

I tried to PM you, but your inbox is full.

### Destiny — 2012-08-05T15:17:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-43035>

> **ColdBren;33789 wrote:** I stopped exercising for about a year, while doing the lepton rx. I just arrived at my lake house where I am going to stay for a month. I wanted to return to exercise and enjoy myself. For two days I swam for hours in the lake and played hide and seek with some kids....sprinting many times. I also went for walks in the woods, probably totaling 2 miles. I love doing these things. But, I am now having complete and awfu panic attack. Started last night and are really bad this morning. I hardly ever get them in the morning. My hands are tingly which makes me think I have neuropathy or something very bad. I feel so broken and just want to be able to be active and play with my children. How do I stop this adrenalin....or my hyperresponsiveness to it?

ColdBren, I think you did too much too fast. Take it much slower. Try to do one of the activities per day. Also when were you exercising? That could play a huge role as well.

I am like you I used to enjoy being active all the time. Now, I really have to pace myself. It will get better but it will take time!

### Glamazon — 2012-08-06T05:47:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-43160>

> **Jacob0;43303 wrote:** Hello guys today the topic is very nice and we discuss about the exercise and the dieting/ You know guys that if you are fat /over weight then you do exercise but its nesseary that you continue the exercise daily routine and dieting is also continue along with exercise/

You dont exercise if you HS CRP is high. Exercise is not the best thing for fat and overweight. Dieting is even worse. Make sure you are reading the blog.

### ColdBren — 2012-08-06T06:12:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-43162>

> **Glamazon;43445 wrote:** You dont exercise if you HS CRP is high. Exercise is not the best thing for fat and overweight. Dieting is even worse. Make sure you are reading the blog.

I think Jacob0 is a spambot......it didn't make any sense.

Destiny- Thanks. I slowed down a bit but continued to exercise while I was out there on vacation. I think the initial shock to my system caused the reaction. However all the exercise was fun, I waterskiied, played hide and seek in the woods, lots of sprinting, swung on tree swings, swam every day....it was great, and my body adapted...there was nothing too cardio though because we all know why that is not good

### Destiny — 2012-08-06T13:16:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-43174>

> **ColdBren;43447 wrote:** I think Jacob0 is a spambot......it didn't make any sense.
>
>
>
> Destiny- Thanks. I slowed down a bit but continued to exercise while I was out there on vacation. I think the initial shock to my system caused the reaction. However all the exercise was fun, I waterskiied, played hide and seek in the woods, lots of sprinting, swung on tree swings, swam every day....it was great, and my body adapted...there was nothing too cardio though because we all know why that is not good

It is good to listen to your body. I am glad you had fun!!

### TerrierMom — 2012-08-14T16:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-44889>

Glad you made it through ColdBren... I have some similar issues to you, and though I rarely get them now (not sure why) panic attacks is one of them. Not fun and very scary no matter how much you tell yourself you're fine

# Thread: Partial Hysterectomy Question about LRX/HCG
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/partial-hysterectomy-question-about-lrx-hcg.2053/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MartiD — 2012-06-26T17:45:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/partial-hysterectomy-question-about-lrx-hcg.2053/post-33680>

I had a partial hysterectomy 4 years ago (still have ovaries). I'm 43 years old with no signs of menopause. My OB/GYN has never mentioned any need for replacement therapy. Just out of curiousity I am wondering how others do compared to those who are pre-menopausal with all parts. Does having a partial or full H. have any effects one way or the other on the Reset? I even wondered how it effects those who do HCG rounds? I read alot of comments of those who are having to supplement hormones and wonder why they are having to do that (hysterectomy or levels just off.) Hormones seem to be such an important part of being optimal.

ASFM...I did two successful rounds of HHCG last year, did reset in October and lost an additional 13 lbs, have done CT since February and lost another 2-3 lbs so it seems it has not had a negative effect on me. Would love to hear from others.

# Thread: Can you help me with DH's labs?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-with-dhs-labs.2056/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-06-26T18:33:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-with-dhs-labs.2056/post-33695>

So I am terrible at hacking labs. I can handle my own, but someone else's... thats another story! Anyway, my husbands labs are getting worse and worse, and I don't know which way to point him. Thanks in advance.

Diet: as paleo as possible (meaning no dairy, no grains, no sugar, unless unknowingly eaten in restaraunt food.) He is CTing, walking a couple times a week. He is working on stress reduction, but its slow going.

Supps: D3, K2, Mag, CoQ10, B complex, NAC, DHEA keto, fish oil,

Here are his labs for comparison (sept 2011 in blue, june 2012 in red)

LDL: 123 (40) 34

Trigs: 142 (

### tweety — 2012-06-27T15:37:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-with-dhs-labs.2056/post-33917>

My husband:

5'7 1/2", weight 275lbs

very heavy dense body frame; very heavy large muscles, very stocky and compact

I don't know his BF%

Past medical history:

\-many sports played, several concussions and has residual neck and shoulder pain probably from these sports injuries

\-tendency to pop ligaments and muscles (inflexible, tight tendons)

\-one bicep is completely torn (has 80% recovery potential)

\-ruptured achilles tendon, repaired surgically 1996

\-prone to injury in general

\- high stress as child

\-overweight since childhood, with the exception of high school

\-stressful last five years (moving, career change, starting his own business, father died...)

he has a high pressure job, and loves it but it requires alot of him. he has started addressing his stress- turning off his phone at home, limiting his strenuous duties when possible, ... we don't watch TV, he reads before bed and goes to bed around 10 when he can;

he loves CT

We are strict with diet because he has celiac. Dairy has been gone about a year. When he tried it after a time he had alot of inflammation, so he is good about keeping it out. The only thing I can imagine would be inadvertent gluten on his restaurant food. Really his appetite is not there. I know a large part of the issue is his stress. His appetite returns when he is not under stress, and he sees this now too. We had a small family vacation over a weekend and he lost 8lbs in three days. As soon as we get home, he puts it back on... from the stress. Fortunately, he is aware of this and has been more diligent about stress reduction since.

Typical days food:

B: 3 eggs, side of pasture raised ham/bacon/GF hamburger and licorice tea

L: broth and a little bit of leftovers

D: small salad, fish/GF beef, and veggie, licorice tea

Anything else?

Get the tests done per my script in post above.

Husband have to get his mind set at of dropping 100# of weight. Likely 120# better, but get this scale, do not worry about weight. You want to see 15% bf or less. That will account for any discussions about heavy build allowed to have more weight, bs.

[http://www.amazon.com/Ozeri-Bathroom-Hydration-Recognition-Technology/dp/B004NXUAXW/ref=sr\_1\_14?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1340816853&sr=1-14&keywords=scale+with+body+fat+percentage](http://http://www.amazon.com/Ozeri-Bathroom-Hydration-Recognition-Technology/dp/B004NXUAXW/ref=sr_1_14?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1340816853&sr=1-14&keywords=scale+with+body+fat+percentage)

\---

No restaurant food.

In restaurant always order plain water and triple order of shrimp scampi with triple butter each or couple pounds of a king crab with 8 tbsp=1/2cup=1stick=800cal butter.

At least 50% of daily food intake within one hr off wakeup.

Remember 70% fat + 30% proteins + incidental carbs.

Look (almost) at sun first 30 min after wakeup.

After he have results from Spectracell, supplement deficiencies, but drop licorice tea, replace with green tea.

...

1 dropperfull/day

# Thread: Women - compression shirt
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-compression-shirt.2057/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-06-26T20:02:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-compression-shirt.2057/post-33711>

Ladies, any suggestions on a particular brand of compression shirt particularly for us really busty girls?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-26T20:05:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-compression-shirt.2057/post-33712>

I have a rash guard from O'Neill and a compression shirt from Under Armour. The compression shirt is a medium and fits tight. I'm a 32DD. I like both of these tops but the Under Armour is thinner so it serves it's compression purpose w/o keeping too much of the cold out. I think my rash guard is a little too thick.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-26T20:07:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-compression-shirt.2057/post-33713>

I've bought tanks at Target for under $20, I've got REI versions of under armor, I've got under armor, I've got spanks, and I've got maternity post c-section compression gear. they're all the same IMO. I say find the stuff you like, that's in your budget. I really like the target tank I bought a while back... though since I've gotten my CT Tub (100gal stock tank) I've just been wearing my swimsuit in it. even the girl has been asking to CT since we CT'd together for about 1hr on Sunday..

# Thread: Restarting CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/restarting-ct.2058/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-06-26T20:09:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/restarting-ct.2058/post-33714>

Alright, so I had been trucking along really well with CT prior to going on vacation. My husband and I were able to stay in 50-55 degree water for an hour+ and had been doing that for over a month. We went on vacation for two weeks and just haven't been able to get back into the groove of things. It's been about 5ish weeks (except for the occasional face dunk).

It's really warm out now in Michigan and the pool is up to almost 80, so we can't CT there. It was so convenient to do that because my hubby and I could CT at the same time and let the kids run around and play. My husband wants to rig something up for the outside we can both CT in, but he isn't mechanically inclined and it seems it would take a ton of ice to get water down to temp each day. Any ideas?

Also, do we need to start back at the beginning of the CT protocol, or do you think we're ok to hop back into the cold baths?

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-26T20:19:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/restarting-ct.2058/post-33716>

you should be able to hop right back into the cold water I did...

I just bought a stock tank; I plan on using it every day, unless I go to the pool at lunch time for a swim (Today was perfect for it too the water was cool under 75 - not cold but it worked, that coupled w/the cool weather we've been having, and the stiff breeze my 30mins of laps I'm calling good!)

w/the stock tank - I'm going to drain it ever 3rd day or so and use it to water our garden... then clean it out, fill it back up. I've been changing my am routine in anticipation of going back to work on a client site - so eventually I' won't be working from home. I'm planning on moving my wake up time back to 5am (right now I'm waking up around 545) having my BAB, and hopping in the CT tub for 30-40mins before getting ready for work.. I'll throw in ICE chunks I make myself... and I'll go from there...

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T20:44:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/restarting-ct.2058/post-33724>

> **Trisha;33852 wrote:** Alright, so I had been trucking along really well with CT prior to going on vacation. My husband and I were able to stay in 50-55 degree water for an hour+ and had been doing that for over a month. We went on vacation for two weeks and just haven't been able to get back into the groove of things. It's been about 5ish weeks (except for the occasional face dunk).
>
>
>
> It's really warm out now in Michigan and the pool is up to almost 80, so we can't CT there. It was so convenient to do that because my hubby and I could CT at the same time and let the kids run around and play. My husband wants to rig something up for the outside we can both CT in, but he isn't mechanically inclined and it seems it would take a ton of ice to get water down to temp each day. Any ideas?
>
>
>
> Also, do we need to start back at the beginning of the CT protocol, or do you think we're ok to hop back into the cold baths?

Do you have a garden hose? Does your tap water stay pretty chilly? Maybe you could augment cold tap water with home-made ice blocks in a stock tank? I'm going to embark on an ice-block-making mission this weekend, because I'm sick of shelling out for bagged ice for my ice packs.

I've been doing paleo for 8 weeks (started a weight loss program previously by following the Slow Carb Diet for a total of 13 weeks changed eating/exercising), following leptin Rx and feeling better, losing weight. I am hoping to try to stop autoimmune/chronic issues:

-   Ulcerative colitis (previously; have had a J-pouch)
-   Crohn's disease
-   Sjogren's syndrome
-   Interstitial cystitis
-   Insulin resistance
    Fatty liver
    Polycystic ovarian syndrome
    Anemia and B12 and D vitamin deficiencies
    Widespread chronic body pain

I'm hoping to get stronger and healthier and lose my goal weight (40 pounds). I'm halfway to my goal weight, but discouraged to learn that if my weight loss continues at the pace it is, it will take half a year to lose my total goal weight! I know; I didn't gain it overnight! I'm looking forward to the day when I can stop being cold all the time!

# Thread: Skin question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-06-26T20:57:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-33731>

I know this is a small issue when comparing it to getting my body to an optimal working condition, but I'm still curious about it. I read recently that enlarged facial pores are a symptom of consuming lots of sugar (which could certainly have been the case for me pre-paleo/pre-leptin Rx)--does anybody know if this is a true fact? Will my facial pores smooth out as I continue avoiding sugars, starches, grains, and dairy?

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T21:15:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-33733>

> **cookworm;33869 wrote:** I know this is a small issue when comparing it to getting my body to an optimal working condition, but I'm still curious about it. I read recently that enlarged facial pores are a symptom of consuming lots of sugar (which could certainly have been the case for me pre-paleo/pre-leptin Rx)--does anybody know if this is a true fact? Will my facial pores smooth out as I continue avoiding sugars, starches, grains, and dairy?

I can't answer the pore-size question specifically, but I can certainly attest to the fact that my skin is LOADS better since going very-low carb. I used to have super-sensitive skin. Everything made me break out...not in zits so much as in itchy little bumps. That's long gone now, and things (laundry detergents, soaps) that used to really make me itch now don't bother me at all. Also, I stopped using soap and started using just pure coconut oil as a moisturizer a few weeks ago, and my skin has improved even more. Not just facial skin - my elbows and heels are the smoothest they've ever been!

### vkiernan — 2012-06-27T05:04:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-33872>

I have tried co twice on my face and both times I broke out afterwards. I know it is supposed to be good for our skin but I am relunctant now. Any ideas? Do you think I just need to acclimate my skin to it? Using too much?

### Inger — 2012-06-27T10:16:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-33893>

I use Argan oil on my face, and coconut and olive oil on my body. This works very well! Argan oil do not clog the pores at all, I think jojoba is great too? Argan oil is quite expnesive, it needs to be a good brand, not roasted and very good quality. I know it on the smell if it is good, I once buyed another Arganoil brand which was terrible.. smelled strong and yucky to me, I did not used it on mys skin at all. So be sure the brand is high quality. Cold pressed.

About pores.. my pores was also always too large, especially on my nose. After doing facedunking and CT they have gotten smaller! So start doing facedunks every morning, they do wonders for the skin! I dunk about 4 to 10 times in a bowl in my kitchen every morning. I love it.

Sometimes when tanning outside in the sun I put a gel icepack on my face to cool it too.. I guess the more cooling your face gets, the better!

### purplemonster — 2012-06-28T04:34:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-34094>

Argan oil on the face? Sounds good. How may times do I have to do this in a day? Or just before I go to sleep?

# Thread: Healthy Seafood ideas for people who dislike seafood
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 41
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cody — 2012-06-26T20:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33732>

I need some ideas for healthy recipes/products for seafood.

Here's what I like:

-   shrimp cocktail
-   clam chowder
-   raw salmon
    any fish deep fat fried and breaded.
    Tuna salad
    salmon salad

What do you all recommend?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-26T21:23:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33735>

I'd suggest you begin to get used to discomfort. You'll see why by BG 5 I bet.

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T21:23:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33736>

Try tuna without the mayo? I like it with lemon juice and a little tiny bit of olive oil, plus some chopped roasted red peppers (or banana peppers, if you like hotter stuff), kalamata olives, capers, red onion...all scooped onto a bed of greens. Shrimp scampi is easy to adapt to a paleo diet, just ditch the pasta and serve it over spaghetti squash or even plain. Shrimp is also wonderful in coconut-milk-based curry, and it's easy to load a curry up with lots of veg and turmeric as well.

If you want to expand your fish horizons a little, cod is super good for you and it's very, very mild...it doesn't taste "fishy" at all. Haddock is similar, though it's more finely grained than cod and a little harder to cook filets of. Both cod and haddock are fantastic in curry, or roasted and served with a tapenade over them.

Have you tried mackerel or sardines at all? If you like salmon, you'd probably like mackerel and sardines. Sardines packed in water seem to be milder-tasting to me than ones packed in oil. Canned mackerel tastes kind of like a cross between tuna and salmon to me. It wasn't nearly as strong as I thought it would be.

Hopefully that helps some?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-26T21:58:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33746>

Try making paleo mayo with coconut oil for your tuna salads. I can't eat eggs so not a rush for me to make it but I've read others have liked it.

Also Dover Sole is an inexpensive, very thin/flat, white fish that has almost no-existent flavor. I pan fry quickly in a little ghee then just put a little dill on the fish and pour some melted ghee with lemon rind over the fish. YUM! From start to end you should be done in 10-15 min.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-26T23:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33765>

Hi Cody, how about shrimp grilled in ghee or butter?

### janagram — 2012-06-27T08:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33889>

### johnnyb — 2012-06-27T08:21:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33891>

You might try cooking seafood differently. One of the reasons "I didn't like seafood" is having had most of it generally overcooked while growing up. My brother taught me that only a couple years ago. I keep trying more and more seafood. I recently cooked a salmon fillet in butter. It was very tasty until the thickest part was too under done. I had already cleared the pan so I hit it with 30 seconds in the microwave. That was too long. It was better undercooked. It is amazing how different seafood can be depending on how it's preparred. Try the widest variety you can in different ways. You will find more you foods you like. Keep going with what you do like too.

### allen001 — 2012-07-04T06:54:02+0300

### Cody — 2012-07-23T14:46:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40372>

I found a winner, winner, fishy dinner recipe to solve my problem. I love eggs, first of all. Really, my favorite food ever.

**1\. Fisherman’s Eggs**

_Easy and very tasty. Try them for breakfast, or pair with a leafy salad for a quick supper. Serves 4. Adapted from_[The Silver Spoon](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0714845310?ie=UTF8&tag=c037-20&linkCode=xm2&camp=1789&creativeASIN=0714845310).

Heat the oven to 500Â°F and preheat an ovenproof serving dish for five minutes. Place a can of sardines, a small sliced shallot, a few sprigs of chopped parsley, and two cloves of finely chopped garlic in the warm dish. Add some black pepper and put the dish back in the oven for six minutes. Remove the dish from the oven, break four eggs into a bowl, and pour them gently on top of the sardine mixture. Season with salt and pepper and put the dish back in the oven for seven minutes, until the egg whites are cooked but still jiggly. Remove the dish and let it sit for five minutes (the eggs keep on cooking even out of the oven). Serve with toast and hot sauce.

[](http://http://www.chow.com/blog-media/2010/05/sardine_fishermans_eggs_600.jpg)

From [http://www.chow.com/food-news/47126/7-things-to-do-with-canned-sardines/](http://http://www.chow.com/food-news/47126/7-things-to-do-with-canned-sardines/)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-23T14:58:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40374>

nice backround of wheat bread.......this is the ying and yang of food.......

>
> Heat the oven to 500Â°F and preheat an ovenproof serving dish for five minutes. Place a can of sardines, a small sliced shallot, a few sprigs of chopped parsley, and two cloves of finely chopped garlic in the warm dish. Add some black pepper and put the dish back in the oven for six minutes. Remove the dish from the oven, break four eggs into a bowl, and pour them gently on top of the sardine mixture. Season with salt and pepper and put the dish back in the oven for seven minutes, until the egg whites are cooked but still jiggly. Remove the dish and let it sit for five minutes (the eggs keep on cooking even out of the oven). Serve with toast and hot sauce.
>
> [](http://http://www.chow.com/blog-media/2010/05/sardine_fishermans_eggs_600.jpg)
