Hot damn that looks good! I'm a big fan of runny-yolked eggs on top of just about anything - kale, burgers, and now fish...

### Cody — 2012-07-23T15:33:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40383>

> **Jack Kruse;40562 wrote:** nice backround of wheat bread.......this is the ying and yang of food.......

The article I linked to is full of wheat!

I eat mine without toast and with pastured eggs.

By the way, ignore the serves four part! I throw in a fifth egg and it serves one. Me!

Also, works great with canned oysters! Even tastier.

### MartiD — 2012-07-23T15:56:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40386>

My husband laughs at my eating habit now. I was always a just shrimp or crab legs seafood eater, hated all other fish. I've been craving salmon since I started LRX in October and now eat tilapia, cod, halibut, etc. This weekend we went to Sams and got a big bag of sea scallops and big section of wild caught salmon. I've never had scallops because my Mom was deadly allergic to them. I wrapped in bacon last night and covered them in lemon butter and they were great. No fishy taste at all! And no reaction!

I'll keep venturing out more and more with time. Not sure I can do the raw thing just yet but who knows!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-23T17:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40394>

and getting to the point of the thread......if you dislike seafood learn to get used to your dislike long term........it is the reason you are sick.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-23T17:55:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40396>

This weekend we went to the local international mkt - I bought 2x1lb bags of frozen mixed seafood for $3.99/bag - that same bag at Tjoes is $11.99!

we also got fresh blue crabs for $1.99/lb - score... unfortunately at that price they'd been picked over by 11am and there were only 6 live ones' left -

### MJ* — 2012-07-23T19:08:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40408>

I just purchased Marder Brands skewered shrimp, and we put them on the BBQ brushed with butter and garlic, YUM!

Another fish I like is Monkfish sometimes referred to as the poor man's lobster. It's a meaty white fish. I like to broil it, you can also grill it. It's a juicy fish so you can put a lemon zest and salt rub on it an hour before you cook it to bring down some of the excess juice. Serve it with drawn butter, it's delicious!

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-24T16:39:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40675>

It seems to me that salmon is usually the most difficult fish for people to like - at least in my experience.

If you like raw salmon - how can you go wrong just eating that?

I've been buying up wild caught alaskan salmon like crazy this summer - it is pricey.

But I just grill it in butter and add some blackened spices on the top.

### Cody — 2012-07-26T18:30:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-41214>

Is it safe to eat raw salmon from the grocer's freezer section?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T18:52:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-41230>

If I eat salmon raw I make sure it is sushi grade, although I have to say I'm not sure what makes it "sushi grade", and wild. Lived in Seattle too many years to even think of eating farm raised. Just cannot do it with salmon. I used to get raw salmon from this Japanese store that never smelled of fish. So clean. Now I haven't eating raw except at sushi restaurants in a year because I'm not near that store. Making a trip next week to that area so going to check out their sea veggies too!

# Thread: The Difference in Optimal and not
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-difference-in-optimal-and-not.2062/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-26T22:10:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-difference-in-optimal-and-not.2062/post-33753>

I would prefer to eat optimally ie grass-fed or naturally raised everything, but I just don't have the monetary assets to do so. Right now I buy grass-fed butter but that's about it. I try to get fish when I can but its not wild caught and I try to stay away from the obviously bad processed foods and grains. I try my best to use the butter and my coconut oil and fruits. Small amounts of fruit...or at least I don't think its too much. Lately for lunch I've been eating a serving of Greek Gods plain yogurt with a handful of blueberries, raspberries, and blackberries.(Too much fruit) A handful is I think around a half cup but I could weigh it I suppose.

I also eat sausage and eggs for breakfast with a little bit of cheese.

What I'm really curious about is people who used to eat like me ie not optimal...but still Paleo and how you felt and what your labs were like in comparison to what they are now that you eat more optimal. I'd like to know what changes seemed to make the most difference. I'm trying to eat three meals LR style...and my weight is sitting still...not sure what to do or change.

### janagram — 2012-06-28T03:47:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-difference-in-optimal-and-not.2062/post-34089>

well I have actually started to sleep better and the scale has started to move downward a bit....and I am on the LR. I don't eat that much fruit and the yogurt also is a carb. try some meat or fish over a salad.

# Thread: Gut parasites...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MartiD — 2012-06-27T00:55:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33791>

Ok, so I know Dr. Oz is probably not well respected on here but he just had a story about intestinal parasites causing Chronic Fatigue Syndrome. According to the Gastroenterolgist he had on, 46% of all CFS clients have some type of a worm/parasite in their gut. Also said these can live for many years and people may not know until tested. Apparently the parasites attach to the lining and suck blood and nutrients from the body. This makes sense but wonder if anyone has ever heard of this and if it has any validity. They said best cure is 1/4 cup of papaya seeds and a couple tablespoons of honey each for day for a week. If you start having more energy then you probably had worms.

Thoughts? Sounds disgusting but maybe makes sense?

### vkiernan — 2012-06-27T01:47:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33800>

> **MartiD;33929 wrote:** Ok, so I know Dr. Oz is probably not well respected on here but he just had a story about intestinal parasites causing Chronic Fatigue Syndrome. According to the Gastroenterolgist he had on, 46% of all CFS clients have some type of a worm/parasite in their gut. Also said these can live for many years and people may not know until tested. Apparently the parasites attach to the lining and suck blood and nutrients from the body. This makes sense but wonder if anyone has ever heard of this and if it has any validity. They said best cure is 1/4 cup of papaya seeds and a couple tablespoons of honey each for day for a week. If you start having more energy then you probably had worms.
>
>
>
> Thoughts? Sounds disgusting but maybe makes sense?

Lot's of people including kids gets worm, etc. You can also get them from your animals as far as I know. I am going to do a test in few days to tell me if I have them or not. I'm surprised you have never heard of it actually. A guy I work with actually lost quite a bit of weight and then found out he had parasites. Got rid of them and felt much better, especially energy wise.

### MartiD — 2012-06-27T02:15:07+0300

So I' m here after taking over a month to read (okay, I skimmed a bit) the monster thread at MDA. I feel like I know some of you already.

In my 4th week of LR...I have eaten primally since spring 2009, and was strictly carnivore for 2 years. I added in some veggies last summer but do not seem to have success with hgher than 50 grams/ day carbs. Occasional alcohol and dark chocolate. In the same 3 lb range since December, and nothing seems to get me back under 120. I am 52, female, 5'2" and 124 as of today.

I am ordering a cortisol test soon, and looking into hormone testing. I never see my PCP so don't quite know how to broach this.

Laura

# Thread: Woke up and smelled...coffee! Hmmm :/
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### freesia — 2012-06-27T05:22:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/post-33876>

I love coffee. Maybe a little too much because my just one per day with my BAB often turns into two. (I think there may be a slight addictive thing going on with me and coffee. We go back a long way.)

This morning...I woke up and smelled it on my skin. As in I think I may be detoxing some component of it via my skin! Is this even possible? It's kind of disturbing. Perhaps I've had some problem metabolizing it all along.

Has anyone else noticed this?

I'm guessing I really need to give it up....it's not going to be easy (not one bit!). My morning coffee makes me feel human!

### fitness@home — 2012-06-27T13:15:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/post-33902>

Freesia, don't feel bad...coffee is my one addiction. Love the smell, taste, and feel of it in my mouth.

Can't comment on detoxing thru your skin... Just wanted to let you know that you are not alone in your love for coffee. Even DH knows to let me have my two cups of heaven in the morning, or else...

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-27T15:20:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/post-33914>

what kind of coffee are you drinking? if you don't have good beans, you're getting toxins and such from the coffee... read The info on upgraded coffee and why its different than other coffee... [http://www.bulletproofexec.com/coffee/](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/coffee/)

### freesia — 2012-06-28T00:06:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/post-34054>

Thanks coffee peeps for your input, and empathy.

Such a delicious addiction is coffee!

I get single source organic beans from a friend who has a coffee business. They're good. Pretty confident they're as good as BP.

I think whatever is coming through my skin must be a component of coffee which makes it _smell_ like coffee. I'm not sure if the change in my tolerance to it (or the way I'm metabolizing it?) is due to CT or not.

Hoping to get genetic results soon because I just read that some coffee compounds, eg polyphenols, can be difficult to clear through the detoxification pathways with certain polymorphisms.

While I'm waiting for genetic results (which I was getting anyway....not just for the coffee mystery) I'll work on cutting back to one cup.

Thx again

# Thread: Evolution
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution.2070/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-06-27T06:31:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution.2070/post-33882>

**In response to msclman's question:

"just finished your most recent blog. im no anthropologist but i do have a question, given the fast acting gene modification system we seem to have wont we evolve eventually to the standard american diet (and arent we selected now after 12k years of wheat to that type of diet)?"

**Jack posted this answer:**

"Here is our modern circle of life......we came from transitional ape with a small brain.....we walked up, and were not too bright. The cold came and the ocean crashed into our neighborhood and the forest and grassy plains died off.......the tectonic plates moved and the land shifted up and the coastal waterways floaded cutting us off from all out natural sources of food......IE carbs from fruit in the trees.......what was around? Lots of seaweed and shellfish......we ate it. Our legs got longer, our guts shortened, we got more viral infections from the clams......and we got even smarter. We stopped eating fruit, did not need it at all, and we ate more of what got us smarter....coastal vegetation and shell fish. Brains got bigger. We used tools and caught fish.......got smarter and we made tools to kill animals that were ten times our size......we got so smart we could make food for large packs of us.......we got bored ate more fish with a special virus that made us impulsive and we left the cradle that birthed us......we ran into some cold weather up North and we moved away from the sea and lost all our sources of seafood and meat. We decided to eat plants to survive because we were smart........or that is what we thought 13000 yrs ago........then our brains (cro magnon) shrank 130 grams and we became us......modern man......and we kept eating grains.....and then fruit.......and then we started feeding our animals the same \*\*\*\*......and we ate them and all the crap we fed them too........then we began making our own seeds and food, and sugar........and our brains shrunk. Our cognition got worse.......we listened to other humans who said whole grains and dairy from cows fed grains weee awesome for us. The smart humans who cared for us said eat what you want because a calorie is a calorie.....diet does not matter......we got fat, and dumb and wonf up with T2D and AD......the AD came earlier and earlier......now we cant think or do anything much. We stay in homes called elder care places where we eat fruit and grasses........as we watch apes and chimps on the discovery channel do the same thing........and we are as smart as them......once again because the TV said they have exactly the same genes we do........the only difference is they eat fruit all day and we dont? The orderly brings your grandmother a bowl of fruit covered in ice cream with a side of cookies. Medicare paid for it because its part of a balanced diet according to the FDA/USDA and has the stamp of approval of Washington DC who sell the grains to the entire world..........Everyone wants to be like us? That is made easy when you dumb it down for the masses to get because their brains no longer work....... That is how evolution leads to de evolution when you mess with your epigenetics. Go re read levee one right now.......epigenetics.......is what determines your cellular terroir......you eat like a chimp.......you think like one. Got it Mike? share it."

_Jack, and msclman I hope you don't mind me posting this here. It's too important to be buried in a closed thread!_**

### nonchalant — 2012-06-27T06:56:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution.2070/post-33884>

Glad you posted this, Indigogirl! I was awed by his response also. I would have just replied "Because the SAD is unable to support good brains." Jack is a natural teacher.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-06-27T14:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution.2070/post-33910>

I would really be interested in getting more information about the cognitive decline Jack describes above.

Can someone point me in the right direction?

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-27T15:12:41+0300

### nonchalant — 2012-06-27T15:46:31+0300

The power of marketing and "socially acceptable" is quite overwhelming isn't it.

I am struggling right now with a good friend. We actually bonded and became friends because we were both Paleo. She started as a client, and moved to #1 playdates and friends as we had boys the same age. They have fallen off and are now eating more sad. She got pregnant after not being able to for a while (yes, that eating got you preggers, lets stop it now!) So she is pregnant and eating bagels and I just can't stand it.

It really bothers me and I feel I've lost a partner in crime. I've found I just don't want to hang out as much, and I don't want my son seeing them going back to processed foods after we had made great strides in eating the same way and could swap kids with no crazy eating rules.

I'm mad. I want my friend back that has similar interest. Wah.

# Thread: Articles and TV shows that reference Factor X - discuss here
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TerrierMom — 2012-06-27T08:06:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33887>

Since my last thread is a few TV shows that touch Factor X implications I thought maybe a new thread where we could post about stuff like that which we come across.

Just caught the last few minutes of 'Mysteries at the Museum' on the Travel channel where the Spanish Flu epidemic of 1918 was discussed. It was mentioned that scientists figured out why the seemingly healthiest people were hit the hardest, and said it was because the virus provoked a strong immune response. Therefore the stronger and more healthy the person, the harder their system would fight the virus, and ultimately kill the host. While more weak and vulnerable populations survived better. The show opines that the people left gained immunity.

I guess it was gained via a normally leaky gut that incorporated the viral DNA? I just thought it was interesting that the stronger people got taken out, has to be more to this story in light of what Jack is talking about but I can't quite grasp it. Anyone want to ruminate on this?

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-27T16:23:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33926>

> **Krusing\_tptimal\_in\_CA;34026 wrote:** Since my last thread is a few TV shows that touch Factor X implications I thought maybe a new thread where we could post about stuff like that which we come across.
>
>
>
> Just caught the last few minutes of 'Mysteries at the Museum' on the Travel channel where the Spanish Flu epidemic of 1918 was discussed. It was mentioned that scientists figured out why the seemingly healthiest people were hit the hardest, and said it was because the virus provoked a strong immune response. Therefore the stronger and more healthy the person, the harder their system would fight the virus, and ultimately kill the host. While more weak and vulnerable populations survived better. The show opines that the people left gained immunity.
>
>
>
> I guess it was gained via a normally leaky gut that incorporated the viral DNA? I just thought it was interesting that the stronger people got taken out, has to be more to this story in light of what Jack is talking about but I can't quite grasp it. Anyone want to ruminate on this?

Just b/c they appeared to be stronger - doesn't mean they were - could be their gut was far more leaky than those who survived. I think you need a happy medium btwn the gut/brain to optimally assimilate the viral dna/rna - to make it useful, and pass on to survivors. If its too leaky you're immune compromised already - even if you show no symptoms. there's more to this than meets the eye.... and I think Jack's going to give us the "Silver bullet" that kills all other theories as we move further into the BG Series.

Go back and re-read level1 of the quilt. then re-read BG1 and BG2, then re-read CT6, then go back and Re-read BG 1 & 2. then pick up cordain's paleo answer and read the autoimmune section -

ETA: if you were to look at me and my health conditions and look at my DH - you'd think he was healthier than me. But when you look at the labs - I'm actually healthier than him... appearances can be deceiving... you've got to look at the whole picture to start to put the puzzle pieces together...

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-27T17:39:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33941>

Thanks Shinjin, I knew there was more there than met the eye but wasnt sure where to go first. Doesn't seem right mother nature would kill the strongest - if they were optimal for their environment then wouldn't they survive? Off to read some more!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-27T19:48:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33991>

Nothin to contribue - but GREAT THREAD!

### maggie — 2012-06-27T20:00:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33996>

I think most of the victims were healthy young adults with strong immune systems in whom the flu created a cytokine storm that overwhelmed them. Children and older adults, who overall had weaker immune systems than the young adults, had a lot fewer victims.

### maggie — 2012-06-27T20:12:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33999>

These are two sci fi novels by Greg Bear that are entirely based on speeded up evolution driven by human endogenous retroviruses. Reviews say the science is very solid. I haven't read Darwin's Children yet, but loved Darwin's Radio when I first read it several years ago--of course it was much more meaningful to me when I went back and reread it recently after learning about Factor X. Here's a blurb from the proposed TV series that never got off the ground (I think the producers wanted to demonize the new children and this caused a breach between them and the author since it didn't reflect the spirit of the books at all.)

Darwin's Children

Set in the future, the story follows the advent of an ancient virus that accelerates evolution, creating "virus babies" - superhuman children considered the next stage of humanity but are persecuted by the human race who fear for their future. The generations clash creating global unrest.

### janagram — 2012-06-27T21:34:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-34015>

Thanks for the interesting ideas here!! Must check out Darwin's children.

I also believe the theory that the strongest died of flu by fighting it so hard they killed off the host (themselves). It fits with some theoretical work of D'Adamo. (blood type doc...maybe not appreciated here, but for whatitsworth)

### Marccus — 2012-10-30T07:24:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-60877>

thats a great idea given here by you i would surly be available for any further help. lets check up now for darwins children

# Thread: Hello from the LCHF center of gravity - SWEDEN!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CoolSwede — 2012-06-27T14:07:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33905>

Hello everyone!

I am a 33 year old Swedish guy that always is on the quest to improve my health. Three years ago, I friend of mine told me about the new LCHF diet and gave me a link to the Diet Doctor ([http://www.dietdoctor.com](http://http://www.dietdoctor.com)). The rest is history as we say!

My current goal is to lose more fat to reach 9%-10% BF. LCHF took me half-way but I have still those annoying love handles that just won't go away. I am currently at ~17% BF.

See ya'!

### janagram — 2012-06-27T17:48:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33944>

### CoolSwede — 2012-06-27T18:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33952>

> **janagram;34083 wrote:** welcome! Come on in, the water's really cold! (you'll love it!)

I know, I have regularly done cold showers since March/April. I have not yet tried the cold tub since I do not own one. However, the Baltic is not boiling hot.

### .lina — 2012-06-27T18:12:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33954>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-27T18:16:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33957>

Welcome.......we love high latitude folks here because cold matches with LCHF diets......its the Diet Doctors missing link.

### CoolSwede — 2012-06-28T14:04:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-34123>

Tjena Lina and thank you Jack for your warm welcome! Yes, we up north have access to cold weather naturally but as every neolithic being, I have been wearing warm clothes to avoid catching a cold. (Horrible expression by the way, should we rename it to "catch a warmth" instead?!)

Yes, I believe that cold is the missing link and maybe that will be the key to evolve into superhumans. I have read Barry's thread about fasted training and I am mighty impressed to say the least.

It is also funny to hear other people's comments changing from "Are you really going to eat that much butter" to "Don't you feel cold wearing only that t-shirt outside?". Well well, maybe those are the voices of a paradigm shift.

Lina: I am really impressed by your photos. They make me really hungry!

See you 'round!

### caroline — 2012-06-30T08:16:17+0300

# Thread: The Maasai and salt
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-maasai-and-salt.2074/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-06-27T15:54:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-maasai-and-salt.2074/post-33922>

There's an interesting post by Chris Masterjohn on the Maasai.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/06/04/the-maasai-an-interlude-what-does-all-this-sociocultural-and-historical-stuff-have-to-do-with-heart-disease/](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/06/04/the-maasai-an-interlude-what-does-all-this-sociocultural-and-historical-stuff-have-to-do-with-heart-disease/)

Among other things it touches on the matter of salt, which has come up for discussion here recently.

Bruce Taylor insisted:

> The Masai do not use salt. Their language contains no word for salt. Whereas their vegetarian neighbors and their cattle crave salt, these carnivores seem to obtain sufficient salt from the flesh and milk which they eat.
>
>
> This statement that they had no word for salt actually seems to be part of an attempt to claim the Maasai as "carnivores" - _since everyone knew that a more plant-based diet needs more salt_. (In truth it seems the Maasai did sometimes eat non-animal foods, although it tends to be more the women that did.)
>
>
>
> In fact:
>
>
>
> According to Moritz Merker, who lived among the Maasai for eight years around the turn of the twentieth century (described in part 2 of this series), the Maasai word for steppe salt is e munjÃ¡n, which they added to their sweet potatoes and beans, and the Maasai word for natron salt is e magÃ¡t, which they added to their chewing tobacco.

I find that quite interesting.

What's that phrase Jack has used? "High carb paleo and metcons" was it?

Well, official paleo may be against salt, but it seems to me that if people are going to eat sweet potatoes, then they'd better put some salt on them like the Maasai knew to do.

### janagram — 2012-06-27T17:52:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-maasai-and-salt.2074/post-33947>

plus the masai used blood as a food......very salty.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-27T18:27:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-maasai-and-salt.2074/post-33964>

I think Masterjohn gets a pass for this missteps because the Masai do use salt on their carbs.....anyone on a LC diet needs salt.....but that salt needs to be good salt with iodine......

# Thread: Cooling Head = Great Night's Sleep
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2012-06-27T16:40:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-33930>

Had to comment here that I had the BEST night's sleep in a very long time last night. Living in Houston means very hot and humid temperatures this time of year.

Last night I placed an ice sheet inside my pillowcase and covered the pillow top with a hand towel. Went to bed as usual. For me, falling asleep is not a problem - _staying asleep_ after midnight is. Usually wake up 4-5 times.

Lo and behold, I only woke up once. The sleep was so deep and refreshing. The alarm woke me 4AM and it took longer than usual to feel alert. Almost like waking from a drug induced sleep.

I will be testing this method again tonight...

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-27T17:45:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-33943>

Hmmm keep us posted! Sounds like you're onto something!

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-27T17:52:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-33946>

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-28T06:13:18+0300

### Mackay — 2012-06-29T13:48:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-34337>

Any more reports on cold pillow sleeping?

### fitness@home — 2012-06-29T17:39:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-34365>

My little experiment...

I am an early to bed, early to rise kind of person; i.e. M-F turn in 8:30PM and get up at 4AM. It is not fully dark when I go to bed these days (use face mask to cover eyes). Don't watch TV in the evening but I do surf and read on my iPad. We don't turn on any extra lighting in the family room.

Night #2: Forgot to wear blue blockers. Surfed for about an hour between 7-8PM. Husband had TV on in room. Slept ok, but not as well as first night. Woke up briefly maybe 2-3 times and was able to fall back asleep quickly.

Night #3: Blue blockers on in evening during my surf time (with husband watching TV in same room). GOOD night's sleep. One wake up before midnight - back asleep quickly. Felt well-rested at 4AM when I got out of bed.

In Conclusion - **Dark and cold** are conducive to restful sleep. But then, we all know that anyway...it is applying it to our daily life that counts.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-29T18:43:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-34377>

When I was overweight I used to sleep with ice bags on my carotid arteries to cool the blood going to my brain.......it raised several hormones to help me sleep to loose the weight fast. I did so many outside the box things it was hilarious......but my family stopped laughing in 11 months when they saw me transform before their eyes. Sometimes unconventional gives exceptional results if you let it.

What one needs is some kind of a icy neck-wrap contraption. Hmmmm.

[http://www.amazon.com/Ace-Reusable-Cold-Compress-Large/dp/B00717YEO8/ref=sr\_1\_2?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1341073081&sr=1-2&keywords=ace+reusable+cold+compress+large](http://http://www.amazon.com/Ace-Reusable-Cold-Compress-Large/dp/B00717YEO8/ref=sr_1_2?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1341073081&sr=1-2&keywords=ace+reusable+cold+compress+large)

It's been hot here, and we don't have a/c, which can make it hard to fall asleep. They grab an ice pack, and fall asleep literally in seconds. It's fascinating! I've been doing spot CT with these as well.They stay cold a long time.

# Thread: What really kicked grain into the human diet...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-06-27T18:14:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-33956>

Talking with my son (degree in anthropology) about grain in the diet, he explained the surprising thing that really brought grain into prominent use. War! The first armies. When large armies were needed to mobilize, how to get soldiers? They were paid with a bag of grain, which could be given to the family to provide for them while the men were gone as well as feed the men while on the move. Before that grain was a lesser diet factor. Suddenly grain is money. And war....the shocking truth!!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-27T18:17:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-33958>

Its called the Younger Dyras.......it will be in the few blog posts and has been in several of mine.

### janagram — 2012-06-27T21:28:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34014>

Oh, I have heard that too....not so sure, but of course the colonists in new england, many being of scotch-irish descent, made rye whiskey from rye, but when the tax on whiskey (Whiskey Rebellion) drove them down into Appalachia rye didn't grow well, so they made corn whiskey. (moonshine, etc.) another factor, while corn will only last so long, whiskey is longlived, and can be shipped. Now you see the wisdom in their ways! Grains are for making whiskey! (I'm an appreciator, but imbibing is now limited to a titch on a holiday.

### Michael — 2012-06-27T22:23:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34022>

> **August;34150 wrote:**
>
> This is what they saw as defining the diet for a civilized man. How wrong they were! Strange the dietitians are still making the same mistake nearly four millennia later.

This was my joke, of course.

As a matter of fact, I guess cereal grains _are what made settled society -- and hence "all this" -- possible. And the _Epic_ was, in that sense, correct. And, of course, countless numbers of people would die, if they vanished tomorrow. This is why Loren Cordain called his famous paper on them "Cereal Grains: Humanity’s Double-Edged Sword":

[http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf](http://http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf)

Brilliant paper. It's a clumsy metaphor, though -- a double-edged sword, such as a knight's sword, is no more a danger to its wielder than a single-edged sword, such as a saber or falchion.

But one gets what the "cuts both ways" metaphor is getting at. They're bad for health, yet we rely on them (although, I guess, how long that reliance can continue and intensive arable farming can go on without destroying soil-structure is the big question.)

But, you don't actually need cereal grains to have civilization, including a high-level literary culture. Iceland proves that. The sagas are a major literary achievement even on a European-wide scale - and a unique one. In some respects they anticipate the novel.

Yet medieval Iceland got by without grain. People lived principally on meat, seafood, and dairy products such as skyr_. It was one of Vilhjalmur Stefansson's first achievements as an anthropologists to do a survey on Icelandic teeth and to demonstrate that in the early period they were mostly free from cavities at the time when bread was rarely available, because the climate was too cold to grow grain.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-27T22:43:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34032>

Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies by Jared M. Diamond states â€œThe adoption of agriculture, supposedly our most decisive step toward a better life, was in many ways a catastrophe from which we have never recovered.â€￾

Also, he explains how grain societies could support a ruling class.

"In contrast, once food can be stockpiled, a political elite can gain control of food produced by others, assert the right of taxation, escape the need to feed itself, and engage full-time in political activities."

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-27T23:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34036>

> **Nonchalant;34172 wrote:** Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies by Jared M. Diamond states “The adoption of agriculture, supposedly our most decisive step toward a better life, was in many ways a catastrophe from which we have never recovered.”
>
>
>
> Also, he explains how grain societies could support a ruling class.
>
> "In contrast, once food can be stockpiled, a political elite can gain control of food produced by others, assert the right of taxation, escape the need to feed itself, and engage full-time in political activities."

THis is a great book...from many perspectives... I was thinking about this last week

### Michael — 2012-06-27T23:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34048>

Oh, dear the board software has malfunctioned and lost an earlier post of mine when I attempted to add a comment. All that about beer, bread and Gilgamesh down the drain ....

Never mind. I had finished:

> This is what they saw as defining the diet for a civilized man. How wrong they were! Strange the dietitians are still making the same mistake nearly four millennia later.

This was my joke, of course.

As a matter of fact, I guess cereal grains _are what made settled society -- and hence "all this" -- possible. And the _Epic_ was, in that sense, correct. And, of course, countless numbers of people would die, if they vanished tomorrow. This is why Loren Cordain called his famous paper on them "Cereal Grains: Humanity’s Double-Edged Sword":

[http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf](http://http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf)

Brilliant paper. It's a clumsy metaphor, though -- a double-edged sword, such as a knight's sword, is no more a danger to its wielder than a single-edged sword, such as a saber or falchion.

But one gets what the "cuts both ways" metaphor is getting at. They're bad for health, yet we rely on them (although, I guess, how long that reliance can continue and intensive arable farming can go on without destroying soil-structure is the big question.)

But, you don't actually need cereal grains to have civilization, including a high-level literary culture. Iceland proves that. The sagas are a major literary achievement even on a European-wide scale - and a unique one. In some respects they anticipate the novel.

Yet medieval Iceland got by without grain. People lived principally on meat, seafood, and dairy products such as skyr_. It was one of Vilhjalmur Stefansson's first achievements as an anthropologists to do a survey on Icelandic teeth and to demonstrate that in the early period they were mostly free from cavities at the time when bread was rarely available, because the climate was too cold to grow grain.

# Thread: Optimal Clothes?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-clothes.2079/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-27T19:04:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-clothes.2079/post-33970>

Anyway...I had this huge ...probably one of the biggest ever headaches I've ever had and call me crazy but I think it might have been my new pair of pants that caused it.

[http://oecotextiles.wordpress.com/tag/chemical-substance/](http://http://oecotextiles.wordpress.com/tag/chemical-substance/)

I looked at this so far and they mention certain things in textiles causing potential issues.....could it have been the pants and if so will washing them help? I also feel like the headache got a bit better after washing the inside of my legs where I got a red area...rash...from the pants possibly? The pants are a 55% polyester 45% repreve polyester(recycled) blend.

My headache after sleeping is still there but substantially lessened....this generally happens with any headache....if its the pants maybe its because there are still toxins from the pants in my system...if anyone has a clue or more info please let me know.

Thanks

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-27T19:05:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-clothes.2079/post-33971>

I didn't have this issue with a similar pair of pants that were my brothers...not sure if its the same blend...maybe his have had the toxins washed out by now since they aren't new...or maybe I'm completely off base with this crazy theory...but since I had a rash on my inner legs corresponding with the wearing of these pants I'm wondering if it had something to do with it.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-27T19:20:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-clothes.2079/post-33978>

I always wash new clothing before I wear it unless it's outerwear that won't have skin contact. There's no telling what kind of chemicals have been used on the fabric so I think it's best to do as much as possible to get rid of it before wearing.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-27T19:32:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-clothes.2079/post-33982>

Yeah something I'll try to keep in mind every time I buy clothes...that headache was horrible. Like migraine type pain...I was really wondering why...as I've been eating pretty good lately. If it was anything else I'd be surprised too many coincidences. The leg rash happening at the same time as the headache was too convenient. I'm glad I picked up on it...because I think washing will fix the issue.....if I wear them after washing and get a headache I'll toss them.

# Thread: Vitamin D
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-28T03:59:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/post-34090>

[http://www.dlife.com/diabetes-food-and-fitness/dietary\_supplements/vitamin\_d\_calcium](http://http://www.dlife.com/diabetes-food-and-fitness/dietary_supplements/vitamin_d_calcium)

Vitamin D and Calcium for Better Blood Sugar

> New Recommendations
>
> But that caution has largely evaporated over the past several years, mainly because those old studies have been rejected due to poor-quality science. Since then vitamin D has entered a nutritional and medical renaissance of sorts.
>
>
>
> Now experts like Michael Holick, M.D., of the Boston University School of Medicine, and Walter C. Willett, M.D., of the Harvard School of Public Health, stress vitamin Dâ€™s safety â€” and recommend much larger amounts.
>
>
>
> An article in Archives of Internal Medicine (2009) reports that vitamin D levels among Americans have decreased over the past 10 or so years. Researchers note that three of every four people do not have adequate vitamin D levels. The numbers may be even worse during the winter months when the sun is low and people spend more time indoors, which interferes with vitamin D production.
>
>
>
> **The growing consensus is that every man, women, child, and infant should take 1,000 IU of vitamin D daily or 2,000 IU if they have a dark complexion. That may sound like a lot compared to the current 200 IU, but you would have to take 50,000 IU daily â€” practically a whole bottle a day â€” for months before you would develop any symptoms of toxicity.**

**

this article is a bit confusing for me... I believe I have calcium in cells versus magnesium because** of my sugar levels. I was hoping to kind of use the glucose testing as means to determine calcium. If anyone has a clue or thought, please share.

### chocolate — 2012-06-28T04:05:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/post-34092>

[http://www.dlife.com/diabetes-food-and-fitness/dietary\_supplements/vitamin\_D\_calcium.page01](http://http://www.dlife.com/diabetes-food-and-fitness/dietary_supplements/vitamin_D_calcium.page01)

> Vitamin D & Calcium for Better Blood Sugar (continued)
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> In a 2007 review of the research, researchers noted that vitamin D and calcium work hand in hand to influence the body’s secretion of insulin, the hormone that controls blood sugar levels.
>
>
>
> In one study of 314 healthy adults, vitamin D appears to have offset or prevented age-related increase in blood glucose levels. In the control group, participants averaged a 6.1 mg/dl increase in fasting blood sugar over three years. _**However, people taking vitamin D (700 IU daily) and calcium (500 mg daily) supplements had virtually no change in their fasting blood sugar levels, according to the 2007 report in Diabetes Care.**_**
>
>
>
> Early in 2009, doctors at the Harvard School of Public Health found that low vitamin D and calcium levels were related to higher, unhealthy levels of C-peptide, a common marker of insulin function. A separate study by researchers at the University of California, Los Angeles, found that people with low vitamin D levels were more likely to have insulin resistance (a form of prediabetes) and also abnormalities in beta cells, which make insulin in the pancreas.**
>
>
>
> In 2008 Australian researchers reported on their use of vitamin D in the treatment of 51 patients with type 2 diabetes who had nerve problems, including numbness, tingling, and burning. After taking an average of 2,000 IU daily for three months, pain decreased by roughly half.
>
>
>
> Call Your Doc
>
> None of this means you should start treating yourself with a high dose of vitamin D. However, it’s probably worthwhile having your vitamin D and calcium blood levels tested and discussing with your doctor the possibility of taking supplements and an appropriate dosage for you. We live in the age of the proactive healthcare consumer: Call your doctor’s office and get the answers you need.

Does anyone know where the blood calcium levels are that they are referring to? If its in your blood, does that mean magnesium is in your cells? I know they didn't even mention the vitamin k2.

### Michael — 2012-06-28T21:02:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/post-34212>

> **chocolate;34247 wrote:**
>
> Does anyone know where the blood calcium levels are that they are referring to?
>
>
> I read the article you linked, and it seemed to me that they were referring to _dietary levels of Vitamin D and calcium. (It turns out that was only partially true.)
>
>
>
> I tried to find the study by Harvard researchers that they refer to. So I googled:
>
>
>
> pubmed 2009 vitamin d calcium c-peptide
>
>
>
> This is what I came up with:
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19141703](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19141703)
>
>
>
> Note the title is:
>
>
>
>
> > Plasma C-peptide is inversely associated with calcium intake in women and with plasma 25-hydroxy vitamin D in men.
>
>
>
> So the article you originally found seems to be conflating calcium intake in women and plasma 25-hydroxy vitamin D in men.
>
>
>
> Not a good start.
>
>
>
> The Abstract begins:
>
>
>
> The consumption of calcium, vitamin D, and dairy products may be associated with a reduced risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus. However, whether this reduction is due to calcium, vitamin D, or other components of dairy products is not clear ...._

_

I'm not inclined to worry much about what they think "may" be true or take a lot of notice of which "component" they're hanging their hat on while admitting that nothing's "clear". Maybe I'm just a cynic.

I think the safest advice is to take some vitamin D if you can't get out in the sun much and to have your plasma level of 25-hydroxy vitamin D checked. You can look at people's recommendations for what that level should be online. Professor Cordain suggests to shoot for a level of 60 ng/ml. Maybe Jack suggests a level somewhere on the site.

If you do push your vitamin D level up, then you probably want to make sure that you're getting adequate vitamin A, too, since they seem to work synergistically. The occasional serving of liver from a grass-fed animal would be a way to get that.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/01/22/new-evidence-of-synergy-between-vitamins-a-and-d-protection-against-autoimmune-diseases/](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/01/22/new-evidence-of-synergy-between-vitamins-a-and-d-protection-against-autoimmune-diseases/)

I wouldn't supplement calcium, whatever the article implies. (Heck, they didn't even seem to have read the Abstract of the Harvard study properly: "whether this reduction is due to calcium, vitamin D, or other components of dairy products is not clear ..._".) And calcium supplements certainly are associated with increased risk of stroke and heart attack.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-28T21:04:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/post-34213>

Vitamin A levels are usually never a problem on primal template......even a SAD.

# Thread: Vilhjalmur Stefansson and the Icelanders
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vilhjalmur-stefansson-and-the-icelanders.2082/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-06-28T10:35:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vilhjalmur-stefansson-and-the-icelanders.2082/post-34117>

There was an earlier discussion in which Vilhjalmur Stefansson and Iceland came up. I thought it would be interesting to post references for that, so I googled "stefansson iceland skulls teeth". Here's what I found.

There's this, which is an article Stefansson wrote for _Harper's Monthly Magazine_ that someone's put online:

> **My first anthropological commission was from the Peabody Museum of Harvard University when they sent John W. Hasting and me to Iceland in 1905. We found in one place a medieval graveyard that was being cut away by the sea. Skulls were rolling about in the water at high tide, at low tide we gathered them and picked up scattered teeth here and there. As wind and water shifted the sands we found more and more teeth until there was a handful. Later we got permission to excavate the cemetery, and eventually we brought with us to Harvard a miscellaneous lot of bone which included 80 skull, and as said, a great many loose teeth.
>
>
>
> The collection has been studied by dentists and physical anthropologists without the discovery of a single cavity in even one tooth.**
>
>
>
> The skulls in the Hastings-Stefansson collection represent persons of ordinary Icelandic blood. There were no aborigines in that island when the Irish discovered it some time before 700 A. D. When the Norsemen got there in 860 they found no people except the Irish. It is now variously estimated that in origin the Icelanders are from 10 percent to 30 percent Irish, 40 percent to 50 percent Norwegian, the remainder, perhaps 10 percent, from Scotland, England, Sweden, and Denmark.
>
>
>
> None of the people whose blood went into the Icelandic stock are racially immune to tooth decay, nor are the modern Icelanders. Then why were the Icelanders of the Middle Ages immune?
>
> ....

[http://www.biblelife.org/stefansson3.htm](http://http://www.biblelife.org/stefansson3.htm)

and this:

[http://blog.zeroinginonhealth.com/2009/01/25/visit-your-dentist-twice-a-year-part-1/](http://http://blog.zeroinginonhealth.com/2009/01/25/visit-your-dentist-twice-a-year-part-1/)

And some references get pulled up from Google Books.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-28T14:41:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vilhjalmur-stefansson-and-the-icelanders.2082/post-34129>

immune to cavities? nope they had no access to carbs.

### Michael — 2012-06-29T12:09:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vilhjalmur-stefansson-and-the-icelanders.2082/post-34332>

I suppose you're interested in the Icelander's access to cold, too, Dr. Kruse.

It's interesting that they seem to have a cultural fascination with feats of strength going back a long way - presumably back to viking times:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strength\_athletics\_in\_Iceland](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strength_athletics_in_Iceland)

# Thread: Does anyone make their food ahead and freeze it?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-28T17:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-34170>

I was thinking I could make a few things, sauce wise anyway and freeze them. The epi paleo is kind of new lifestyle. We had always baked off a turkey and made cranberries and gravy and froze servings in tupperware, so we always had something to dump on toast. I don't mind continuing to slowly kill my husband in such a fashion, I just have issues with myself and daughter. My son is very precise about what he eats and he tags along if its convenient.

The really good spices we want to use often and sometimes its hard on the run. Sardines and codfish are easy no matter what. Herbed ghee, coconut fat, and bacon grease frozen in batches or cut into cubes would help. We're getting a taste for our shrimp fried in bacon grease.

I was also thinking some DAL with chili grind beef instead of lentils, just to get the spices.

The raspberries are in season and there is definitely something to the ketones. The oily urine happens instantly. I just wonder, since they are in season, do you freeze the sauces.... and eat them before season ends? This is an issue for me, because I live in grocery hell/hell part time.

### ealachan — 2012-06-28T20:17:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-34201>

> **chocolate;34327 wrote:** I was thinking I could make a few things, sauce wise anyway and freeze them. The epi paleo is kind of new lifestyle. We had always baked off a turkey and made cranberries and gravy and froze servings in tupperware, so we always had something to dump on toast. I don't mind continuing to slowly kill my husband in such a fashion, I just have issues with myself and daughter. My son is very precise about what he eats and he tags along if its convenient.
>
> The really good spices we want to use often and sometimes its hard on the run. Sardines and codfish are easy no matter what. Herbed ghee, coconut fat, and bacon grease frozen in batches or cut into cubes would help. We're getting a taste for our shrimp fried in bacon grease.
>
> I was also thinking some DAL with chili grind beef instead of lentils, just to get the spices.
>
>
>
> The raspberries are in season and there is definitely something to the ketones. The oily urine happens instantly. I just wonder, since they are in season, do you freeze the sauces.... and eat them before season ends? This is an issue for me, because I live in grocery hell/hell part time.

I live in the land of "epic-quality produce for about 3 months a year", because we have such a short growing season and such long winters. As such, I've certainly gotten into the habit of buying lots of veg and berries from my favorite local farm, prepping it and freezing it up for later use. Whole meals, I don't normally make ahead and freeze, mostly because I just can't be arsed. I do sometimes make up a super big batch of soup (cauldo verde, chicken, egg-drop but I leave the egg out until I'm ready to eat), chili, or bolognase sauce and freeze half of it, and I've had good luck making big batches of potstickers and freezing them ahead, too.

Personally, I wouldn't suggest using something like pre-frozen shrimp or fish in a dish that you were then going to re-freeze, because the texture of the meat will likely suffer greatly. If mushy/chewy shrimp or fish don't bother you of course, then have at it...

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-28T20:18:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-34202>

Pesto freezes AWESOME! So does Dr. K's veggie/oil sauces.

### chocolate — 2012-07-14T04:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37676>

> Personally, I wouldn't suggest using something like pre-frozen shrimp or fish in a dish that you were then going to re-freeze, because the texture of the meat will likely suffer greatly. If mushy/chewy shrimp or fish don't bother you of course, then have at it...

So, I tried to ask on your blog... literacy issues again... do you have pork/shrimp/ginger egg roll stuffing type recipe without the eggroll? I'm trying to use a lot of sesame oil on meat salads or something.

### ealachan — 2012-07-14T13:55:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37714>

> **chocolate;37843 wrote:** So, I tried to ask on your blog... literacy issues again... do you have pork/shrimp/ginger egg roll stuffing type recipe without the eggroll? I'm trying to use a lot of sesame oil on meat salads or something.

I have one somewhere, yeah...I'll dig it up for you. It's what I use to fill pork dumplings, but it's also really nice cooked up with scrambled eggs or just cooked and tossed with veggies. Would probably make nice meatballs in an Asian chicken or fish broth, too.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-14T16:20:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37738>

I freeze cooked bacon & sausage all the time.

I buy large packages of bacon (I have 80 oz. of bacon in my fridge now, to cook this weekend). I cook the slices all at once (I use my George Foreman grill, very time-consuming but nice straight slices), then place in a ziploc bag & freeze.

For sausage, I either cook it all crumbly & place in plastic containers in the freezer, or make patties & put them in a ziploc freezer bag.

This way, each morning, I just pull out what I want and throw it into the pan with my eggs & cook it all together. The heat of the pan thaws out the bacon or sausage enough so that I don't have to use my microwave. Trying to use the microwave lots less these days, don't need extra radiation.

### chocolate — 2012-07-14T16:55:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37753>

The bacon idea is great. I could clean the fat at the same time. The sausage, too. I keep avoiding large packages and one of our grocery stores has an ok mix.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-14T17:01:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37756>

Re: The fat. I use ceramic soup bowls to pour the fat while it's still hot into while the bacon or sausage is cooking, which I then place in the fridge to harden. For the 80 oz. of bacon I'm cooking today, it will take about 3-4 soup bowls.

Once hardened, the fat can then be scooped into plastic containers for use later in other foods. Since I live alone, it's way too much fat, so I scoop the extra into ziploc bags to throw away. This way, no clogged pipes, which I did have last year. Notsomuch fun when your kitchen sinks back up due to your bacon grease clogs.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-14T17:03:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37757>

Something else I do: Since I'm always in a hurry each morning, I like to just crumble the bacon and add to eggs while cooking, and I've thought of every time-saver I can.

With bacon, sometimes I'll cut up an entire package into about 1" pieces prior to cooking. That way, as it cooks, I get bacon crumbles and no messy bacon-hands in the morning. The entire package of crumbles goes into my ziploc freezer bag, and they thaw while cooking in the eggs. For this, I just cook it in a pan (with one of those grease-splatter lids), rather than the George Foreman.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-14T22:55:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37838>

> **PrimalPam;37924 wrote:** Something else I do: Since I'm always in a hurry each morning, I like to just crumble the bacon and add to eggs while cooking, and I've thought of every time-saver I can.
>
>
>
> With bacon, sometimes I'll cut up an entire package into about 1" pieces prior to cooking. That way, as it cooks, I get bacon crumbles and no messy bacon-hands in the morning. The entire package of crumbles goes into my ziploc freezer bag, and they thaw while cooking in the eggs. For this, I just cook it in a pan (with one of those grease-splatter lids), rather than the George Foreman.

Ok ok, i like this! Plus less grease spatter stove to clean.

### ealachan — 2012-07-21T12:38:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-39965>

My husband was just recently diagnosed as diabetic (he's only been doing paleo / optimal about half the time and eating SAD the rest of the time, so he didn't do himself any favors there), and since he pretty much has no clue what being diabetic means, I literally had to make a "stuff that will eventually kill you if you keep eating it" list for him yesterday. I also made a "things you SHOULD eat" list to go with it. I printed out two copies - one to hang on the fridge, and the other one he put in his wallet so that he could refer to it while at work (where most of the bad eating was happening).

Trying to explain to him this morning why bananas and a chocolate bar for breakfast are not a good idea was interesting. I've got my work cut out for me...oy!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-41166>

> **ealachan;40152 wrote:** My husband was just recently diagnosed as diabetic (he's only been doing paleo / optimal about half the time and eating SAD the rest of the time, so he didn't do himself any favors there), and since he pretty much has no clue what being diabetic means, I literally had to make a "stuff that will eventually kill you if you keep eating it" list for him yesterday. I also made a "things you SHOULD eat" list to go with it. I printed out two copies - one to hang on the fridge, and the other one he put in his wallet so that he could refer to it while at work (where most of the bad eating was happening).
>
>
>
> Trying to explain to him this morning why bananas and a chocolate bar for breakfast are not a good idea was interesting. I've got my work cut out for me...oy!

I used to teach diabetics and found that they hate to feel or be deprived, blood sugar issues hypo/hyper and all that, it's tricky especially when you have to take blood sugar lowering meds as well. So had some luck with adding on instead of taking away. Example: half a banana, 3-4 raw eggs, Tbs of chocolate, tsp to tbs of coconut oil and heavy cream and plenty of ice cubes and maybe some stevia for a morning milk shake. Go easy on the oil at first and build up. Get him to agree to eat some protein with every junk food snack. Perhaps a container of 4 glasses of water which he should finish before the end of work each day. (this doesn't include water he should be drinking before and after work). He shouldn't let himself feel hungry so give him plenty of high protein snacks for work. hard boiled eggs, pemmican, nuts, whatever you all can agree on. He should eat one before any junk food snack. You all should be reading everything you can on DM and he should be as knowledgeable as an MD. Dr Richard Bernstein has a very thorough book on DM basics. So as his nutrition improves he can gradually cut out the junk food and be more secure at work that he won't be having stomach pain. Writing down everything eaten every day for a while (like a couple of years) and how many ounces and how much protein, fat and CHO is immensely helpful. I use Fit Day as my online log as it calculates all that. Of course blood sugar monitoring with a meter before and after meals is a must. I'm a firm believer in the BAB and if it's not the perfect one at first at least it's a start. Pretty soon he'll realize he feels better and when he's telling you what to do then you know you've got him where you want him  Good Luck, I know it's not an easy task.

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T14:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-54083>

> **ealachan;40152 wrote:** My husband was just recently diagnosed as diabetic (he's only been doing paleo / optimal about half the time and eating SAD the rest of the time, so he didn't do himself any favors there), and since he pretty much has no clue what being diabetic means, I literally had to make a "stuff that will eventually kill you if you keep eating it" list for him yesterday. I also made a "things you SHOULD eat" list to go with it. I printed out two copies - one to hang on the fridge, and the other one he put in his wallet so that he could refer to it while at work (where most of the bad eating was happening).
>
>
>
> Trying to explain to him this morning why bananas and a chocolate bar for breakfast are not a good idea was interesting. I've got my work cut out for me...oy!

I was declared pre-diabetic back in September 2011 with an A1c of 11.2. I was already gung-ho in my talks with the Endocrinologist about a lower carbohydrate intake and deleting everything from the grains-group. She had given me the green light.

Fast forward three months - November 2011, I had a 12hr fasting done and my A1c DROPPED to 5.2, my cholesterol did a massive flip that lit up my home telephone of three practitioners all begging me "what did you do to get such a great CHO and drop your A1c so fast?!?!?"... My PCP, My Endo and my Dermatologist all called me when they got the lab results, and I told them the truth: no breads, no rice, no pasta, no corn, no flour, no milk, no soymilk.... that is EXACTLY what I did. My husband lost 25lbs in that 3 month time frame with the food changes I made in the house and even he said he felt like he ate better! He limits his consumption of them to only when he is out on the road where he is a truck driver...

Im telling you - that whole category of foods is a definite issue... my HDL right now is 86 - my Endo just stood there and shook her head - she couldnt believe someone with a metabolic disorder could achieve that...

now if we can just figure out why my body wont drop the fat despite my dedication to much better food choices, Ill be happier... ugh...

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T15:00:30+0300

> **Jack Kruse;54790 wrote:** well dont tell some of the paleo bloggers this.......they will start a war with you over it. Congrats!!!

Dr.Kruse - I honestly could care less, LOL!!!!! The fact that one food group created such a hell for me, and was EASILY rectified by simply removing it from the equation has angered many of the RD's in the physician group I work in. The die-hard bread-lovers I work with in my AR department cant believe I could ever give up such a thing... Honestly, Id rather put a roll in the bell of my Bassoon, hit a low Bflat and watch that roll catapult into the air while someone takes a good swing with a bat to it! BWAHAHAHAHAHHAA!!!!

My CHO one year later (labs done in June 2012) still remain the same - maintained all of the numbers, but everything improved even more by a few points, A1c maintained.

To the reply for throw the scale away - the only scale I use religiously is the food scale when it comes to the carbohydrates. The 'other' scale is used once per month but Im not foolish to think its accurate.. I would much rather use measuring tape and current clothing as the true form of measurment.

I have been able to drop about 40+ pounds on my own, but I am in the worst plateau possible... that is why I am here on this site. Im hoping that the missing puzzle piece to my body holding on to the weight can be found here... I need to drop the remaining 100lbs.. but I sure as hell wont rely on an Endo telling me to take Qsymia... that is just frigging rediculous... Im on enough damn prescriptions right now and would love to get rid of all of them....

### cramernh — 2012-09-26T12:42:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-54382>

The other side of this equation is the fact them damn RD's are so mind-screwed into the "MYPLATE" concept, you have to eat breads, grains, blah blah blah... Honestly speaking I could care less about that philosophy. Its a one-method plan that they _think_ is appropriate for everyone.. well sorry, thats not the kind of crack I get involved in, thank you very much. I had an argument with a supposed RD on myfitnesspal.com last month stating that RD's would probably be better off working in conjunction with a PCP, or a Specialist to collaborate nutritional care with the patient because their methods are absolutely askewed. She didnt like that. She feels her Master's Degree should hold the same weight as an MD.... I CRACKED UP LAUGHING. I asked her "are you versed in diagnosing patients? Can you honestly sit there - the keyboard commando you are trying to pull off, and tell me you know the complete medical history of Stein-Leventhal, how it was discovered, what it was referring to, the year it was declared, the proper treatments of a patient with Stein-Leventhal, the proper tests to run and actually to read and understand the labs with your female patients who are Stein-Leventhal??? Dont sit there and tell me your RD should weigh the same as an MD - it just doesnt work that way in real life.... She tried to rip me a new oriface with the whole elimination of the bread/grains group citing I am not reaping the benefits of whole grains and the much needed fiber for my colon. I asked her "I think you forgot about the fiber content of vegetables - its much more natural and not processed as the precious bread you are praising."

She thought it was damn near impossible to completely irradicate my A1c - I told her "keep on smoking that crack because Ive got labs to prove it - and Im trained to be able to read and understand them as well"... yeah.. I got cranky.. I dont 'do' well with Keyboard Commandos, LOL!

I scanned my labs onto the thread (and blackened out my personal info with the exception of my last name) and told her to mind her RD-mouth when she tells people 'its not possible', because it bloody well is possible.

# Thread: Not so hot anymore
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-so-hot-anymore.2092/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cowboy — 2012-06-28T22:38:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-so-hot-anymore.2092/post-34223>

I have spent most of my adult life feeling hot. Sweaty head at night, sweaty brow if the temp was at all a bit on the warm side. Been using CT in a shower for the last couple of months and finally got a outside tub to use about 2.5 weeks ago. I have noticed I don't get the night time sweaty head, and I don't have that general " I'm too warm" feeling any more. Played some golf in 97 degree temps (humidity was low at 9%) and really didn't feel "too warm" I am sure I was sweating some but it was dry it was hard to notice. Did some yard work the last few days in 85 degree heat, and humidity in the 20-30% range and did sweat a normal amount but seemed to cool off real fast. We have been using AC lately and I find it is pretty comfortable at 72 degrees rather than the 68 degrees I always kept it at. Is this what should be happening?

### nonchalant — 2012-06-28T23:10:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-so-hot-anymore.2092/post-34231>

Hi Cowboy. Yes, I know what you're saying. It's easier to deal with the heat when you're not burning up inside as well.
