### chocolate — 2012-07-15T22:47:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-know-why-factor-x-matters-now.2118/post-38116>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/07/15/discovery.chemical.affects.biological.clock.offers.new.way.treat.diabetes?utm\_source=feedburner&utm\_medium=feed&utm\_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm\_content=FaceBook](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/07/15/discovery.chemical.affects.biological.clock.offers.new.way.treat.diabetes?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm_content=FaceBook)

Discovery of chemical that affects biological clock offers new way to treat diabetes

Published: Sunday, July 15, 2012 - 13:31 in Biology & Nature

Biologists at UC San Diego have discovered a chemical that offers a completely new and promising direction for the development of drugs to treat metabolic disorders such as type 2 diabetes -- a major public health concern in the United States due to the current obesity epidemic. Their discovery, detailed in a paper published July 13 in an advance online issue of the journal Science, initially came as a surprise because the chemical they isolated does not directly control glucose production in the liver, but instead affects the activity of a key protein that regulates the internal mechanisms of our daily night and day activities, which scientists call our circadian rhythm or biological clock.

Scientists had long suspected that diabetes and obesity could be linked to problems in the biological clock. Laboratory mice with altered biological clocks, for example, often become obese and develop diabetes. Two years ago, a team headed by Steve Kay, dean of the Division of Biological Sciences at UC San Diego, discovered the first biochemical link between the biological clock and diabetes. **It found that a key protein, cryptochrome, that regulates the biological clocks of plants, insects and mammals also regulates glucose production in the liver and that altering the levels of this protein could improve the health of diabetic mice.**

Now Kay and his team have discovered a small molecule -- one that can be easily developed into a drug -- that controls the intricate molecular cogs or timekeeping mechanisms of cryptochrome in such a manner that it can repress the production of glucose by the liver. Like mice and other animals, humans have evolved biochemical mechanisms to keep a steady supply of glucose flowing to the brain at night, when we're not eating or otherwise active.

"At the end of the night, our hormones signal that we're in a fasting state," said Kay. "And during the day, when we're active, our biological clock shuts down those fasting signals that tell our liver to make more glucose because that's when we're eating."

Diabetes is caused by an accumulation of glucose in the blood, which can lead to heart disease, strokes, kidney failure and blindness. In type 1 diabetes, destruction of insulin producing cells in the pancreas results in the high blood sugar. In type 2 diabetes, which makes up 90 percent of the cases, gradual resistance to insulin because of obesity or other problems, leads to high blood sugar.

Kay and his collaborators discovered in 2010 that cryptochrome plays a critical role in regulating the internal timing of our cyclical eating patterns, timing our fasting at night with our eating during the day to maintain a steady supply of glucose in our bloodstream. Other researchers have recently discovered that cryptochrome also has the potential to reduce high blood sugar from asthma medication by adjusting the time of day a patient takes their medication. "We found that if we increased cryptochrome levels genetically in the liver we could inhibit the production of glucose by the liver," said Kay.

What he and his team found in their most recent discovery was that a much smaller molecule, dubbed "KL001" (for the first such compound from the Kay Lab), can regulate that activity as well. It slowed down the biological clock by stabilizing the cryptochrome protein -- that is, it essentially prevented crypotochrome from being sent to the cellular garbage can, the proteasomes.

The discovery of KL001 was serendipitous, a complete surprise to the scientists that came about from a parallel effort in Kay's laboratory to identify molecules that lengthen the biological clock. Two years ago, Tsuyoshi Hirota, a postdoctoral fellow in Kay's laboratory found a compound that had the greatest effect ever seen on circadian rhythm, a chemical the biologists dubbed "longdaysin" because it lengthened the daily biological clocks of human cells by more than 10 hours.

Continuing his search, Hirota resumed his efforts to find more chemicals that lengthened or slowed down circadian rhythms, enabling the scientists to understand more about the intricate chemical and genetic machinery of the biological clock. He and his colleagues in Kay's lab did this by screening thousands of compounds from a chemical library with human cells in individual micro-titer wells in which a luciferase gene from fireflies is attached to the biological clock machinery, enabling the scientists to detect a glow whenever the biological clock is activated. Their molecular fishing expedition came up with a number of other compounds, one of which was KL001.

"We found other compounds that like longdaysin slowed down the biological clock," said Kay. "But unlike longdaysin, these compounds did not inhibit the protein kinases that longdaysin inhibits so we knew this compound must be working differently. What we needed to know was what is this compound interacting with? And we were absolutely stunned when we discovered that what was binding most specifically to our compound,KL001, was the clock protein cryptochrome that our lab has worked on in plants, flies and mammals for the last 20 years."

Kay's team turned to biological chemists in Peter Schultz's laboratory at The Scripps Research Institute to characterize the compound and understand better chemically how it affected cryptochrome to lengthen the biological clock.

"Those biochemical studies showed us that KL001 prevents cryptochrome from being degraded by the proteasome system, which was another big surprise," said Kay. "It essentially interferes with the signal to send cryptochrome to the garbage can."

To understand how KL001 worked mechanistically with cryptochrome to control the biological clock, the team initiated a collaboration with Frank Doyle and his group at UC Santa Barbara. "They constructed a beautiful mathematical model of cryptochrome's role in the clock," said Kay. "That model was essential in allowing us to understand the action of the compound because the biological clock is very complicated. It's like opening the back of a Rolex and seeing the hundreds of tiny little cogs that are tightly integrated."

Based on that mathematical model, the scientists predicted that adding KL001 to mouse liver cells should stabilize cryptochrome and that the increased level of cryptochrome would inhibit the production of enzymes in the liver that stimulate the process of gluconeogenesis -- the generation of glucose during fasting. Experiments done together with the laboratory of David Brenner, dean of the UC San Diego School of Medicine and Vice Chancellor for Health Sciences, confirmed that prediction to be true.

"In mouse liver cells," said Kay, "we showed that KL001 inhibited gene expression for gluconeogenesis that is induced when exposed to the hormone glucagon, which promotes glucose production by the liver. It's a hormone we all produce in fasting states. And our compound, in a dose dependent way, inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis, the actual production of glucose by those liver cells."

Kay said the next step for the research group is to understand how KL001 and similar molecules that affect cryptochrome function in living systems, such has laboratory mice. The scientists also plan to probe how such compounds affect other processes besides the liver that may tie the biological clock to metabolic diseases. "As with any surprise discovery," he notes, "this opens the door to more opportunities for novel therapeutics than we can currently imagine."

Besides Kay, Hirota, Schultz, Doyle and Brenner, other scientists involved in the discovery were Mariko Sawa, Pagkapol Y. Pongsawakul and Tim Sonntag of UC San Diego's Division of Biological Sciences; Jae Wook Lee of TSRI; Peter St. John of UC Santa Barbara; Keiko Iwaisako, Takako Noguchi and David Welsh of UC San Diego's School of Medicine.

The study was supported by grants from the National Institutes of Health (GM074868, H051573 GM085764, GM096873, MH082945).

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-02T20:45:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-know-why-factor-x-matters-now.2118/post-49007>

Isn't it good to lower our levels of IGF-1 so we don't burn through our stem cell depots and bring cell senescence closer? IGF-1 has been likened to throwing high octane fuel on a fire. If I remember correctly Jack or someone else here linked a video of a UK journalist who mentioned the South American dwarves who were immune to neolithic disease because their epigenetics coded for extremely low production if IGF-1. The same video talked about alternating feast and fast days which also lowered IGF-1 levels. Is it possible for someone to have long telomeres and constant high levels of IGF-1?

Incidentally I learned elsewhere that eating high fat requires at least 5 hours of no snacking between each high fat meal to avoid neolithic disease. Otherwise the body doesn't have time to prepare for the next meal. Mice put on high fat diets with plenty of time between meals did far better than mice allowed to browse on fat all day long.

Jack is absolutely correct. I went heavy on the seafood and regular CT for a four month period and had dramatic lipid profile changes (total cholesterol went up but all other parameters went in the right direction) and all markers of inflammation dropped like a rock as well. For example, HDL went from 40 to 70 over the four month period.  My doctor said what I did was nearly impossible to do in such a short period of time. I have just started another five month test period to see if sustained seafood, regular lifting of heavy things (once or twice a week max) and CT can even further bring me to optimal health. Note: I exercised maybe four times during the first test period. I plan on having a complete hormone panel completed, thorough blood analysis, and telomeres measured. I have an identical twin brother so we are comparing results with life style practices. We have the same doctor and she is taking copious notes! For those curious, I am 59 years young.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-04-09T17:47:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-know-why-factor-x-matters-now.2118/post-350372>

No. GLP1 drugs cause a lot of chaos in CYP pathways. Your better off using NIF and MB if you want speed, but you'll risk a seminoma or ovarian cancer if you do.

Not a great trade off.

# Thread: One little glass of Malbec wreaks havoc...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-06-30T16:41:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34584>

I was going to put the alcohol question in "Ask Jack" today, but I see someone else has, so he answered my question! Thanks Dr. K.!

Wow. So I've been doing the LR since Jan. 2012, and 3 weeks ago, began CT, and got right into it: 5x/week, 50-53F @ 40 mins in the tub. Did one face dunk, and I was in the tub, full body. Love it! Have followed the protocol exactly: the large glass of ice water, the high fat/high protein meal, no alcohol, strict keto-Paleo diet, seafood every day. Lost 6 lbs. in 3 weeks!

Wed. night, went to dinner with a friend, and decided that a glass of Malbec sounded nice. And so did the bread on the table, along with the pasta on my shrimp plate which I usually sub for veggies! Doh!). And so did the Sprinkles cupcakes afterwards.

I woke up Thursday, feeling drunk (from one glass!). I weighed 3 lbs. heavier. I was up half the night sweating. Miserable!!!

Thursday, I got right back on the keto-Paleo wagon with the CT, and feel good again.

Now I realize that I've eaten cleaner, so my liver lacks the enzymes to properly process the alcohol, but I had no idea it would happen that quickly, and that one glass of wine would make me feel THAT bad and help me make such bad food decisions. OK, now I get it...no more alcohol!

### LinD — 2012-06-30T16:44:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34586>

If I read your post correctly, I would blame the bread, pasta, & cupcakes, and NOT the Malbec!

### skywaykate — 2012-06-30T16:57:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34590>

> **LinD;34746 wrote:** If I read your post correctly, I would blame the bread, pasta, & cupcakes, and NOT the Malbec!

You can feel drunk/hung over from food. I've come back from Whole Foods sampling everything (and no alcohol) and felt that way, in my case at that time, probably from the cheese. Probably wasn't the wine, unless that made it ok to have the bread, pasta and cupcake, and even then it wasn't a physical reason like your liver, but a physiologic reason.

sent from my phone while wrangling at least 1 toddler...expect typos, smile when you see them...the toddlers or the typos

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-30T17:29:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34599>

LOL...yes, it was probably the combo of wheat, sugar, & alcohol.

I felt very tipsy after the wine, which was before the food. So it must have been the combo.

And yes, the wine made my defenses against the sugar & wheat go down. It always does. I've never been a huge drinker; have never even tasted "hard alcohol", like vodka, scotch, etc. So even a little wine is now too much.

I can usually handle a couple glasses of Malbec though. This was really different!

### janagram — 2012-06-30T18:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34612>

it doesn't take long to lose your tolerance to alcohol! If I don't drink wine for a week...(and that never happens!) I feel high after half a glass! Everyone is right tho', the grain and sugar did you in!

### LinD — 2012-07-01T05:51:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34747>

Same here! It's a good thing, though, actually. Like sugar, there's no benefit with alcohol. I do like wine, though. I just do. I have it two-three times a week, but can easily go without, and sometimes do.

### Destiny — 2012-07-01T14:19:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34760>

I was at a wedding yesterday and had the opposite reaction to drinking one glass of wine! I was normal! I usually get drunk after 1/2 a glass!

I am thinking it was due to a lot of seafood I ate there! Of course, there was a lot of other carb stuff but I was not tempted.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-01T15:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34765>

I've never been much of a drinker, but usually, I can drink a couple glasses with dinner and resist all the sugar, grains, etc. This was SO different. Perhaps because I hadn't drank in a month; perhaps because of CT. But I felt tipsy about 1/2 glass in.

That was Wednesday; today is Sunday, and I'm finally down the 3 lbs. I gained from that one cheat meal. Sooooo not worth it. Have CT'd, eaten very clean keto-Paleo. And no more alcohol!

### LinD — 2012-07-01T16:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34780>

Yay, Pam.

### johnnyb — 2012-07-01T17:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34787>

> **PrimalPam;34744 wrote:** I woke up Thursday, feeling drunk (from one glass!). I weighed 3 lbs. heavier. I was up half the night sweating. Miserable!!!

This sounds familiar. When I was eating 80/20 Primal I would eat Mexican food out every second or third Friday with friends. Sometimes that would include the works, a margarita, chips, rice, and beans. I would wake up the same way, all hot and sweaty. I wish I had actually taken my temperature. I'd swear if I had an infrared camera I would be illuminating the whole room. Losing the carbs was the best fix, but sleeping without any covers or a sheet on top seemed to relieve the sweating (probably weather dependent). I figured my body was doing a good thing by burning off the extra calories.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-01T17:39:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34792>

> **johnnyb;34947 wrote:** This sounds familiar. When I was eating 80/20 Primal I would eat Mexican food out every second or third Friday with friends.

The 80/20 rule just doesn't work for me, as I take too much leeway with it. My new doctor (who eats Paleo) says that for her, 80/20 means that if she's meeting friends for dinner, and it's a few hours later than she's used to eating, and the only choice on the table is bread, she'll have a piece to not starve before she orders her Paleo-type dinner. For me, that one piece leads to the whole meal.

Mexican food is a huge one for me, and I'm bringing in Tex-Mex for July 4th. I'm ordering fajitas, but I'll only eat the beef & shrimp, with some guacamole (I make it, so I know what's in it!). I have to avoid the rest like the plague. And ooooh, the margaritas that my sister-in-law makes....(slapping hand right now).

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-01T17:52:37+0300

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34805>

Mexican food is still on the menu. I can get the meat and vegetables with salsa, guacamole and sometimes sour cream. Hold the rice, beans, chips, margeriita, and lager. I just wish the local restaurant had fish and shrimp options instead of only beef, chicken, or pork.

### janagram — 2012-07-01T18:45:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34810>

Well, last night was my art opening and another party...!!! I had a couple of glasses of wine. But. Zero grains.....(yay me!) but then the chocolate got to me: I nibbled a super thin slice of flourless chocolate cake (from trader joe's) with friends at a table. Hey...there's quite a bit of sugar in that! Didn't feel so good this morning. Gained 2 lbs. Now I'm gulping coffee.....just to get the fuzz outta my head...

This type of slip up is becoming extremly rare in my life, and will become extinct!

### fitness@home — 2012-07-01T18:52:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34814>

Johnnyb, I am lucky in that our local Mexican restaurant (right in the neighborhood!) offers seafood fajitas. We order fajitas for two and I eat the seafood, pico de gallo, guacamole, and veggies. Husband utilizes everything I won't eat - rice, bean, tortillas...

My problem is the Friday night "decompression" drink we get with the meal. They also make wonderful top shelf margaritas - only need one. Tart, not sweet...just the way I like them with freshly squeezed lime juice.

### janagram — 2012-07-01T19:01:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34817>

# Thread: good reason to get cold adapted
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-reason-to-get-cold-adapted.2123/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-30T17:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-reason-to-get-cold-adapted.2123/post-34602>

[https://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=cold-heart-attacks](http://https://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=cold-heart-attacks)

Temperature Drops Put the Squeeze on Heart Attack Risk

A slight daily mean temperature decline can increase the number of heart attacks for up to a month, new research shows

> The cold facts
>
> Despite perennial tales about snow shoveling-induced heart attacks, there seems to be more to the cold story than physical exertion. **Researchers are still parsing out the precise physiologic effects of cold on the cardiovascular system, but some controlled lab studies run on volunteers have provided clues. These lab tests have shown that when the ambient temperature drops, blood viscosity increases, blood pressure changes, and the heart has to work harder, likely increasing the risk for clots and ultimately a heart attack.
>
>
>
> Although overexertion and deep freezes do seem to contribute to heart attack risk, the new data suggests that **overall temperature matters less than its variation. In other words, Bhaskaran explains, there did not seem to be a chilly threshold temperature after which the one-degree rule took hold. That means that even if the mean daily temperature is 10 degrees C one day, 9 the next, and 10 again the following day, there are still likely to be an extra 200 heart attacks during the next month.
>
>
>
> The striking lag time, which tended to be about two weeks after a temperature drop, although some** extended to 28 days,** seem to be more pronounced with cold weather heart troubles than in those spurred by heat waves. But "why that is, we're not entirely sure," Bhaskaran says. The cold might simply start broader changes in the body that can eventually lead to a heart attack, he suggests.

### Joann — 2012-06-30T21:07:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-reason-to-get-cold-adapted.2123/post-34648>

More reason to eat FISH or seafood in winter!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-30T22:29:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-reason-to-get-cold-adapted.2123/post-34669>

> **chocolate;34762 wrote:** [https://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=cold-heart-attacks](http://https://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=cold-heart-attacks)
>
>
>
> Temperature Drops Put the Squeeze on Heart Attack Risk
>
>
>
> A slight daily mean temperature decline can increase the number of heart attacks for up to a month, new research shows

Perfect example of a good thing ( CT) being studied in a warm adapted diet........and the real data being lost. Horrible study

Thanks for your comment Souldanzer !

I'm red-haired with light skin & lots of freckles but I don't get easily burn and this is the first year I see my skin lightly suntan. Before that I was avoiding the sun like the plague & got easily sunburnt.

Unluckily I just have the regular CRP (which is ok) and not DHEA level. Sleep seems ok, I get tired around 10pm but I can't go to bed at this time everyday for social reasons. I'm the first awaken in the morning (around 7am), no matter what time I went to bed the day before.

T4 Free : 11.6 pmol/L

T4 free : 0.9 ng/dl

TSH : 1.51

(this is only what I got for hormones)

I definitely eat a paleo diet, basically meat, fish & veggies (I like home made veggies juices) with lots of coconut oil. My favourite fat is CO & I use red palm oil also. I totally cut out dairy 2 weeks ago as I feel crappy everytime I eat cheese and can't help overeating them. I love apples & berries -it's the season!!. My diet is quite simple : egg yolks fried in coconut oils, juices (carrot, cabbage, fennel, ginger, lettuce,..), meats like pork chop, steak, lamb chop, chicken wings + stews, salmon, sardines and other available seafood (2-3 times a week). Nuts are rare. I eat chocolate 100% -pure cocoa! No sugar! Saveurs & Nature brand or Moulin des Moines. I'm lucky to live in Belgium- I don't need lots of variety & feel fine with that.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-06T06:15:19+0300

Your thyroid needs some help and is surely not optimized. FT3 & FT4 (assuming one of those FT4s are FT#) both should be in the top of the range so you are too low. You probably should get TPO and TgAb antibody tests to check for Hashimotos.

I take BioMulsion D for my vitamin D. I need to test in the next month to see how it is raising the D. My last test (oh my I hate to admit this) was 50 for D but I realized after I got the results that after supplementing 20,000 units/day I was supplementing with Vitamin E! The company shipped the wrong item and my eyesite is so bad and I don't wear my glasses when I take my supps. I check every supplement order now! \*hiding head in embarrassment\*

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-07T22:51:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-to-understand-labs.2124/post-36133>

Surprisingly the Vit E was just OK. So I'm still supplementing just not quite as much!

Also forgot to say that your thyroid issues could stem from an underlying adrenal problem. If you can get a saliva cortisol test I would definitely do that. If you do have adrenal dysfunction showing up in your results it's best to treat that first and maybe your thyroid will come around.

# Thread: JohnnyB's Life Optimizing Goals
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### johnnyb — 2012-06-30T20:47:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-34642>

I never really introduced myself. I have experiences to share and questions to explore. I figure starting here will get this done even if only a little at a time....

A couple years ago I decided I was tired of being obese and didn't want to see my blood pressure slowly keep creeping up. I started bringing lunch to work but it was still SAD. That didn't work very well. I started low carb Atkins style. Joe Branca on this forum suggested Paleo eating and Mark's Daily Apple and other sites. Primal eating has been a very good thing. Later I made it here and to Leptin Rx and an optimal journey.

### johnnyb — 2012-07-05T03:43:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-35579>

I was diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnea a few years ago. Here are a few quotes from Dr. K's blogs...

> People with sleep apnea have some of the lowest levels of growth hormone measured and this helps partially explain why most of them have body composition issues.
>
> > The normal large circadian prolactin surge we should see at around midnight, after leptin enters the brain, does not happen if the patient has leptin resistance, sleep apnea, or has eaten food too close (within 3-4 hours) to bedtime
> > Cognitive decline is obvious with sleep deprivation, and learning is impaired. Sleep apnea is a known killer in humans and generally appears in those with obesity, again linking energy metabolism to sleep.
>
> Sometimes I think sleep should be levee #1 of the quilt and not #28. Of course, that's only because sleep issues are a huge mismatch for me. I'm surprised apnea doesn't come up more here.
>
>
>
> Would you know if you have apnea? I didn't. I was a snorer, but snoring may not mean anything, but apnea often means snoring. I am a slow, steady breather so "breathing pauses" are tuff to spot. Even now my CPAP can think I'm taking to long to breath even when I'm awake and fine relaxing as I go to sleep. I can pass an PulseOx test staying above 95% O2 saturation even with 60 apnea events an hour. Sleep Doc says my brain is very good at waking me up. DW says she never heard me make gasping for air sounds. It was the cognitive decline and general lack of patience and mental ability that drove me to the doctor. One major symptom I totally missed was waking at night. I started waking more nights then not. I easily dismissed this as a need to urinate or stress as my mind would start reeling with yesterdays problems. I often ended up sleeping in an easy chair downstairs. Within 2 days of CPAP treatment that stopped. I didn't realize how much sleep deprivation builds up over time. Bedtime meant nothing. I was usually stupid with the need for sleep long before bed. After sleeping well now I can pretty much spot a bad nights sleep before my head leaves the pillow in the morning.
>
>
>
> If you are leptin resistant for any reason, have sleep apnea, you will always have an altered body composition because of a low GH level and an altered sex steroid profiles on testing. The reason is because DHEA is the immediate precursor for those hormones and is always low in people with bad sleep efficiency. Most VLCers who are warm adapted face this very problem today.

This is a very interesting quote as well as the recent DHEA blog. I need to work out what to test and where to optimize next. I think I have DW on board for education consults for each of us. Too bad I didn't get her onboard last weekend for the 2 for 1 steal, I mean deal.

### chocolate — 2012-07-05T04:29:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-35581>

My apnea got better with the leptin reset and a mouthpiece. I bet the dhea would kick butt.

### johnnyb — 2012-07-05T05:28:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-35587>

I had lost a good 60lbs by the time of my last sleep Doc visit. They said keep up the good work. I may be a candidate for a mouthpiece next time. I was pretty disappointed to be told I had apnea from being fat and still having it after so much weight loss. My brother has OSD too. They visit later this month. I'm hoping to get him interested in a more optimal path.

When I was a child and got braces one orthodontist said my jaw needed to be pulled forward. My parents went with the one who only moved my teeth around. I can tell my airway is more open with my jaw forward even a little bit. I'm very hopeful a mouthpiece will work well.

### johnnyb — 2012-07-11T08:21:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-36763>

> **http://jackkruse.com/primal-cortisol-response wrote: You must â€œtightenâ€￾ your retinal exposure to sunlight, because this sharpens our brainâ€™s perception of our circadian cycle of melatonin in the pineal-thyroid neural pathways in the brain.\* When you pay attention to the AM light cycle, it helps tremendously optimizing your appropriate diurnal cortisol levels during the day, and minimizes cortisol production at night. It is critical to avoid living and working in semi-darkness during the day**.

I spend much of my time reading computer screens during the day. Until a few years ago my office was a cave with only a desk lamp on to keep glare down and monitor contrast up. We changed buildings and I scored a windowed office. Looking back that was probably another unintended brick in my road to optimal. Looks like it's time to brighten the office. I stopped wearing my hat for the walk to lunch. Time too lose the sun glasses too.

### kathylu — 2012-07-11T15:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-36787>

Thanks for digging up those quotes. I have OSA too, but I tend to ignore it because I adjusted fairly well to CPAP use. So, I generally feel rested in the morning, though not always. However, my hormone panel is s\*\*t and Dr. K thinks I would benefit from growth hormone injections. Trying to find an anti-aging doctor to get things started. Not trivial when you think about the cost involved and the lack of expertise out there....

### johnnyb — 2012-07-15T06:22:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-37986>

Chickens come from eggs and eggs come from chickens, but which came first. Classic grade school paradox.

We repeatedly do things because it's a habit, and it's a habit because we repeatedly do them. But how to form a new behavior? How to shed the Neolithic paths for optimal ones?

I decide to take a step like eating a brazil nut everyday. Pick up a bag. At one a day they go into the freezer for better keeping. I didn't eat one yesterday, so I forget to eat one today too. Repeat. I'm so clever I put several in my chocolate stash, so I'll grab one right after dinner. That's all good except I don't actually eat chocolate everyday. If only thoughts and intentions were actualizatons. I'm sure before long it will be an old habit too....

Maybe if I start eating mackerel smoothies.

### johnnyb — 2012-07-16T16:02:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-38338>

I didn't get the giveaway consult but I did sign up for the email consult and send in the labs I have and a medical history. I forgot to mention magnesium under on the supplement list. Funny how the mind works. Most supplements are taken with BAB or dinner. Magnesium I try to take avoiding a meal. I complete missed them. I probably should have mentioned increased sleep weirdness too. I keep waking up at 4 or 5 generally heading to bed asap after 10. I slightly increased the CPAP pressure in case its apnea events, not helping so far. I see this especially on nights I got to do a real CT session in the ocean.

### johnnyb — 2012-07-17T16:28:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-38707>

Leptin may be the one hormone to rule them all! but sleep is what I covet. Sleep, my precious, gollum gollum....

My brain ever mindful of the sting of sleep deprivation I value sleep like little else. My sleep, DW's sleep, the neighbors sleep (I despise the overly loud car alarm chirp of one of our cars on a rare night we are out late enough to wake a neighbor). What is one willing to do get good sleep? Go to bed at the same time each night; separate beds; separate bedrooms; lock the pets out, [Ssscat](http://http://contechstayawaymotion-activatedpetdet.blogspot.com/2011/05/innotek-ssscat-cat-training-aid.html) any one? This was true before blue blockers, SCN, melatonin, hormone cascade, autophagy, prolactin surge, HGH release, or any of the other whats, whys and golden nuggets to be mined from the blogs here.

I'm starting to ask myself if the clock is to me what a weight scale is to someone with an ED. If I wake up and it's dark I try to go back to sleep with much success I should add. If I wake for too long I look at the clock which I have to grab and move so I can see it since I have its leds point at the wall. I find I wake quite a bit between 2-2:30 which seems part of the 2 phase sleep pattern I have read about. Waking at 4-4:30 pretty much sucks. That can be ~6 hours of sleep which can be too close to the 7-8 hours of sleep I covet to go back to sleep, but it's also too early for breakfast within an hour of waking. I'd love to go for a walk but would hate to raise cortisol. At this point I think the clock is still a tool and not my nemesis. I guess I'll keep it for now.

Sleep, my precious, you will be mine every night. gollum gollum

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-17T19:32:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-38774>

I'd say don't look at the clock!! If you are waking between 2 and 3 o'clock, it's mostly a problem with cortisol and/or insulin. Looking at the clock and telling yourself you HAVE to go back to sleep will only raise cortisol and make matter worst. You should cover that alarm to make sure it is very dark in your room.

I was having the same problem: waking up in the middle of the night and having difficulties to go back to sleep. I would turn on the light, do some reading, sometimes surf the web. ALL WRONG. Stay in the dark. Do some meditation or listen to calm music, and you'll fall back to sleep.

After testing, I found out that DHEA was in the toilet. I took high dose of DHEA to help me find sleep again, and lowered it after a few days. I'm now sleeping much better. I sometimes wake up in the night to use the bathroom, but I fall right back to sleep.

Have a good day...and a good night sleep

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-17T23:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-38840>

If only thoughts and intentions were actualizatons. I'm sure before long it will be an old habit too....

### johnnyb — 2012-08-01T05:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-42212>

Even with the few number of days since I started with the blue blockers I now sleep all night. I expect that will continue. Covering my head with a T-shirt keeps it dark. The CPAP keeps lots of fresh air under the shirt and the white noise it makes covers the noise of the open window too. Now if only the T-shirt provided cool to my head.

### johnnyb — 2012-08-01T06:38:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-42216>

I had an email consult with Dr. K. reviewing my history and suggesting blogs and tests. Very interesting reading the blogs with the new perspective from his suggestions and the experience of writing up my medical history. Actually writing it up opened my eyes to some stuff like the probability of leaky gut. Not having any (recognized) food intolerance I kinda panned leaky gut. Rereading blogs my medical history is littered with leaky gut red flags.

I met with my PCP to talk over recent issues and to see which of the recommended tests I could get. I like my doc very much but she is a CW doc and my interface to a CW system. Some of the tests are not done by the lab. I'll probably have to get one of the saliva cortisol test done else where. She did order a blood cortisol test and 24 hour urine cortisol test. I'll get at least TPO Ab, TSH and T4 but apparently not T3 or rT3. No vit D because it was normal in Feb. She wasn't going to do the hsCRP because the last two CPR tests were lower then the tests range. She asked why get the hsCRP in my case? I was at a bit of a loss other then its importance as an inflammation marker. She put it on the lab sheet so I'll get it.

I think I learned a key element to getting tests and working with her. One of the criteria is what action do you take based on test results. I didn't really want to explain that if I have a high hsCRP I'll break out a big can of CT woopass. That'll have to wait for a followup. The follow up consult with Dr. will likely clue me in a bit to work better with her too. I'm some what hopeful if I can record the skype session I may get her to listen it or maybe selected important parts of where Dr. K says to head.

### johnnyb — 2012-08-02T06:56:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-42456>

The vampires took 8 or more vials of blood this morning and supplied a large orange vessel for a 24 hour urine test. I hope test results return quickly. One of the tests was fasting so breakfast was late. I figured out how to make some breakfast at work so I didn't have to go back home. I missed bringing lunch since I had planned to make that after breakfast.

Fast food is getting harder to go clean. The food is the same but my awareness and desire for clean food increases. I started to like the occasional Asian food lunch choice after I figured out I could get vegatables as the side instead of rice or noodles and a shrimp entree. The shrimp while looking sauteed on closer inspection have a light flour or starch coating. I wish they have scampi. The soy sauce is crap of course. A friend suggested a hack at the fish taco/buritto place. Sounds like I can go off menu, get a bowl with the fish and some Teriyaki vegatables sans the Teriyaki.

Mothers Market had 6 or more flavors of Kavita to try. The coconut flavor was OK with dinner. I missed the gentle part of "shake gently" but without incident  They had Bubbies pickles and sourkraut and as a bonus their horseradish. The regular store brands all had serveral kinds of crap with them. I do like some horseradish on cold shrimp. Mother even had organic Tamari sauce in the little pouches.

I eliminated night shades a couple days ago in the hopes of seeing if shoulder issues are related. Shrimp at lunch came with bell peppers. Picked them out. I hope thats clean enough. You never know how many tomatoes and peppers you eat until you avoid them. Starting to look at the leaky gut Rx too which reminds me I need to get that them bones in the slow cooker.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-02T17:56:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-42517>

Hi johnnyb! At my favorite sushi place I asked about what they use as a coating for the soft shell crabs. Lo and behold, it's water chestnut flour. Not sure if that's OK but it's not a grain, don't think it's a nightshade but it may not be the most desirable. From what I gleaned on the internet it's basically a water chestnut dried and ground into powder. Used a lot in Japanese and Indian foods. I would guess it is worth the asking!

### johnnyb — 2012-08-04T05:19:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-42807>

Thanks for the feedback/tips Destiny and Patty.

I need to find a recipe or make one for avocado sauce as a mayo replacement to go with tuna and maybe salmon. Maybe CO would be good on tuna if I warm the tuna a bit. I don't care for tuna straight out of a can. Probably too low in fat since I like it quite a bit with mayo, even more with egg, pickle and sometimes a little mustard. Either way I need to keep some tuna cans at work. I'm all for my changing palate but going with the intersection of epi-paleo choice and what I enjoy seems best.

Blood labs all came back "normal". I guess that's better then all @%$#% up, but I want the numbers and ranges for what ever method they use even if I'm not suppose to know how to interpret results. Since I asked they said they will USPS mail me a copy. They be a PDF for a Dr. K consult.

Has CAFO sausage always had MSG? I was at the regular super market to get 6 eggs to get by until the farmers market, and see what they had for seafood for diner that night. The only sausage without MSG was Farmer John brand and that was checking 5 brands worth. I'm glad I was looking to see how much sugar they add. No MSG for me if I know its there. ### johnnyb — 2012-08-25T18:33:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-47036>

I think my doc sees me as healthy and I suppose she's right in a CW mediocre way. It's still too soon how on board she will be with helping me get optimal. No matter how that falls with my doc or how much I learn from Doc K. My optimal health is up to me.

### johnnyb — 2012-09-04T19:53:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-49398>

When the time changed in the spring I started riding my bike to work. That start was soon thwarted with a crash preventing maneuver that injured my knee. Some time and lots of ice later I resumed riding. My initial riding goal was to get more NEAT, Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis, and now to rehab my knee. Ironic that the activity the lead to the injury was also a good one to build up my knee again. I was very concerned about avoiding chronic cardio on the ride. It's a very nice ride. Down hill all the way to work along a river trail, then up the hill to the office. I actually started walking up one short hill/path instead of gearing down and grinding up it. The days I ride into work I generally feel great and ready to take on the world.

So while I though the ride was all about getting increased daily NEAT I now think that is only a bonus. What I'm really getting is a 25 minute morning dose of sun light to entrain my SCN to circadian rhythms. Yup, I get a steaming hot cup of Good Morning! for my brain. I could swear I have read get morning light to increase your morning cortisol and also get morning light to reduce morning cortisol. I suppose the answer is entrain the brain, get the correct amount of cortisol at the correct time of day. Which reminds me, that ASI should be coming back this week....

### johnnyb — 2012-09-08T08:07:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-50204>

ZRT labs results came back. The good news they appear very "normal". Vit D was normal but not high in the range like I hoped. Gotta get consult going. I'm happy that I most likely need to optimize from mediocre and not some of the suspect issue like Hashis. That's 2 TPO negative results.

### johnnyb — 2012-09-19T06:31:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/johnnybs-life-optimizing-goals.2125/post-52640>

So much goes on and I don't make time to write......

I finally had my consult with Jack. He really gives a rip about each person. I figured he would have a high level of competency and professionalism but he really is trying to put statfish back in the water even one at a time.

I got to hear him say "you are an easy case". The short version is I'm a functional hypothyroid. I have mild pregnenolone steal and mild andropause. All in all I'm pretty happy. He even had a compliment about one of my Doc choice of labs. I think he said, good she's a thinker. My labs were odd and constrained by what the medical group's lab offers supplemented by ones I got done. It will be interesting to see how my optimal desires play out with a good CW Doc.

I scored a bunch of size large tshirts to replace the XLs I'm starting to swim in. Some with very retro themes. DW had saved a bunch of my shirts that I became far too obese to fit. Its almost like a new wardrobe. Thanks honey! Now lot of clothes that are too big are going bye bye. Those clothes will **never** fit again.

I scored lots of seaweed both dried and fresh, and a jar of oysters at Hmart. The white kimchee is good which is convenient given they only had it in a 1.6kg jar. It took 2 days to get it all home in my back pack from work. I scored it on a lunch run to Hmart and I have been riding my bike to work.

Life is good.

# Thread: Beach outing ideas
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-06-30T20:56:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/post-34644>

I know this might be a dumb question, but it's often very hard for me to think outside the box--I'm willing to try new things, but don't always know what my options are.

I'm hoping to go on a beach outing in a couple of weeks; we'll be there all day. I'll have a cooler, and what I always did pre-paleo was to pack some Subway sandwiches (they seemed to hold up pretty well). I'm not at the point right now where I go without eating lunch because I still get hungry (wish I could...I \*HATE\* dealing with lunch!), so I have to plan for a leptin/paleo-friendly lunch.

Any ideas for things that can last with limited cooling?

### janagram — 2012-06-30T21:06:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/post-34647>

hardboiled eggs and even some cooked meats are fine...like salami, bacon, prosciutto,mustard, dill pickles, (leave the mayo at home) and then, since it's midsummerish...perhaps a banana? Oh did I forget cheese? If you can have it....I stick to goat or sheep cheese, slices of manchego or petite basque (costco)...but most of all...have fun!

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-30T21:16:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/post-34652>

You could make yourself a Paleo salad. Buy the organic pre-washed greens, and whatever else you like in a salad. Keep in separate plastic containers. Bring some crabmeat (you can find it in the deli section; make sure it's just crabmeat), and some CO for dressing, which you can put in a small plastic container.

Pre-Paleo, I did the Subway sandwich thing too, and then I switched to their salads. Since I know they're not using organic, plus their dressings aren't good, I'd bring my own and assemble it.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-07-01T00:13:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/post-34684>

A few years ago I got my daughter a Chinese Lunch Kit. I can tell you it caused social problems with her peers at school! I never heard the end of it.

Nevertheless, I love the simplicity of these stainless steel kits and use them all the time for camping and road trips. Here are a few pictures. You could fill the containers with what ever you please.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/34844=293-chinese-lunch-kit.jpg)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/34844=291-lunch-kit-1.jpg)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/34844=292-lunch-kit-2.jpg)

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-01T17:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/post-34790>

When I go ski I bring pre-cooked burgers, pemmican and coconut oil. A baggie with a few raw veggies. Late in the season the car gets hot some days and they all do well in a cooler. You could bring canned tuna if you're weary. But I'm super simple and don't care much as long as it's protein and fat

### cookworm — 2012-07-01T19:30:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/post-34827>

### cookworm — 2012-07-01T19:30:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/post-34829>

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-01T21:45:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beach-outing-ideas.2126/post-34858>

I like mixing up some canned salmon or sardines with some dijon mustard & maybe some dill relish. Then you could eat it with some celery or cucumber slices.

# Thread: Hypercholesterolemia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercholesterolemia.2128/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-06-30T21:13:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercholesterolemia.2128/post-34651>

What is it, really?

I've had high cholesterol since I was a teen and my ED took control of my life. I've known for a long time that is co-occurs with anorexia and even if you fix the anorexia, most are still stuck with high cholesterol for life.

I just got diagnosed with heart disease in probably more than one way and my cardiologist made sure to put it all over my medical records in BIG LETTERS. My last internal med doc suggested statins but then said that wouldn't be so good for a woman of child-bearing age.

I looked at my own numbers and they don't look so scary to me at this point:

Total 281

HDL 106

LDL 163

VLDL 12

Trigs 61

HS-CRP 0.47 (was drawn a couple months after the lipid panel though)

This was a non-fasting blood draw which I think affects at least trigs result. My ratios are fine.

Am I looking at a problem here or is this just CW threatening?

What are acceptable or good cholesterol numbers.... you don't want them too low as well!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-01T03:30:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercholesterolemia.2128/post-34719>

> RESULTANT HORMONE CASCADE
>
>
>
> Leptin resistance occurs first. Then insulin resistance happens next. This eventually leads to adrenal resistance. Cortisol is the stress hormone that allows for fight or flight syndrome (life or death). This is the hormone that allows you to run away from a hungry lion fast and live. **Evolution always makes sure cortisol production stays ready for action at the expense of the other hormones that also are made from the same precursors. That precursor is pregnenolone. Pregnenolone is made from cholesterol, and cholesterol is made from LDL. So anytime the body is stressed or inflamed, it up-regulates cholesterol production to make more lifesaving hormones. It requires T3 and vitamin A as cofactors to complete this step. Blocking cholesterol production will increase cellular stress. This is why biochemically, to me, no statin drugs have ever made any sense under any circumstance in medicine. Moreover, this is why there is a chronic association of cancer to low cholesterol levels in the literature. In times of infection or stress, LDL levels always need to rise to protect the cell.
>
>
>
> A diet should provide a substrate of animal protein to sustain the hormone substrates. This is why a Paleo diet is ideal for hormone health because it provides ample substrate for all hormones. A Paleolithic diet is best for longevity in my opinion. It supports all of our hormones that are present in abundance in youth and health. Many other physicians, like Dr. Ron Rosedale, tell folks that protein stimulates the mTOR pathway which leads to death. The real issue I have there is that he gives no context to the statement. Here is my context. **His belief is only true if the background cellular terroir has a high HS CRP back drop, and if your gut is leaky as shown by a low HDL level. If your HS CRP is treated aggressively with a Paleo diet, your HS CRP will be low and your HDL will be high, and then a moderate protein diet is protective.** So I go hard after HS CRP in all of the patients I work with. It is a staple in my practice. Once solved, it is hard-core Paleolithic Robb Wolf style advice to affect the most important part of our biology.
>
>
>
> HORMONES. So anytime cellular stress is high (high HS CRP), it also forces all the hormone backbone substrate called pregnenolone to be shunted to cortisol production. This is called pregnenolone steal syndrome. What exactly does this mean, Doc? DHEA, Androstenedione, Vitamin D, testosterone, estrogen, and aldosterone production all fall dramatically. These are all of the hormones that are made from a common precursor. Remember that chronic Leptin resistance leads to huge hypercortisolism all the time!** NEVER FORGET THIS BIOLOGIC FACT! So that means we need to understand well what high cortisol does to the cellular terroir as well. (Levee 1)

I get that my elevated LDL means I still have inflammation/stress. However, my pregnenolone tested at upper end of range..... so that would mean I'm actually converting my LDL to the precursor. Am I understanding right that this conversion requires T3 and vitamin A? So.... that would mean my thyroid isn't necessarily in such bad shape?

Now, my DHEA is sorta low, as all other hormones, so therefore the pregnenolone has to go somewhere else.... and the only thing I didn't test was salivary cortisol. I sent this one in a week ago and am waiting for results.

Next, low HDL means leaky gut, but my HDL is high. Yet I have a leaky gut according to my low DHEA (learned that from Dr K). But not too bad, b/c preg is still pretty good. Yet even my hs CRP isn't all terrible in itself. I know it needs to improve.... I will ask for a lipid panel with my next hopefully more useful doc on Tuesday and will ask to include this measure.

So I guess my conclusion is my high LDL is from a leaky gut that's not too bad which sends my cortisol haywire. D used to be low but is fixed now with supplementation. T is a bit low, and I have no idea about aldosterone. E looked fine to me but my P/E ratio was estrogen dominant.

Therefore, my main concern would be my "not too bad" leaky gut.... correct? (yes, besides the other minor issues like fixing my brain and heart.... can't hurt to look ahead a bit ) And a leaky gut implies LR by definition, correct? But if I was entirely LR I shouldn't be converting my LDL to preg b/c my thyroid would then be offline.... which it should be anyways b/c of AN. So how AN am I exactly at this point?

### JanSz — 2012-07-01T18:15:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercholesterolemia.2128/post-34804>

Get DHEAs, pregnenolone and cortisol to (very) good levels.

What is the particle size of your LDL.

\====

big fluffy ----good

or

small like bullets

...

### Lyndra — 2012-07-01T18:40:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercholesterolemia.2128/post-34808>

Hmm, yours labs are similar to mine. I'm thinking my LDL is high because of poor thyroid function. You need T3 and vitamin A to convert LDL to hormones, so if T3 is lacking, your LDL won't get converted.

There are ways to test for your aldosterone level - check out stopthethyroidmadness.com. You can check your blood pressure lying down and then immediately after standing up. Also check the responsiveness of your pupils in total darkness by holding a flashlight to the side of yours eyes to see whether your pupils adjust or keep flickering. Symptoms of low aldosterone (which I have) include feeling lightheaded when you get up from sitting or bending over.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-01T22:19:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercholesterolemia.2128/post-34865>

> big fluffy ----good
>
> or

> small like bullets
>

>
>

Thanks, Jan. I'm needing another round of testing I'm realizing. I just sent in my salivary cortisol and waiting for results. Haven't done the particle size testing but I'm pretty sure doc will do it now due to changed circumstances..... otherwise, I'll order it myself. Will be back to this thread once I get this organized.

I think I actually pushed the DHEA up too much for a little while... sleep went bye bye after being great for weeks. Now backed down and I'm sleeping again.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-01T22:26:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercholesterolemia.2128/post-34866>

> **Lyndra;34968 wrote:** Hmm, yours labs are similar to mine. I'm thinking my LDL is high because of poor thyroid function. You need T3 and vitamin A to convert LDL to hormones, so if T3 is lacking, your LDL won't get converted.
>
>
>
> There are ways to test for your aldosterone level - check out stopthethyroidmadness.com. You can check your blood pressure lying down and then immediately after standing up. Also check the responsiveness of your pupils in total darkness by holding a flashlight to the side of yours eyes to see whether your pupils adjust or keep flickering. Symptoms of low aldosterone (which I have) include feeling lightheaded when you get up from sitting or bending over.

I pass all of those tests on a normal day. Every once in a while I get light headed getting up but it's getting more rare. I get checked for orthostatic hypotension every time I see a doc (no matter which one out of my collection) b/c of my history it's standard for me - I've never failed it in the office (but then I'm so nervous there....). I used to have to be careful getting up but now I don't even remember and just up and go. Goes well most of the time.

How's your pregnenolone, lyndra? B/c mine was rather unexpectedly high. Which actually would mean I \*do\* convert and must have at least moderate amount of T3, no? I think my problem is somewhere else..... all that preg probably goes to cortisol? Which should shut off my thyroid and stop conversion but that's not what my labs are saying.

I'll put the aldosterone on my list for next round of testing.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-01T22:30:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypercholesterolemia.2128/post-34868>

The other thing that came to me this morning (while raiding Lafayette's Vitamin Cottage of their entire supply of Artisana and unadulterated coconut milk ) .... AN is a disease of hypercortisolism, which then would set one up for elevated cholesterol... that's why all AN's have high cholesterol!!!! Bingo... that took a while to register!!

Now why doesn't it resolve with remission of AN? B/c there's still inflammation going on that was caused by AN? Or b/c people never fix the underlying leaky gut that pretty much every ED person has?

And can my cortisol even still be that high b/c AN is in remission.... and I'm not just following CW meal plans from my nutritionist like some others do.... I eat on my own, I eat without counting calories?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-01T19:45:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-i-do-with-23andme-results.2130/post-34833>

I copied these sites from another thread so I could find them. Haven't had time to investigate any of them but hopefully they help.

[http://www.mthfrsupport.com/helpful-links.html](http://http://www.mthfrsupport.com/helpful-links.html)

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-07-04T05:04:32+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-04T15:08:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-i-do-with-23andme-results.2130/post-35457>

Husband and I both got back our 23andme results. AND snagged a 4th of July consult while the gettin was good. Before getting the consult, I loved loved having the 23andme stuff . . . but I too don't know that I would have made the correct inferences from the data. It was great to see I did not have some things (alzheimers and bc genes). When Jack looked at the results and helped, wow - he was able to tell me some things I did not realize. Same for my Husband. . it turned 'pay attention to light' into a theory that he "tries" to do to something that if he does most likely will increase the quality and span of his life. THAT was worth it.

I found out that I have a super sensitive and great for adaptation gut. I totally had thought I was not a leaky gut person. I've never had any digestion issues and SEEM to be able to eat any types of foods. SO now I have a whole new batch of things to consider.

Another great rabbit hole for me to start exploring was Genetic BMI, I need to research more on this - but perhaps knowing will help me be more content at a certain weight rather than fighting to lose down.

# Thread: CT tub placement dilemma
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-tub-placement-dilemma.2132/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-06-30T23:49:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-tub-placement-dilemma.2132/post-34681>

Right now I have a tub on a small landing paved with brick. I just got a great new umbrella fabric (umbrella broke in a windstorm). My plan is to rig up a pulley system amongst the trees, so I can have some shade for the CT session. Then I can bring the fabric down over the tub when I am not using it. Right now I cover the tub with an old window between uses but it gets too hot. I really would like some shade for morning CT, and some protection for the tub, but would I also miss all the nature? I have some time for this, because right now, the tub heats up so much that it is a one-use deal with cold tapwater and then watering the plants. In the winter I would like to not have to replace the water so often. An alternative is to move the tub to a shady part, but more in view of the neighbors. What would you do?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-01T17:26:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-tub-placement-dilemma.2132/post-34788>

Maybe some sort of reflective covering like you use in car windows?

### NakedToTheWind — 2012-07-01T19:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-tub-placement-dilemma.2132/post-34834>

There is a material that I have used for similar purposes. It is available at Home Depot for about seven dollars per sheet of 8 foot by 4 foot size. It comes with a reflective aluminized mylar on one side. It's mass is only a few ounces per sheet. It can be cut to various sizes with just a little box cutter knife. An insulating light weight box to cover your pool could be constructed from this material using nothing more than duct tape to join the pieces together.

[http://www.insulfoam.com/](http://http://www.insulfoam.com/)

### Birdy — 2012-07-02T07:42:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-tub-placement-dilemma.2132/post-34965>

hmmmm, maybe I'll switch to some sort of foam. Just that it looks ugly.

### NakedToTheWind — 2012-07-02T19:33:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-tub-placement-dilemma.2132/post-35063>

> **Birdy;35125 wrote:** hmmmm, maybe I'll switch to some sort of foam. Just that it looks ugly.

The white side with the blue writing would not show in an application such as you would need to construct. You would have to put the silver side out to reflect the sun. It would then look like a UFO had landed on your pool. And even more so if you took care to put the joining tape on the inside surface or used metal aluminum tape on the outside. The whole thing would be easy to move by the weakest people but not strong enough to stand or sit on unless you used the thickest panels.

# Thread: All night shivering after ocean CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-night-shivering-after-ocean-ct.2133/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Caroline Cooper — 2012-07-01T00:29:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-night-shivering-after-ocean-ct.2133/post-34685>

Hi Friends,

I have a question about CT. I am new to CT and had my first ocean experience. I did about 20 minutes in the water. I don't know what the water temperature was but it didn't feel horribly cold to me at the time. I have been doing tub CT at about a temperature of 16C(62F).

My temperature after the ocean was 34.5C(94.1F). Yes, that is right. I felt find and my temperature was back to a low normal temperature (36.5C/97.7F) by bedtime. But during the night I had a really tough time. I had a series of shivering fits, making it difficult to sleep. The shivering fit was centered around my kidney area. I was in a tent and during the night I put on a jacket under my sleeping bag but I was still cold and shivering most of the night.

Does anyone know what causing this type of shivering fit centered around the kidney area? Is this just normal and I got colder then I thought?

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-07-06T03:17:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-night-shivering-after-ocean-ct.2133/post-35766>

I have no idea, Caroline...surprised no one has chimed in on your problem.

Bump

### msclman — 2012-07-11T20:14:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-night-shivering-after-ocean-ct.2133/post-36893>

i could be wrong but hypothermia is below 95 degrees no? i try not to go below it. i do 60 - 80 minutes in 53 degree water and have serious shivering for about an hour but its not localized and does go away. I would post the question in the "ask jack" section.

### Destiny — 2012-07-12T15:11:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-night-shivering-after-ocean-ct.2133/post-37129>

> **msclman;37059 wrote:** i could be wrong but hypothermia is below 95 degrees no? i try not to go below it. i do 60 - 80 minutes in 53 degree water and have serious shivering for about an hour but its not localized and does go away. I would post the question in the "ask jack" section.

It was answered by "ask Jack" on 7/1 there. See below:

My bet is you might have a thyroid issue that is co-morbid......hard to say with this limited data.

### growltgc — 2012-07-23T21:39:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-night-shivering-after-ocean-ct.2133/post-40434>

the first time I did ocean swim (firs time any type of CT) I shivered the longest. I shivered ~20 minutes after a 20 minute swim. All the times afterwards I have only shivered for 10 minutes or so. I'm not sure why there was this variation.

I probably reduce the shivering time a lot by laying in the sand. I lay completely in the same and flip over once or twice to new hot sand, then the shivering is gone. And the air temp where I swim has been 62-68 F. the water temp 53-59 F

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-24T20:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-night-shivering-after-ocean-ct.2133/post-40757>

When I started I had random shivering up to 12 hours after my baths in 53F.

It didn't last, and I don't do that now.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34714>

Thought I'd put this out here as I'm trying to understand.... (August I'm joining you in your quest )

**My question:

After all this talk about thyroid hormone replacement during the webinar I'm a bit confused. Can we still avoid having to deal with all this fine tuning by using cold? In one of the blogs it was explained that you can bypass the thyroid and theoretically live without one if you're cold enough.

**Dr K's answer:

You need to go back and re listen to the Webinar and then re read the Hormone 101 blog......if cortisol/inflammation is an issue you might need BHRT long term. I not you wont. The answers are there and if your still confused there is always your doc to ask.

My doc's are a little useless so I'll ask you guys since you're all experts by now

> THYROID TOO?
>
>
>
> ALL REUNITE (MORE GEEKY THOUGH): THE WARM ADAPTED STORY of metabolism and biochemistry YOU HEAR REGURGITATED EVERYWHERE ON THE NET:
>
>
>
> Once leptin enters and binds to its receptors, it effects the lateral hypothalamic tracts to immediately send a second messenger signal to the thyroid to signal it to up-regulate thyroid function and efficiency. See whne we are warm adapted it require help to partially access this pathway from T3 hormone. If you are LR you never can access this pathway. This specifically is how we can raise our basal metabolic rate when we are leptin sensitive. These coupled events, matched with leptinâ€™s actions peripherally in muscles, occur at the UCP3 sitesâ€¦â€¦to burn fat as we sleep at a higher basal metabolic rate. This means electron chain transport does not make ATP as usual. When leptin allows this uncoupling to occur we make heat and not energy from normal metabolism. This means we will burn off our excess calories as pure heat. This is one reason why calories in and calories out argument makes no biologic sense once you understand how leptin works. Humans are built to burn fat at night as we sleep to loose excess weight we donâ€™t need. This is our modern day equivalent of hibernation. Out big fat amazing human brain got rid of winter sleep and took over a two hour window during sleep to replace it. CT-7 covers the gorgeous symphony of human circadian biology. Since it shrunk our risk of mismatches has risen exponentially. This is how your brain or feelings, can undercut you, when you are unaware of what a sped up epigenetics meant to human mammalian biochemistry. SKEPTICS: You wanted data, and now I am killing you with it.
>
>
>
> THE COLD ADAPTED HUMAN THYROID FXN: does not bother with T3 at all. Why? When you are supremely LS by cold you go straight the the source, the hypothalamus and make TRH from the brain. The brain controls all thyroid function in coldâ€¦â€¦.forget the Moose thyroid.
>
>
>
> SKEPTIC BOMB: You bypass all hormones and TSH too. TRH drives the whole show. The brain is completely in control and it up regualtes fat burning everywhere. This is how the Ancient Pathway lights your pilot light. The warm adapted human always complains about the cold and always feels coldâ€¦â€¦the cold adapted on is always pink to cherry in cold radiating heat like a furnace. You can thank TRH for this. This is does not even require a thyroid gland either. Is not life grand in the cold, folks?

**

> We have already established in earlier blogs that inflammation causes Leptin resistance. Leptin controls all energy production in the body. Inflammation stops T4 to T3 conversion in the liver and abruptly turns off your thyroidâ€™s ability to function properly despite normal thyroid labs.

**/>

> The high cortisol also directly affects the Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis too. Cortisol directly blocks 5′ deiodinase enzyme that converts T4 to T3 (this occurs in the liver). Cortisol Releasing Hormone (CRH) (seen elevated in high cortisol production states) directly blocks TSH. The implication is huge because Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH) and T3, the active thyroid hormone, are inhibited quickly in this process. Immediately, any excess T4 is then shunted to reverse T3. Reverse T3 is a COMPETITIVE inhibitor to T3, the active thyroid hormone. This basically turns off the thyroid!

So I guess the issue is inflammation and if I am understanding correctly, even if you're entirely cold-adapted, you can still have inflammation and that would prevent you from being "supremely" LS, since inflammation causes LR by definition. So therefore, you can be cold adapted and not LS, and that means you still need your thyroid BHRT fix despite it all?

What would cause someone to still have inflammation for the rest of their lives despite doing everything optimal?

And, using hs CRP as a marker for inflammation, what's the desired level for the cold thyroid bypassing to happen? Or LDL as that is another marker of inflammation as I'm understanding.

And then, at that point, does it even \*matter\*?? Since you're already supremely LS, your cortisol isn't running amok, and not blocking conversion of T4 to T3 and CRH isn't blocking TSH. So everything should be optimal no matter if cold-adapted or not, summer and winter.

So really, this would mean that this is a good thing for folks who have a damaged or missing thyroid for whatever reason but not b/c of LR, but for those who have other issues that would still cause inflammation for the rest of their lives, it would not matter at all? They will be stuck with BHRT for their thyroids no matter if cold or warm.

Sorry if I'm being dense here. The brain is actually cooperating a bit for a change today, so maybe I should be getting this on my own.....

### Lyndra — 2012-07-01T18:44:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34809>

I did CT for 2-1/2 months and didn't feel like I improved a lot. My overall cold tolerance got a little better, but by the 25-30 minute mark at 50 degrees, I would start shivering and feel really cold. I was forcing myself to stay in. Post-CT warm up took a looooooong time.

My strategy now is to improve adrenal & thyroid function and then ease back into CT. Hopefully that can happen before the summer is over, as I'd love to start taking cold showers and then adapt myself naturally as the water temperature decreases into fall & winter.

### AKMan — 2012-07-01T20:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34836>

There are quite a few animal studies out there where they would destroy an animal's thyroid and then subject it to cold. In most cases, the animal wouldn't survive for long. However, if they 'cold-adapted' the animal first, the animal would survive. 'Cold adapting' usually consisted of housing the animal in 50 deg F temps for a week to 10 days.

In human terms, mild hypothyroidism usually presents in subtle ways; weight gain, hair loss, depression, constipation, etc... I've never heard of someone dying of hypothermia where the cause was hypothyroidism. In the animal studies, they used death by exposure as the end-point.

In my own case, I was diagnosed hypothyroid 7 years ago due to high TSH levels, with no real symptoms. After starting CT this year, I quit taking my Synthroid and my TSH, T4, and T3 levels have stayed normal. Prior to CT, I had been taking kelp and brazil nuts and eating a clean paleo mostly keto diet. So, impossible to say if the CT had anything to do with it, but I like to think it did.

My take on this is, if you are hypothyroid and don't have an autoimmune condition (hashi's), supplement with kelp and selenium, eat keto paleo high seafood, CT and test thyroid every 3 months.

I swim in the pool almost everyday to get my head under, I dunk my head in 35 degree water for the pain and crave it, I think a 45 degree bath is easier than a 65 degree one. I do still shiver after but I can do several CTs a day and warm up. And I had entirely no problem skiing in t-shirts and tanks while my beer-bellied ski partner was wrapped up in down jacket, thick wool sweater, scarf and hat.
