### ATL_Paleo — 2012-07-02T04:04:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34934>

PLEASE EXPLAIN THE CONNECTION BETWEEN LDL CHOLESTEROL AND PREGNENOLONE ?

We make our pregnenolone from LDL cholesterol, ie:

LDL cholesterol ---> pregnenolone

When LDL cholesterol is too high, then it's due to either:

a) pregnenolone is too low, cortisol is too low, and despite this: ACTH is also too low even though it should be high (hypothalamus mismanagement)

Ie: the liver enzymes which produce LDL cholesterol are upregulated by too low pregnenolone, but ACTH is too low to ensure the LDL cholesterol is then synthesized into pregnenolone, so LDL cholesterol gets "backed up".

or:

b) Cortisol is low, thus ACTH is high (as it should be), LDL cholesterol is high, pregnenolone is high, but pregnenolone is not synthesizing into cortisol. In this case pregnenolone is "backed up".

Source: [http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/content.php?118-cortisol-boost-101](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/content.php?118-cortisol-boost-101)

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-02T04:13:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34935>

@ALT: Is that a question? LDL gets converted to preg but sometimes that conversion doesn't work well, the conversion needs T3 and vitamin A so if you're having trouble with thyroid you might not have enough T3 to make the conversion

LDL will be high if there is inflammation/stress....

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-04T14:32:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-35445>

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?17834-Heart-Palpitations-on-oral-pregnenolone](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?17834-Heart-Palpitations-on-oral-pregnenolone)

> **Irregular heart rate (eg: palpitations) is a symptom of temporarily too low cortisol in relation to thyroid hormones, and it's severity is determined by the relative difference in cortisol and thyroid hormones.
>
>
>
> When it's triggered exclusively by an increase in dose of supplementatary preg, then it's due to the following process:
>
>
>
> 0) Initially resting metabolic rate is very low, so cortisol levels and thyroid hormones are relatively low - but they are balanced to each other.
>
>
>
> 1) supplementation with preg initially raises preg levels
>
>
>
> 2) adrenals synthesize preg along the cortisol-production-line into cortisol
>
>
>
> 3) cells absorb more cortisol and increase their metabolic rate (good)
>
>
>
> \[when cells increase their metabolic rate, they consume thyroid hormones faster, and they consume cortisol faster\]
>
>
>
> 4) cells consume T3 faster, cells consume cortisol faster
>
>
>
> 5) cells start to invoke their genetic programming which raises their resting metabolic rate, ie: they start to "stockpile" all hormones - especially T4 and cortisol (and they hypothalamus allows serum levels of all hormones to rise) THIS IS THE FUNDAMENTAL GOAL OF THIS EXERCISE
>
>
>
> 6) but when preg dose is too high for the initial T4 levels, cells won't absorb all of the extra cortisol, and some of that extra cortisol stays temporarily in serum, and the hypothalamus detects this as "too high cortsol" and backs off CRH, which tells the pituitary to back off ACTH, which tells the adrenals to back off cortisol production - by more than the amount of additional cortisol being synthesized by the supplementary preg.
>
>
>
> \[ The reason why the hypothalamus over-reacts this way is due to the speed cortisol levels increased. The faster cortisol levels increase, the more harshly the hypothalamus responds by lowering ACTH harshly. Ie: if you had slowed down the rate of absorption of preg, eg: via using transdermal preg, then cortisol would rise more slowly, and the hypothalamus would only react optimally to the excess cortisol \]
>
>
>
> 7) As a result of the hypothalamus' over-reaction, cortisol levels are now temporarily too low
>
>
>
> 8) At this time cells are loaded with more T4 and T3, but they've had their cortisol supply reduced, so they have their thyroid hormones in excess of their cortisol.
>
>
>
>
>
> In Hormone Modulation Therapy 101 I've improved the explanation of this phenomenon as follows:
>
>
>
> Tune in to the following symptoms of excess preg, excess cortisol and excess T3, ie:
>
>
>
> a) Symptoms of preg-is-in-excess-of-T3 (same as T3-is-too-low-compared-to-preg) are slightly spacey feelings - try reducing preg.
>
>
>
> b) Symptoms of T3-is-in-excess-of-cortisol (same as cortisol-is-too-low-compared-to-T3) are awareness of your heart beat / erratic heart beat - this has a complex explanation: it's due to an initial increase in metabolic rate causing an initial boost in cells absorbing both cortisol and thryoid hormones, followed by running low on thyroid hormones, followed by temporary ACTH suppression due to excess cortisol due to excess preg. Result is temporary excess of thyroid hormones in relation to cortisol. Try decreasing preg to reduce the likelihood of ACTH suppression, or switch from oral to TD preg to slow absorption of preg to prevent the excess cortisol.
>
>
>
> c) Symptoms of cortisol-is-in-excess-of-T3 (same as T3-is-too-low-compared-to-cortisol) are temporary excess cortisol causing ACTH suppression, causing too low preg, causing drowsiness and / or the yawns - try reducing preg.**

c) sounds a heck of a lot like what happens post CT to me.... I got 20-30min then I pass out for an hour or two.

b) sounds a heck of a lot like my every-day state no matter if I supp preg or not. I get worse after eating, after exercise, after upset, after anything that would increase my cortisol temporarily..... makes my heart thump even more.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-04T14:35:54+0300

>
>
>

> ) but when preg dose is too high for the initial T4 levels, cells won't absorb all of the extra cortisol, ....

does this relate to not enough T4, so the body can't convert enough to T3 to counterbalance increased cortisol? There wasn't any talk of T4 before. To really it's a too low T3 b/c there isn't enough T4??

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-05T17:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-35629>

Soul - thanks for posting this.

The other day I took a minimal thyroid dose in the morning in preparation for thyroid labs that afternoon. I took my regular amount of pregnenelone, and noticed that afternoon the weird spacey feeling described. Now I know what it was (not enough thyroid hormone on board). Tells me I'm still mainly running off of the T3 in Armour, and I will likely do better adding in a little T4. I am now spacing the preg doses and doing them at the same time as my Armour doses. I'm hoping this will help my morning symptoms. That link you posted really helped clarify what is going on when we're attempting to run hormones on manual, so thanks again!

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-05T17:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-35631>

I also wonder if this means cortisol testing does not tell the gland's potential when someone is hypothyroid. That link seems to say that pregnenelone is made to match the level of thyroid hormone available. Less available thyroid hormone = less pregnenelone = less cortisol (but enough to balance out the amount of thyroid hormone, but a slower metabolic rate than optimal) no matter what the adrenal gland is capable of making. I always thought that in the face of hypothyroidism, a (healthy) adrenal response is to upregulate cortisol to cope with the stress of being hypothyroid. I'm not sure now how to assemble my little tidbits of knowledge...

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T18:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-35638>

My guess is it could go both ways possibly.... depending on where your issue is. If your problem starts with low thyroid, I could see how that could lower your cortisol. If your problem starts with HPA, that will affect your thyroid function. You could probably have them both trashed at the same time.

Finding this information really opened my eyes to what might be going on in my body.... whether or not I take preg. And CT would affect all of this, as well. I might have a hole in my heart that's causing all sorts of havoc.... but I'm pretty sure I've got some serious imbalance between HPA and thyroid as well and I'm starting to think that the heart palpitations might be caused by HPA/thyroid dysfunction much more than my PFO.

I dropped preg and DHEA yesterday (but also CT and leptin RX as I decided to have a wtf day) and I slept deeply, woke a couple of times but fell right back asleep. 10pm to 8am. Not bad at all. Not sure how I'm going to go about today yet.

# Thread: one keto adapting athlete's journey
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### pbj27349 — 2012-07-01T14:18:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-34759>

I stumbled upon this interview with Steve Phinney at

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/04/14/steve-phinney-low-carb-preserves-glycogen-better-than-high-carb/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/04/14/steve-phinney-low-carb-preserves-glycogen-better-than-high-carb/)

and was impressed enough by his reasoning that I purchased both of his and Volek's art and science books. I also did a google search for keto adapted athletes and found Barry's thread about fasted state training. In it Jack said

"Im hoping that more keto adapted athletes open up and talk about what they are doing."

Well, I will divulge. I will use this thread as a training/diet journal. I am training for this.

[http://www.setupevents.com/?fuseaction=event\_detail&eventID=2091](http://http://www.setupevents.com/?fuseaction=event_detail&eventID=2091)

I have done this race a few years ago. My time was 50 minutes. My goal for this one is to get under 40 min. I started keto adapting 23 May 2012. I am keto adapting because of all of the positive health effects associated with said state, and if keto adaptation enables me to perform better in the aquathlon then I will be a convert striving to stay in ketosis for life.

I am almost done with my mug of plain black coffee, so now it is time for a bowel movement and then a workout (in a fasted state of course). I'll check back in after the workout while I break my fast.

Peace,

Paul (in Snow Camp NC)

### Lyndra — 2012-07-01T18:49:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-34812>

Cool. I'll be interested in hearing how that works for you. I've read about a number of people using a keto diet for ultra events but haven't heard much about people using it for shorter duration events. Good luck and let us know how it goes!

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-01T18:52:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-34813>

This morning's workout out in my front yard done barefoot went as follows:

parallel bar dips supersetted with full close grip pullups

(1+1/:15) \* 79 for a total time of 19:52. That amounts to 79+79/19:52.

The dips were done with a controlled descent down to below the upper arms parallel with the ground like this

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WYwiTxnWtho\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;WYwiTxnWtho\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WYwiTxnWtho\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;WYwiTxnWtho)

I keep my knees tucked slightly forward throughout the rep and my head is forward so that I am looking down at my knees throughout as well. I use a little forward knee kip to get through the sticking point.

The pullups are started from a full dead hang and I touch my chest to my thumb knuckles at the top. I use a little knee kip to get all the way up. The descent is controlled. The full range of motion was inspired by

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XvM3JYC--hM&feature=related\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;XvM3JYC--hM\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XvM3JYC--hM&feature=related\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;XvM3JYC--hM)

Sun Yang has a long, clean, powerful stroke (no bubbles and only 27 strokes per 50m). I am aspiring to that.

This workout was better than the previous one because I lasted longer going at the same pace (17:19 vs 19:52).

After catching my breath and drinking some water, I proceeded to the next part of the workout which was:

jumping alternating lunges supersetted with jumping deadlifts

(8+8/:25) \* 32 for a total time of 13:13. That amounts to 254+254/13:13 (I only did 6+6 for the last superset).

I count the back knee touches to the ground for the lunges and hold my arms out to the sides for balance.

I touch the ground at the bottom of each jumping deadlift rep. Actually, I call these jumping toe touches. As I go up, I swing my arms up out to the sides to use my whole body to get me some air time.

This workout was better than the previous one (10:43 vs 13:13)

Ideally, I would like to have my total times for each section of the session be between 20 and 30 minutes, but I recently increased the pace for both sections and dropped the total times below 20. So, I can stay with these paces for a few more workouts.

These supersetting workouts are my eggs and butter workouts because they are convenient. I can do them at home first thing in the morning to take advantage of the fasted training effects. I will have to do some 1500m swims and 5k runs for specificity, but these home workouts enable me to simulate race demands more frequently.

Speaking of eggs and butter, I ate 8 home grown chicken eggs scrambled in a stick of salted butter and washed them down with a quart of goat meat broth. The broth was from meat I butchered myself and cooked in a crock pot with salt, garlic, and minced onion for seasoning. Supplements included 500mg of ascorbic acid, b50 complex, and a slow mag. For dessert, I drank about a pint of plain heavy whipping cream. Yum!

I am 6' 3.5" tall, and I weighed 205lbs during this workout. I started out at 220 to 225 at the beginning of keto adapting back in May. I think a lot of the lost weight was glycogen/water because there was one night during the process where I got up three times to urinate. I usually only get up once during the night. The next morning I weighed in at 210 down from 220. It was a lot of pee. I have been pretty stable at 205 to 210 since then. I do look leaner now and I thought I was lean before. The superficial veins are busting out more so now than ever before. So much for "fat makes you fat". It ain't so when keto adapted.

Tomorrow will be a much deserved day off. I've done six days in a row of fasted morning workouts. I'll jump back in Tuesday morning bright and early. Until then,

Paul

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-01T23:38:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-34877>

Addendum,

second and Last meal of the day consisted of a chunk of cheddar cheese and about a pint of home made goats milk ricotta cheese washed down with the remainder of the quart of heavy whipping cream that I started this morning. It was a refrigerator cleaning meal heavy on the dairy. I had a six hour feeding window from 9AM to 3PM. I took another slow mag and vit. C. The rest of the day shall be food free. I like to go to bed with an empty stomach. I feel that my sleep efficiency is higher that way.

Sweltering in NC,

Paul

### earlyriser — 2012-07-02T02:45:41+0300

### Barry — 2012-07-02T17:46:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-35034>

Paul,

Fair play, best of luck with this. Just a few things

\- use CT if you can too, we need to see the affect of combined CT + FST

\- I'm not sure you will see direct performance gains at that distance/duration. The real benefits of FST/CT are the adaptations that improve RER/RQ i.e. exercise economy. You really don't see the performance gains with this until you go long... I think its needs to be minimum marathon distance and longer.. so 70.3, full ironman, ultramarathons etc. A 1500m swim and 5K run is done a lot closer to threshold. Not sure if keto will increase your performance at this pace. But will be interesting to see.

\-for running, get out fasted and run long, and run off-road, the further into the wilderness the better... thats all I'm going to say !

You will definitely reap the longevity awards by doing this, I'm just not so sure on peak performance. And it is this place where we have a trade-off.... perform better/live shorter... or perform equally, potentially slower but live longer !!

Good luck

### AKMan — 2012-07-02T18:33:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-35044>

Paul, Barry - Do you feel there is a need to replenish glycogen stores in the muscles after an all-out workout or event? CW says you need carbs to do this, how can it be done while in ketosis?

### Barry — 2012-07-02T19:25:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-35061>

Akman

a few things to explain:

1\. The Sport

First thing to decide is whether you actually need to use glucose as the primary fuel. If you are going sub max i.e. long distance, steady state... then through keto/FST/fat adaptation... you can easily perform using ketones and fatty acids as your primary fuel. Now if you are going all out, 5/10K runner, Competitive Cyclist doing TT's etc, then you will dip into glucose more. How much all depends on your adaptations. But first things first, glycogen replenishment is only needed if you are using glycogen as your primary fuel. You're either carb adapted.. not doing FST/CT or keto.... or you are doing those things, but race at such a high intensity that you need to add carbs in at certain times

2\. Upregulate Fat Usage: so one of the main aims of the whole FST/CT thing is to lower your RER at higher intensity levels. For example, the typical sports physiologist will say that you can only burn fat in zone 1/2. However, what I;ve found is that you can upregulate this process so that you can burn fat in zone 3/4... maybe dip into zone 5. In other words, you don't use glucose... you use fats.. so you don't need to glycogen replenish

3\. Gluconeogensis: people seem to forget that there are other ways of making glucose other than eating carbs. Amino Acids, Intermediates and Glycerol can be converted to glucose. So there will always be glucose in the muscle.

Also, remember, you can still eat carbs... and if you are an athlete, I think you can eat more than 50g.

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-04T23:00:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-35533>

2 July 2012 was a day off for me and a day of dietary transition. Sunday evening while lying in bed trying to relax and nod off my heart rate felt faster than normal, so the following morning I measured my RHR. It was 45 bpm which is significantly higher than what it has been (in the low 30's as low as 31) before I embarked on keto adaptation more than a month ago. I searched for anything about ketosis and heart rate. It seems that others have experienced elevated RHR's while in ketosis. The proposed explanations for why this might happen were inadequate IMO. Low magnesium? I've been supplementing and drinking meat broth. Low potassium? Again meat broth and adding sodium as well. It just doesn't make sense to me. If RER goes down when keto adapted and fat is THE preferred fuel for the heart then why would RHR increase?

To test the idea that ketosis increases RHR, I decided to carb load on Monday and Tuesday. My RHR this morning was 37 BPM. That is closer to my previous norm. My Tuesday and Wednesday morning fasted workouts went according to my planned progressions. I think that I am going to continue eating a large mixed macros lunch and an evening low fat carb load meal, something that will clear from my stomach quickly so that I do not feel like I have a brick in my gut at bed time. My thinking is that I will be glycogen repleted and have an empty stomach for the following morning fasted workout in which I will deplete much of the glycogen. I will continue monitoring my RHR to see where it settles at.

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-04T23:40:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-35538>

totals were 92+92/22:57 better than 7-1-12

jumping alternating lunges (back knee touches ground) supersetted with jumping toe touches

I am in ketosis only intermittently now after my fasted workout and before I break the fast at midday. I am shooting for an eight hour or shorter feeding window a la leangains but without the obsessive compulsive consumption of BCAA's before and after exercise. I don't mind if I lose some lean mass. It would be beneficial for me to get down below 190lbs for the event that I am going to race. The only way I can get there is to lose some lean mass. My BF% is already very low. My overarching goal is to get under 40 minutes in the aquathlon which means under 20min for the 1500m swim and under 20min for the 5k run. elite athletes at these distances have a lower (body weight/height cubed) than I have. That is my version of the body mass index. Mass is proportional to volume (or height cubed).

Barry,

### Barry — 2012-07-05T01:04:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-35544>

> **Anna K.;35635 wrote:** Barry, just to clarify about replenishing glycogen stores after a hard workout. I understand your explanation above from the perspective of fat adaptation for performance, but what about muscle mass? If your goal is to bulk up and build muscle mass, do you need to replenish glycogen after a work out? For a fat adapted person do carbs help to increase muscle mass or help speed up the recovery? thanks!

Hi Anna

Muscle is not made from glycogen. Its made from amino's, fats and water. Do you see polar bears replenishing glycogen ?? ;-)

Muscle mass comes from protein synthesis and an anabolic hormonal environment. Insulin is a strong anabolic hormone and it is triggered by glucose. However, its not the only thing that triggers insulin. So muscle building and recovery can still happen without the need for carbs. Carbs might be effective in certain cases but they're not the only option.

Cold showers need to be longer I think. Ideally CT ice baths pre and even post training (although I'm not a huge fan of that). Really would need to get some hormonal profiling to measure this. Maybe run your CT options by Jack and see what he thinks.

You are now doing CKD. You are not fully keto. Not sure if you are going to optimize RER. You would be better off just having carb re-feed days when you feel they are needed rather than doing carbs everyday. Also, when you are eating carbs, put them in with your meal post work-out. Better for insulin sensitivity and protein synthesis that way.

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-06T03:45:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-35781>

I should probably change the name of this thread to "one fat adapted athletes journey". I am intermittently in ketosis due to the combination of intermittent fasting and fasted workouts. I was curious to find any discourse comparing "fat adaped" vs "keto adapted" and I came across this.

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/what-does-it-mean-to-be-fat-adapted/#axzz1zi30i3zm](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/what-does-it-mean-to-be-fat-adapted/#axzz1zi30i3zm)

Sisson is of the opinion that one can be fat adapted without being keto adapted, whereas, I think, Phinney and Volek would disagree. I found a couple of abstracts that support Sisson.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12388154](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12388154)

[http://www.ajcn.org/content/77/2/313.abstract](http://http://www.ajcn.org/content/77/2/313.abstract)

It would appear that intermittent fasting combined with high fat meals can increase stored triglycerides in the muscles and increase the muscles abilities to oxidize the fat without even exercising. Add fasted exercise in there and the effects would be amplified I bet.

So, I guess I'm striving to maximize my capacity to burn fat (IMTG's) during exercise while having well stocked glycogen stores come race day.

This is an experiment with an N=1. It's the workout data along the way that I hope will be informative with the race result as the end point.

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-07T18:26:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-36101>

7-7-12

break fast consisted of

10 eggs scrambled in half a stick of butter, a couple of slices of cheese, a pint of heavy whipping cream, a little peanut butter with honey, and a pint of plain yogurt (with active cultures)

supps: c 500, b50, slow mag

I'm shooting for this to be the only meal of the day. Feeling pretty full right now.

Looking forward to 10 \* 500m runs on the shortest interval possible for tomorrow morning's fasted fun.

I like the sponge analogy in this article.

[http://www.t-nation.com/readArticle.do?id=1628994](http://http://www.t-nation.com/readArticle.do?id=1628994)

Doing glycogen depleting exercise in a fasted state maximizes carb tolerance I think. empty muscle sponges (heightened carb tolerance) increase muscle insulin sensitivity. High muscle insulin sensitivity keeps insulin levels lower until glycogen stores are full. Lower average insulin levels improve prospects for increased longevity. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15771611](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15771611)

Glycogen depleting exercise in a fasted state combined with intermittent fasting

(reminder: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12388154](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12388154))

will increase my ability to store and access muscle glycogen and muscle triglycerides simultaneously. That's my story and I'm sticking to it. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CbxLbDdfhbY\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;CbxLbDdfhbY\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CbxLbDdfhbY\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;CbxLbDdfhbY)

We shall see how this hypothesis works for my performance in the aquathlon.

Until tomorrow,

Cheers.

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-11T05:13:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-36734>

7-9-12

day off

I ate an early low carb high fat breakfast consisting of scrambled eggs in salted butter with some cheese slices, the I ate a second meal in the evening that was low fat high carb consisting of plain yogurt with blueberries and raisins and then dates for afters.

During the day I noticed that more lactic acid than normal seemed to accumulate when I did things like climb a few flights of stairs in the lab building. I felt crappy during my bike ride from campus to the park and ride. I had lots of lactic acid and felt weak on the pedals in gears that are normally easier. I carb loaded the previous night as well. Also, I did not have my usual mug of coffee Monday morning. Too many variables it would seem, but I think there is a common effect. More carbs and less caffeine both cause the body to burn more glucose. Burning more glucose causes more potential lactate accumulation. Also, less caffeine means more adenosine acting as a neurotransmitter making me feel the pain of lactic acid accumulation more. The adenosine is telling my brain to stop and rest.

I got to bed late (11:30) and woke up at 4:45AM feeling wide awake and agitated with a too fast heart rate. I could not go back to sleep, so I got up and prepared for my morning workout by drinking a mug of coffee and purging the GI system.

7-10-12

AM fasted (sort of) workout

first segment

dips and full close grip pullups

1+1/:15 x 122 = 122 dips+122 pullups /30:27

I dropped back a little on the pace and went for longer. The work power product was a PR.

work = total # of reps (bodyweight or force is set at 1 no matter what my weight is, for simplicity)

average power = total work/total time in seconds

work power product = WxP = total reps squared / total time = 122reps(122reps/1827sec) = 8.147

I don't combine the reps for the two different exercises. I keep them separate. Their numbers are the same even though the distances per rep are not. I just do it this way again for simplicity.

This part of the workout was good in spite of the crappy feeling earlier. But the second part was not so good. A new pain on the right side of my tailbone blossomed during the off day (resulting from the run on Sunday? during which I felt off). This new pain in my ass prevented me from doing jumping lunges. So, I did jumping squats and jumping toe touches instead.

I did 4+4/:20 x 93 = 372+372/30:44

WxP = 75.05 (I like to use four sig figs, so sue me.)

I just wanted to go for a full 30 minutes for each segment to blast out the cobwebs. Afterwards, I drank another mug of coffee And packed my scrambled eggs, cheese slices, natural peanut butter, and supplements and went to work. I did not eat my low carb high fat fare until lunch time. That was my one and only meal for the day. By the way, I felt like superman biking into work this morning. It seemed like no lactate was accumulating. That must have been the caffeine talking.

I am renewing my commitment to keto adapting and intermittent fasting (one meal per day) in an attempt to get closer to the ideal body proportions for an elite 1500m swimmer and an elite 5000m runner. I have gathered height and weight data for elite swimmers and runners at these distances. I divided their weights by their heights cubed. their ratios range from 0.0004 to 0.00045. My ratio is around 0.0005 (210lbs/75in cubed). I want to get down into the 180 to 190 lbs range. This most recent carb load binge has gotten me up to 210 to 212lbs. I intend to tweak the autophagy process during the last few weeks before the race in order to accomplish the weight loss.

I plan to focus on raw endurance performances (without carb loading but with caffeine) during workouts. Hopefully, the tail pain will dull quickly. It's annoying and frustrating. Ketosis is anti inflammatory, right?

I'm going to take two aspirin and hit the hay.

Deuces,

Paul

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-11T13:55:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-36773>

Jonnyh,

Concerning gaining lean mass while mostly low carbing, check out the following:

[http://www.stack.com/2012/06/04/carb-back-loading/](http://http://www.stack.com/2012/06/04/carb-back-loading/)

The carb back loading protocol is mostly low carb/ketogenic except after afternoon workouts. The originator of CBL presents a fairly well referenced rationale for his ideas.

[http://www.dangerouslyhardcore.com/370/carb-back-loading-the-final-follow-up/](http://http://www.dangerouslyhardcore.com/370/carb-back-loading-the-final-follow-up/)

One example of how it works

[http://www.dangerouslyhardcore.com/1394/julia-ladewski-competing-on-carb-back-loading/](http://http://www.dangerouslyhardcore.com/1394/julia-ladewski-competing-on-carb-back-loading/)

The discourse about IF is interesting too

[http://www.dangerouslyhardcore.com/1270/intermittent-fasting-part-1/](http://http://www.dangerouslyhardcore.com/1270/intermittent-fasting-part-1/)

One simple recipe for adding fat to the diet is what I call drinkable peanut butter. I mix virgin coconut oil with natural style peanut butter until it is runny. It may sound gross, but it is yummy. The peanut and coconut flavors blend nicely. I consider it a dessert.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-07-11T15:47:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-36804>

> **Barry;35221 wrote:** Akman
>
>
>
> a few things to explain:
>
>
>
> 1\. The Sport
>
>
>
> First thing to decide is whether you actually need to use glucose as the primary fuel. If you are going sub max i.e. long distance, steady state... then through keto/FST/fat adaptation... you can easily perform using ketones and fatty acids as your primary fuel. Now if you are going all out, 5/10K runner, Competitive Cyclist doing TT's etc, then you will dip into glucose more. How much all depends on your adaptations. But first things first, glycogen replenishment is only needed if you are using glycogen as your primary fuel. You're either carb adapted.. not doing FST/CT or keto.... or you are doing those things, but race at such a high intensity that you need to add carbs in at certain times
>
>
>
> 2\. Upregulate Fat Usage: so one of the main aims of the whole FST/CT thing is to lower your RER at higher intensity levels. For example, the typical sports physiologist will say that you can only burn fat in zone 1/2. However, what I;ve found is that you can upregulate this process so that you can burn fat in zone 3/4... maybe dip into zone 5. In other words, you don't use glucose... you use fats.. so you don't need to glycogen replenish
>
>
>
> 3\. Gluconeogensis: people seem to forget that there are other ways of making glucose other than eating carbs. Amino Acids, Intermediates and Glycerol can be converted to glucose. So there will always be glucose in the muscle.
>
>
>
> Also, remember, you can still eat carbs... and if you are an athlete, I think you can eat more than 50g.

What is your opinion on martial arts type training? I plan on starting karate again in September and would prefer to be able to do it 5 times/ week. I don't want to 'over-train' and wear out my adrenals (need those for later on in my life with menapause). I also want keto adapted benefits since I enjoy a pretty good effects from keto currently.

I am 4'11 and 105lbs with good musculature. (I guess you say trim).

Thanks.

### pbj27349 — 2012-07-22T13:54:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-40201>

7-22-12

Sorry about the gap in entries. My wife and children are back from their vacation, so I have less free time to write. I have been continuing with the fasted morning workouts and the one low carb high fat meal per day during midday protocol. My bodyweight has been holding steady at around 205lbs and my resting heart rate was a remarkable 30 to 31 BPM this morning. I think the elevated RHR that I had previously was due to slightly overtraining.

I am hoping that my wife will join me in a fasted bike ride this morning. We will do our long hill laps on the hilliest stretch of road near our house. Each lap is about 4.5 miles. I'll shoot for 4 in under an hour on my mountain bike.

I hope everyone has a wonderful Sunday.

Paul

### pbj27349 — 2012-08-23T13:04:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-46502>

8-23-12

It has been a couple of weeks since the aquathlon. I did not do as well as I was hoping. I overdid it with the coffee the morning of the race, so I felt panicy during the swim. That took me by surprise because I was a competitive swimmer in college. I was also fasted during the race which is my normal state during morning workouts.

Bottom line, [http://www.setupevents.com/index.cfm?fuseaction=event\_results&id=3051](http://http://www.setupevents.com/index.cfm?fuseaction=event_results&id=3051)

My time for the swim was two minutes slower than my effort three years ago, but my run time was a whole 15 second faster than the previous effort. I bombed out on the swim. My weight was around 210lbs at a bf% of less than 10. Not ideal for a distance athlete.

Since the race I have switched training gears. I want to see how strong I can get while maintaining keto adaptation. I will try to maximize weight squared times reps or the work power product for a single set in which the reps are done with a constant speed (blow up and 3 pants down) with no rest/pause at lockout (as continuous tension as possible).

The exercises are:

workout A- overhead squats, full close grip pull ups

workout B- full close grip deadlifts, three chair pushups

I should call my measurements NET work power products because I do not include any of my bodyweight into the calculations... just the added weight. And yes, it is possible to add weight to pushups. I put plates on my lower back.

The most recent workout was the deadlift pushup one. I did it the morning of 8-21-12.

full cg deadlifts (FCGDL): 185lbs squared times 16 reps = 547600 lbs squared reps

three chair pushups (3CPU): 52.5lbs squared times 4 reps = 11025 lbs squared reps

This morning I am going to do overhead squats and full cg pullups... after a MODERATE dose of coffee with some butter. Yum.

Deuces,

Paul

### Danco3636 — 2013-11-25T20:07:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-keto-adapting-athletes-journey.2140/post-101618>

Any more updates and how is your journey going?

# Thread: I'm sleeping!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### snapper — 2012-07-01T14:24:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34761>

I've seen numerous people here say that they are sleeping better in one way or another--longer, deeper, less awakenings.

I was sceptical of the claim that CT could make you sleep better. I'm blown away by how much better I am sleeping. I keep a daily log of how sleep-deprived I feel. I am extraordinarily sensitive to sleep even a little sleep deprivation. Even an hour or two to little sleep means torture the next day.

I got almost instant results from CT. From the time I began face dunking, sleep improved. After a couple of weeks, it had improved very consistently. It's getting to the point that I expect to sleep well. I've never had this in my adult life. It's just amazing.

Has anyone else with chronic sleeping difficulties seen them improve (or resolve) with CT? I guess that for a lot of people who sleep reasonably well, the difference may not be so obvious. For me, it promises to give a good proportion of my life back. To be precise, my records over a long period indicate that I sleep poorly on average about 1-2 nights per week. On those days, I feel horrible, function poorly, and life is just miserable. It's still early days--I've been doing CT for seven weeks. However, I can't believe this is placebo. I didn't even believe it was going to work.

This is marvelous. I just hope it lasts! I dont' know whether I need to progress through to intensive CT (I don't need to lose weight, I just tried it because someone suggested it could help sleep). I'd love to hear of any experiences, especially if anyone has had chronic sleeping problems and either seen or not seen improvement.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-01T15:26:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34767>

Yes, I sleep SO much better with the combo of CT and the keto-Paleo diet. And I'm one of those who was chronically sleep-deprived. I thought it was normal to wake up around 3 a.m., turn the t.v. on for 1-2 hours, then finally sleep again for about an hour before my alarm shook me.

While I still occasionally wake up in the middle of the night, I don't even look at the clock. I wake up, realize it's still dark, and fall back asleep. I never could do this before Paleo & CT! I'd toss & turn for hours.

For me, it's definitely the combo of CT & Paleo that does it. If I eat poorly, I still have a bad night's sleep. I'm doing CT 5 x/week now, and like Dr. K. says, the effects seem to be cumulative, because I still sleep well on my non-CT nights.

### MartiD — 2012-07-01T17:11:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34785>

If I over do carbs, even just veggie carbs ( sweet potato, mushrooms, etc) I can wake up once during night. If I eat my 25 gr carbs for breakfast or lunch I have no issues. I never had chronic sleep issues but now I sleep like a rock thanks to reset and CT.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-01T17:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34795>

Marti that is a sure sing Cortisol/DHEA and Melatonin axis is bad......

+1 on this. I'm sure the DHEA (combined with CT) has been critical to my improved sleep. I generally no longer wake in the middle of the night anymore and if I do on rare occasion, I'm able to fall back to sleep unlike previously where I'd toss and turn for hours. Now if only I could wake up feeling ready to crawl out of bed and not be so stinkin' sore!!

### snapper — 2012-07-02T03:44:55+0300

Thanks. I'm the same: I still wake up during the night, but I usually fall back to sleep. I'm also the same on diet--I'm on a paleo diet. I'm not so fussed about carbs, but they're typically below 60 g and often quite a bit lower.

I hope that's correct about it being cumulative. I need to travel soon and may not find time or opportunity to do something each day. I hope the sleep continues to be good when I do! That would be fantastic.

### snapper — 2012-07-02T03:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34929>

That's really interesting. I eat most of my carbs in the evening. Back when I was on a fairly high carb diet, it seemed that I needed carbs to be able to fall asleep. Now, as long as I get my mind reasonably clear and "still", I just fall asleep when I'm ready. I do wake up during the night, but it really doesn't matter that much because I fall back to sleep. I understand that it's normal to come back to a waking or at least near-waking state during each cycle of sleep. As long as I don't wake up too much, I almost always drop back to sleep quickly. Still, I might experiment with avoiding carbs in the evening. Certainly, if I have a nice amount of fat in the evening, it seems to help induce sleep. Cheers.

# Thread: Side effects of DHEA/Pregnenolone

### BJK77 — 2012-07-01T20:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-dhea-pregnenolone.2143/post-34835>

For those who supplement with DHEA or pregnenolone have you noticed any negative side effects? I'm definitely seeing some positive benefits like increased energy, improved sleeping, amongst others, but my hunger has been out of control ever since I increased my dose.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-01T20:39:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-dhea-pregnenolone.2143/post-34838>

Not on the hunger realm but I did get heart palps from too much preg. I started that one first ended up cutting the dose in half and it was fine. Then a few weeks later I added the DHEA and definitely better sleep, which had already gotten better from CT.

Maybe you are burning more energy thus your body wants more food???? Maybe the dose is too high??? Just throwing some things out there as I don't really know.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-01T22:13:52+0300

Hey Soul,

Yes, I'm the one that got the pure powders. I am weighing my doses out on a mini scale too - I can't say they are always exact. Even with the scale that's difficult to do with the powder, but I'm close - always within 10 grams of my target. I shoot for 100 mg of both pregnenolone and DHEA in the AM and 100 mg pregnenolone and 50 mg DHEA in the afternoon. According to Dr. Kruse, I could probably even increase my DHEA a bit more to 200 mg total per day, but I'm doing a gradual titration . . . much unlike what I want to do

You need pregnenolone

you have to reduce or eliminate thyroid hormone supplementation.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-03T12:28:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-dhea-pregnenolone.2143/post-35258>

Anyone know if too much DHEA can have a paradoxical effect? I started sleeping really well when I began to take it and went up to 120mg per day.... no side effects at all. I loved it.

Then my skiing accident happened and two weeks later, while CTing like crazy (pretty much 24/7), my sleep went entirely bye bye. Eventually I dropped the DHEA and within a few days I was sleeping again.

Started DHEA again after AN relapse and everything was well as long as I stay under 50mg per day. Took it up a notch yesterday just to try it out (probably 80mg) also while increasing CT these days to prepare for getting my PFO fixed.... and boom I'm up again in the middle of the night.

However, after just a few days of regular DHEA, insomnia became much worse, so I stopped that. I have 25 mg sublingual pregnenolone which I started with only 1/2 tab. Definitely improves mental acuity and energy, but after only 3 days, caused a near panic attack (which I have never had before). I dropped down to only 1/4 tab (around 6mg). It seems to help with energy during the day. I am wondering if this "energy" I feel is just pregnenolone being shunted to cortisol as per the hormonal cascade [http://huntforwellness.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/07/Steroid-Hormone-Synthesis.jpg](http://http://huntforwellness.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/07/Steroid-Hormone-Synthesis.jpg) Also I wonder if these hormones all act on a negative feedback system ie does supplementing suppress our own endogenous production of these hormones? If so, would it not be better to "pulse dose" the hormone supplements so we don't encourage our bodies to down regulate its own production?

# Thread: Why is a 4 chambered heart better than a 3 chambered heart?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-a-4-chambered-heart-better-than-a-3-chambered-heart.2145/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-01T23:12:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-a-4-chambered-heart-better-than-a-3-chambered-heart.2145/post-34873>

[http://wiki.answers.com/Q/Why\_is\_a\_4\_chambered\_heart\_better\_than\_a\_3\_chambered\_heart](http://http://wiki.answers.com/Q/Why_is_a_4_chambered_heart_better_than_a_3_chambered_heart)

> A four chambered heart is a closed system and ensures that all blood passing through it is oxygenated. A three chambered heart has both oxygenated an non-oxygenated blood mixed in the single ventricle therefore the blood is at best only half oxygenated. A three chambered creature would suffer from exhaustion long before a four chambered creature.
>
>
>
>
>
> However, it should be noted that there's a reason why frogs have a three-chambered heart. Though they breathe air using their lungs on land, they breathe through their skin using capillaries near their skin under water. When breathing through their lungs, ideally the blood entering the lungs should have CO2, so that it can be diffused out and O2 can be diffused in. When breathing through their skin, ideally the blood in the body has some CO2 so that it can be released through the skin, and O2 can be absorbed. In order to enable the frog to have CO2 in the blood in both the capillaries and lungs, it mixes the blood so that whether it's breathing through its lungs or its skin, it can manage either method passably. Although it is not specialized in breathing either on land or underwater, it is adapted to accomplish both passably, which is part of what makes it amphibian.
>
>
>
> Read more: [http://wiki.answers.com/Q/Why\_is\_a\_4\_chambered\_heart\_better\_than\_a\_3\_chambered\_heart#ixzz1zP5VxHOW](http://http://wiki.answers.com/Q/Why_is_a_4_chambered_heart_better_than_a_3_chambered_heart#ixzz1zP5VxHOW)

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T02:21:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-a-4-chambered-heart-better-than-a-3-chambered-heart.2145/post-35554>

I need to go check if I am part amphibian.... b/c I'm convinced this is some sort of adaptation and it would be too cool if I can breathe underwater

What lab test do I order to check for amphibianism?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T02:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-a-4-chambered-heart-better-than-a-3-chambered-heart.2145/post-35562>

[http://circres.ahajournals.org/content/106/5/809.full](http://http://circres.ahajournals.org/content/106/5/809.full)

> Comparative evolutionary studies have greatly aided our understanding of how the heart develops. Two recent articles from Benoit Bruneau’s laboratory reveal the power of the so-called evo-devo approach and provide yet further details of development, both of the heart itself and of the cells that build it.
>
> .....
>
> The fourth chamber has arisen via the separation of one large ventricle into two. **In animals in which this occurs, a transcription factor called Tbx5 concentrates in the prospective left ventricle during heart development.** Bruneau’s team wondered what the pattern of Tbx5 mRNA expression would look like in the three-chambered hearts of non-crocodilian reptiles. They chose to compare a turtle with a lizard.2 Turtle hearts are interesting because, although they have just one large ventricle, there is evidence of a primitive interventricular septum (IVS)-like structure. Lizards, on the other hand, are considered evolutionarily less complex creatures and have no hint of an IVS.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T02:45:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-a-4-chambered-heart-better-than-a-3-chambered-heart.2145/post-35563>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TBX5\_(gene)](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TBX5_\(gene\))

> T-box transcription factor TBX5 is a protein that in humans is encoded by the TBX5 gene.\[1\]\[2\]\[3\]
>
> This gene is a member of a phylogenetically conserved family of genes that share a common DNA-binding domain, the T-box. **T-box genes encode transcription factors involved in the regulation of developmental processes. This gene is closely linked to related family member T-box 3 (ulnar mammary syndrome) on human chromosome 12. The encoded protein may play a role in heart development** and specification of limb identity. Mutations in this gene have been associated with Holt-Oram syndrome, a developmental disorder affecting the heart and upper limbs. Several transcript variants encoding different isoforms have been described for this gene.\[3\]

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T02:48:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-a-4-chambered-heart-better-than-a-3-chambered-heart.2145/post-35564>

[http://circres.ahajournals.org/content/98/5/651.full](http://http://circres.ahajournals.org/content/98/5/651.full)

Abstract

Atrial septal defect (ASD) is a common congenital heart disease (CHD) occurring in 5 to 7 per 10 000 live births. **Mutations in 5 human genes (NKX2.5, TBX5, GATA4, MYHC, ACTC) are known to cause dominant ASD, but these account for a minority of cases. Human and mouse data suggest that ASD exists in an anatomical continuum with milder septal variants patent foramen ovale (PFO) and atrial septal aneurysm, strongly associated with ischemic stroke and migraine.** We have previously shown in inbred mice that the incidence of PFO strongly correlates with length of the interatrial septum primum, defining a quantitative trait underlying PFO risk. To better understand genetic causation of atrial septal abnormalities, we mapped quantitative trait loci (QTL) influencing septal morphology using mouse strains (QSi5 and 129T2/SvEms) maximally informative for PFO incidence and 3 quantitative septal anatomical traits including septum primum length. \[QSi5Ã—129T2/SvEms\]F2 intercross animals (n=1437) were phenotyped and a whole genome scan performed at an average 17-cM interval. Statistical methodology scoring PFO as a binary phenotype was developed as a confirmatory mapping technique. We mapped 7 significant and 6 suggestive QTL modifying quantitative phenotypes, with 4 supported by binary analysis. Quantitative traits, although strongly associated with PFO (P

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T03:05:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-a-4-chambered-heart-better-than-a-3-chambered-heart.2145/post-35568>

[http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/162914-overview](http://http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/162914-overview)

> Atrial septal defect (ASD) is one of the more commonly recognized congenital cardiac anomalies presenting in adulthood. Atrial septal defect is characterized by a defect in the interatrial septum allowing pulmonary venous return from the left atrium to pass directly to the right atrium. Depending on the size of the defect, size of the shunt, and associated anomalies, this can result in a spectrum of disease from no significant cardiac sequelae to right-sided volume overload, pulmonary arterial hypertension, and even atrial arrhythmias.
>
>
>
> With the routine use of echocardiography, the incidence of atrial septal defect is increased compared to earlier incidence studies using catheterization, surgery, or autopsy for diagnosis. **The subtle physical examination findings and often minimal symptoms during the first 2-3 decades contribute to a delay in diagnosis until adulthood, the majority (more than 70%) of which is detected by the fifth decade. However, earlier intervention of most types of atrial septal defect is recommended.
>
> .....
>
> **Age: Patients with ASD can be asymptomatic through infancy and childhood, though the timing of clinical presentation depends on the degree of left-to-right shunt. Symptoms become more common with advancing age. By the age of 40 years, 90% of untreated patients have symptoms of exertional dyspnea, fatigue, palpitation, sustained arrhythmia, or even evidence of heart failure.
>
> ....
>
> If undetected in childhood, **symptoms can develop gradually over decades** and are largely the result of changing compliance with age, pulmonary arterial hypertension, atrial arrhythmias, and, sometimes, those associated with mitral valve disease in a primum ASD. Virtually all patients with ASD who survive beyond the sixth decade are symptomatic.
>
> ...
>
> Because of the lifetime risk associated with ASD, as outlined including paradoxical embolization, there should be ongoing evaluation and review of the indication and risks for closure, even for patients with small shunts. However, such closure remains controversial because patients with small defects generally have a good prognosis, and the risk of cardiopulmonary bypass may not be warranted. The widespread use of catheter closure of secundum ASD with lower mortality and without cardiopulmonary bypass has raised the question regarding the need to close even small defects.**
>
> ....
>
> Either method of closure, whether transcatheter or surgical, results in excellent hemodynamic outcomes with no significant differences with regard to survival, functional capacity, atrial arrhythmias, or embolic neurologic events.** However, atrial arrhythmia and neurologic events remain long-term risks particularly for patients with pre-existing events.
>
> ....
>
> Closure of an atrial septal defect (ASD) is not recommended in patients with a clinically insignificant shunt (Qp-Qs ratio 0.7 or below) and in those who have severe pulmonary arterial hypertension or irreversible pulmonary vascular occlusive disease who have a reversed shunt with at-rest arterial oxygen saturations of less than 90%.

# Thread: Hey Ya'll From WV

### swedishhill — 2012-07-02T00:28:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-34893>

I am a born and bred city girl from Baltimore, moved to the mountains 4 years ago with my family. I love these hills and all the friendly people. I was thin very thin for that matter. Birth Control screwed me up in my late 20's. Through my thirties I continued to yo yo until at one point I weighted 203. Although I am a tall gal I was heavy. I am currently doing the LR with great success, I think because I was already mostly paleo it was easy to fall in place. The first thing I noticed was my feet aside from losing 8 pounds the first 4 days. They were full of callous' before I started. They are as smooth as a baby's bottom. LOL My skin has also became smoother and glowing. I started at 193 and am now 175. I have not been this weight in over 2 years. I would love to hear all of "Your" stories on this wonderful journey.

### Melinda — 2012-07-02T00:57:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-34897>

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-02T02:00:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-34907>

So, I've read out there that were was a recipe for leaky gut with Greek yogurt, tumeric, etc. But Dr K has said that American dairy is crap. And if you are AI, you should avoid dairy. Plus there is the whole blood sugar issue. So, is yogurt a yes or a no? Personally, I'd love to be eating it, but that of course makes me very suspicious, 'cause I usually love what's bad for me.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-02T05:38:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-34947>

Greek Yogurt commercially isn't great for various reasons...its pasteurized many kinds have extra sugar..etc...I still think if you aren't terribly sensitive to dairy its probably ok depending on type. I eat the Greek Gods plain yogurt none of the flavored kinds it has several different beneficial bacteria.

Its probably not as good as Raw organic from a made from the milk of a Jersey cow or A2 certified cow(if you don't know what A2 is google A2 A1 milk and Devils in the Milk)..but I still think its good for you as long as you don't eat a ton since it does have carbohydrates. Its not really that much though..but some are more sensitive than others. I copy and pasted this from the comment section of Leaky Gut RX blog post.

Those spices are tumeric (which the active ingredient is cucurmin), cinnamon, cumin, black pepper, maca powder and raw cacao nibsâ€¦plus coconut oil. I was wondering how and I going to get these things down me. I finally hit upon mixing all into my greek yogurt. I like

Greek Gods brand plain yogurt. Green Label. Now avail at some WalMarts.

1/2 cup yogurt

2 tablespoons tumeric

1 teaspoon cinnamon

1/2 teaspoon cumin (careful, it is spicy hot)

1 teaspoon Maca Powder

3 tablespoons raw cacao nibs

1/8 teaspoon fresh ground black pepper

2 tablespoons melted coconut oil

Someone on the marksdailyapple.com forum posted it several times on their and also on the Leaky Gut RX blog post comment section. Dextery? I think

### colleencoble — 2012-07-02T05:40:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-34949>

Is raw yogurt even possible to find? I was reading online about making my own yogurt with raw milk and all the recipes recommend heating it when basically pasteurizes it from what I've read.

### Kiera2 — 2012-07-02T05:49:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-34951>

> **colleencoble;35109 wrote:** Is raw yogurt even possible to find? I was reading online about making my own yogurt with raw milk and all the recipes recommend heating it when basically pasteurizes it from what I've read.

I made yogurt with raw milk for a long time. If you don't heat the raw milk first, the bacteria in the milk battles the bacteria in the yogurt culture, so you don't have a consistant end product. But I don't think slowly heating the milk in a double boiler is the same as commercial pasturising.

I always heated the milk to 80c, then let it cool to 40c before adding the culture. Perfect every time.

I make raw cow's milk yogurt from organic, grass fed raw milk. Just warm to 110f add culture. Maintain temp for 8+ hrs. Done. If you bring the milk up to 80c it is no longer raw. This tends to be a thinner yogurt, but it's good.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-02T16:39:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-35006>

> **Jennifer;35118 wrote:** Just open a capsule and pour it on? Let it sit out uncovered?
>
> A little off topic but I've been reading on Facebook that you need to ferment kraut in air tight container. I have a batch I just did in open jar. Hmm. The direction I got w my water kefir said open too and now I'm reading that's a closed bottle ferment.

I think I put a lid on the jar... not quite sure anymore. Yep, all I did was add some probiotics. Mine come in powder form. Stir it up a bit and wait. It should taste nicely sour when ready. If you use kefir cultures, then don't put a lid on.

Kraut needs and airtight container. There is a difference between aerobic and an-aerobic ferments.

Water Kefir and Kombucha need aerobic fermentation. Then you remove the culture and let it ferment with an air-tight lid for a while to get the fizziness if so desired. That step is different. It makes a difference in which cultures will thrive.

### kathylu — 2012-07-02T17:49:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-35035>

Staying below 115 degrees F keeps milk a raw product. Fermentation is an anaerobic process (meaning without oxygen). If oxygen is present, respiration is occurring. Lactic acid forming bacteria, the kind responsible for fermenting milk into yogurt are mostly anaerobes (occasionally microaerophillic). Lactobacilli are highly tolerant to low pH and can easily maintain a low pH and protect raw milk from Gram-negative and Gram-positive pathogenic bacteria, which are not tolerant of low pH values. If you use raw milk for yogurt making (I do), please ferment for at least 8 hours to make sure that pathogenic bacteria have been eliminated by the lactic acid formation. The yogurt tastes a bit more sour, but I don't want a case of brucellosis, thank you.

### ealachan — 2012-07-02T17:52:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-35036>

Jennifer, I'll dig out my yogurt tutorial and PM it to you so as not to spam the board (it's wordy).

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-02T17:54:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-35038>

> Fermentation refers to catabolic processes where organic molecules, such as sugars or amino acids, are broken down to produce energy without the use of a membrane-bound electron transport chain. **Depending upon the organism, fermentation can occur in the presence (aerobic) and/or in the absence (anaerobic) of oxygen.** Fermentation pathways produce byproducts such as carbon dioxide, ethanol (alcohol), or organic acids (lactic acid or acetic acid, for example). Yeast cells can ferment sugar and in the process produce carbon dioxide and alcohol. Lactobacillus bacteria can ferment the sugar lactose and produce lactic acid; this is how yogurt is made. Wine vinegar is produced by bacteria that ferment the sugars in grape juice and produce acetic acid. Fermentation is sometimes called anaerobic respiration, but microbiologists disagree with this usage. The term respiration refers to energy-yielding metabolic processes that involve a membrane-bound electron transport chain.
>
>
>
> Read more: [http://wiki.answers.com/Q/What\_are\_the\_Differences\_between\_aerobic\_and\_anaerobic\_fermentation#ixzz1zTeCNOL9](http://http://wiki.answers.com/Q/What_are_the_Differences_between_aerobic_and_anaerobic_fermentation#ixzz1zTeCNOL9)

It just depends on which ones you want to culture.

### vlynnb — 2012-07-02T20:28:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-35086>

> **kathylu;35195 wrote:** Staying below 115 degrees F keeps milk a raw product. Fermentation is an anaerobic process (meaning without oxygen). If oxygen is present, respiration is occurring. Lactic acid forming bacteria, the kind responsible for fermenting milk into yogurt are mostly anaerobes (occasionally microaerophillic). Lactobacilli are highly tolerant to low pH and can easily maintain a low pH and protect raw milk from Gram-negative and Gram-positive pathogenic bacteria, which are not tolerant of low pH values. If you use raw milk for yogurt making (I do), please ferment for at least 8 hours to make sure that pathogenic bacteria have been eliminated by the lactic acid formation. The yogurt tastes a bit more sour, but I don't want a case of brucellosis, thank you.

Kathylu, This information is just what I needed to start looking for a raw milk source. My husband, apPhysican, was totally against raw milk because of the serious illnesses he had seen in children in this rural area from the raw milk. Not just brucellosis but pathogenic bacteria that can cause kidney failure.

I am against it because I was raised on it and even under the most sanitary conditions it can taste bad just from whatever the cows are eating in the pasture, think garlic in your milk.

But if I could make it into yogurt free of the pathogens that is great. Wonder if fermenting gets rid of wild onion/garlic flavor?

### vlynnb — 2012-07-02T20:29:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-35087>

> **kathylu;35195 wrote:** Staying below 115 degrees F keeps milk a raw product. Fermentation is an anaerobic process (meaning without oxygen). If oxygen is present, respiration is occurring. Lactic acid forming bacteria, the kind responsible for fermenting milk into yogurt are mostly anaerobes (occasionally microaerophillic). Lactobacilli are highly tolerant to low pH and can easily maintain a low pH and protect raw milk from Gram-negative and Gram-positive pathogenic bacteria, which are not tolerant of low pH values. If you use raw milk for yogurt making (I do), please ferment for at least 8 hours to make sure that pathogenic bacteria have been eliminated by the lactic acid formation. The yogurt tastes a bit more sour, but I don't want a case of brucellosis, thank you.

Kathylu, This information is just what I needed to start looking for a raw milk source. My husband, a Physican, was totally against raw milk because of the serious illnesses he had seen in children in this rural area from the raw milk. Not just brucellosis but the pathogenic bacteria that can cause kidney failure.

I am against it because I was raised on it and even under the most sanitary conditions it can taste bad just from whatever the cows are eating in the pasture, think garlic in your milk.

But if I could make it into yogurt free of the pathogens that is great. Wonder if fermenting gets rid of wild onion/garlic flavor?

### kathylu — 2012-07-02T23:33:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yogurt.2147/post-35125>

> **vlynnb;35246 wrote:** Kathylu, This information is just what I needed to start looking for a raw milk source. My husband, apPhysican, was totally against raw milk because of the serious illnesses he had seen in children in this rural area from the raw milk. Not just brucellosis but pathogenic bacteria that can cause kidney failure.
>
>
>
> I am against it because I was raised on it and even under the most sanitary conditions it can taste bad just from whatever the cows are eating in the pasture, think garlic in your milk.
>
>
>
> But if I could make it into yogurt free of the pathogens that is great. Wonder if fermenting gets rid of wild onion/garlic flavor?

As for the taste...unfortunately the answer is no. If your raw milk has that "grassy, spring onion" flavor, that will come through in the yogurt too. I console myself with the fact that rapidly growing forage makes the cows make healthier milk and that that is the way it is supposed to taste! Bland, sweet milk means the cows have been eating grains....

For the food safety issue, remember that ANY raw food can be risky. Always be sure of your source and how the products are handled and stored. Fortunately, fermentation does increase the safety of dairy products. Can't promise 100% though, but remember that the vast majority of contaminated dairy cases occur in pasteurized products. And most cases of food borne illness actually occur in fruits and veggies, not meat or dairy. Things get interesting when you look at the numbers...not at all what you are led to believe.

# Thread: Tick Bite Causes Meat Allergy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tick-bite-causes-meat-allergy.2152/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-07-02T14:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tick-bite-causes-meat-allergy.2152/post-34977>

> There's a new weapon in the war on meat: a tiny tick, whose bite might be spreading meat allergies up the East Coast.
>
>
>
> A bite from the lone star tick, so-called for the white spot on its back, looks innocent enough. But University of Virginia researchers say saliva that sneaks into the tiny wound may trigger an allergic reaction to meat -- agonizing enough to convert lifelong carnivores into wary vegetarians.
>
>
>
> "People will eat beef and then anywhere from three to six hours later start having a reaction; anything from hives to full-blown anaphylactic shock," said Dr. Scott Commins, assistant professor of medicine at the University of Virginia in Charlottesville. "And most people want to avoid having the reaction, so they try to stay away from the food that triggers it."
>
>
>
> Commins said cases of the bizarre allergy are popping up along the East Coast and into the Bible Belt, areas ripe with lone star ticks. He's already seen 400 or so. And 90 percent of them have a history of tick bites, he said.

Read more at [http://abcnews.go.com/Health/Wellness/allergic-meat-lone-star-tick-spreading-vegetarianism/story?id=16610228#.T\_GL23DdKkJ](http://http://abcnews.go.com/Health/Wellness/allergic-meat-lone-star-tick-spreading-vegetarianism/story?id=16610228#.T_GL23DdKkJ)

Sounds like a government conspiracy to me.

### chocolate — 2012-07-02T21:14:35+0300

This allergy only popped up after I started eating in restaurants. # Thread: Stuff that Will kill Me  List

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuff-that-will-kill-me-list.2157/post-34999>

I got the inspiration to do this from something ealachan said recently:

_Make a list of bad stuff, and call it the "Stuff That Will Kill Me" list? I'm a big fan of cutting to the chase_

I am a visual learner so I have to have reminders I can see.

First on my list:

SUGAR

Grains

Watching commercials

Negative energy people

There are so many more. Please add some of your own.

Stuff that will help me stay alive and thrive:

No sugar/carbs in da house.

No TV

Laying in the green grass

Cultivating gratitude

### ealachan — 2012-07-02T16:41:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuff-that-will-kill-me-list.2157/post-35007>

Haha, I love it!

I will add:

KFC (as in, Kentucky Fried Chicken, yes)

stressing over stuff that I have no control over anyway

video games

And to the Stay Alive list, I will add:

finding humor in things that would otherwise frustrate me

taking time to appreciate the beauty around me, or to find it if it's not obvious at first

### Glamazon — 2012-07-02T17:29:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuff-that-will-kill-me-list.2157/post-35028>

> **Souldanzer;35170 wrote:** Why would you want to remind yourself and activate all those cravings pathways every time you open the fridge?
>
>
>
> JMHO. How about a list with yummy things you CAN eat instead?

Sugar and grains are an addiction as strong as heroin per current literature. They attach to the opiate receptors of the brain. It is a matter of altered brain chemistry not will power. Ask an addict what they would rather have...alcohol or a hug?

All grains and sugar no longer exist in my house or in the fridge. I am a visual learner. I am a wonderful and great person with an awesome sense of humor. I teach people how to meditate and live with deep spiritual intention all over this world for the last 15 years. If what you saying was accomplishable for me to do at the level of wellness I am currently at it would be done.

I use what works for me. ealachan seems like a very intelligent and self aware person. What she said resonated for me.

Peace.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-02T17:39:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuff-that-will-kill-me-list.2157/post-35032>

You are doing what I am saying or you wouldn't be on this forum and wouldn't just have had a consult with Dr. K.

If you really know the biochemistry and neurological workings of an addict's brain, you would never post that list on your fridge. Opposite action.

Put an alcoholic into a bar with all their old friends and they will relapse. Put them into a sober environment with non-drinking mates and they will have much less of a problem staying clean. If a heroin addict shoots up in an environment that he isn't adapted to using heroin, he will get an overdose from his normal dose b/c his body won't be doing all those things it usually does in anticipation of the shot. Put yourself in front of a list of foods you're not supposed to eat and your insulin will shoot up. You removed sugar and grains physically from your home why would you bring them back in a verbal version.

Anyways, looks like I'm not welcome here, so that's all I got to say.
