**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gout-study-poster-sardines-and-herring-are-bad-wth.2159/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-02T18:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gout-study-poster-sardines-and-herring-are-bad-wth.2159/post-35046>

[https://sphotos.xx.fbcdn.net/hphotos-prn1/555902\_385531728162130\_228578399\_n.jpg](http://https://sphotos.xx.fbcdn.net/hphotos-prn1/555902_385531728162130_228578399_n.jpg)

### janagram — 2012-07-02T18:46:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gout-study-poster-sardines-and-herring-are-bad-wth.2159/post-35049>

really curious about that...it's cw. hope Dr. K. casts some light...

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-02T19:01:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gout-study-poster-sardines-and-herring-are-bad-wth.2159/post-35053>

Gout is a really interesting case. A few years back I thought I was developing a case of gout from symptoms in my large toe.

I suggest you hit up pubmed and read about xanthine oxidase.

But there's a few studies that suggest uric acid is actually an antioxidant in the body - and a lack of it is a risk factor for parkinsons.

My conclusion after reading everything was that I had iron overload - so that was one of the reason for my frequent blood donations.

Once I started doing that and reducing my iron levels, I never had a toe problem again. There's a few studies that suggest having a high level of uric acid + a high level of iron, can lead to gout.....But just like cholesterol - what you eat doesn't necessarily translate into your blood levels - since the body is tightly regulating it.

### chocolate — 2012-07-02T19:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gout-study-poster-sardines-and-herring-are-bad-wth.2159/post-35058>

thanks....we are going to start giving blood... I used to think there might be something wrong... but honestly, we can't find anything "contagious" and its seems to be the smartest option for some turnover. I wish they would test blood better when you donate... seems like a fair trade and it helps everyone.

### Michael — 2012-07-02T20:54:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gout-study-poster-sardines-and-herring-are-bad-wth.2159/post-35091>

> **chocolate;35206 wrote:** [https://sphotos.xx.fbcdn.net/hphotos-prn1/555902\_385531728162130\_228578399\_n.jpg](http://https://sphotos.xx.fbcdn.net/hphotos-prn1/555902_385531728162130_228578399_n.jpg)
>
>
> It's on the basis of their containing relatively high levels of purines, as your diagram says.
>
>
>
> Thomas Cowan, the WAPF doctor, is interesting on this. It does, indeed, seem that excessive consumption of protein is s factor; however, there also seems to be a link with the metabolic syndrome.
>
>
>
> Historically, it seems gout began to become more common in the late medieval period. (Interestingly - what he doesn't say, but what you'll find elsewhere - it's about this time that left and right shoes began to be made. Previously, there was no difference between L & R. But if you had painful joints in your toes, you might want a more shaped shoe that might press less, since it followed the shape of the foot more closely.)
>
>
>
> Anyway, you think about wealthy males _circa_ the fifteenth-century and you see you've got people that are likely to be both eating more than their fair share of meat, and doing refined carbs. They're possibly preferring the leaner cuts of meat, too. These are also the people who can afford the imported sugar and who probably over-indulge in sweet dishes and alcohol.
>
>
>
> Dr. Cowan says:
>
>
>
>
> > ... people with historically high protein intake who also ate lots of fats and soup broths, with no refined carbohydrates, rarely if ever suffer from gout.
>
>
>
> And continues:
>
>
>
> Finally, it will probably come as no surprise to the readers of Wise Traditions that the specific nutrient that seems to prevent the buildup of uric acid is vitamin A, a fat-soluble vitamin that is particularly associated with the kidney. In fact, gout can be seen as a problem of the kidneys not being able to excrete enough of the uric acid to prevent the buildup, rather than simply a matter of excessive protein intake.

Since vitamin A is involved, maybe these guys were preferring steak to liver, too.

At any rate, the historical connection is interesting. And Dr. Cowan says he has success with patients with some simple steps:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/ask-the-doctor/gout](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/ask-the-doctor/gout)

### AKMan — 2012-07-02T22:51:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gout-study-poster-sardines-and-herring-are-bad-wth.2159/post-35115>

I am a 46 yo male. I had my first gout attack in 1999 when I was 34 after losing 20 lbs on Atkins. I was a bit overweight and firmly entrenched in the SAD. I read up on gout and tried avoiding purine rich foods. Second attack was a year later, I was a little heavier, and still eating badly. After that I got an attack every 4-8 months for several years, progressing into every 4-8 weeks. I was taking colchisine, a medicine which helps get over the gout attack faster, but makes you sick as hell (dosing instructions: take 2 pills every hour until you get diarrhea then stop).

2009 was the worst year. I was nearly 100 pounds overweight, high bp, high trigs, high chol, fatty liver, hypothyroid, and couldn't break the chain. I bucked all the wisdom I was given about weight loss and started an antiinflammatory diet high in antioxidant rich foods and low carb. Last gout attack was in Apr 2009. Discovered paleo in 2010. Now down to 165lbs, off all meds, and eating everything that is supposed to cause gout with wild abandon!

### chocolate — 2012-11-18T18:25:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gout-study-poster-sardines-and-herring-are-bad-wth.2159/post-65153>

[http://metabolichealing.com/key-integrated-functions-of-your-body/cardiovascular/gout-cvd-and-fructose-uric-acid-inhibits-nitric-oxide/](http://http://metabolichealing.com/key-integrated-functions-of-your-body/cardiovascular/gout-cvd-and-fructose-uric-acid-inhibits-nitric-oxide/)

> Gout, CVD & Fructose: Uric Acid Inhibits Nitric Oxide
>
> by Michael McEvoy 30/11/2011 10:10pm | 2 comments
>
> heartonwall thampapon1
>
> photo: thampapon1
>
>
>
> Uric acid is a double edged sword. It is a waste product of purine and nucelic acid metabolism. It is also an antioxidant. When it is elevated, it is implicated as a causative factor in cardiovascular disease, diabetes type 2 and metabolic syndrome. Chronically elevated levels of uric acid is also a primary factor in gout, in which uric acid crystals precipitate in the tissues. Over 3/4ths of patients with heart disease and elevated triglycerides will present with high levels of uric acid.
>
>
>
> High levels of uric acid are one of the key factors associated with cardiovascular disease and metabolic syndrome.
>
>
>
> An article I wrote months ago, indicated **the evidence that elevated uric acid may be due in large part to excess mycotoxins, which are fungal metabolites. This was first purported 20 years ago by the former head of the World Health Organization, A.V. Constantini, MD who contends that high uric acid is fungal in origin, and not metabolic in origin.**
>
>
>
> There is also a large body of evidence which reveals that high levels of uric acid can be generated through the consumption of fructose. Fructose is fruit sugar, and it is also present in fructose that is highly refined to create high fructose corn syrup (HFCS).
>
> Nitric Oxide
>
>
>
> Nitric oxide (NO) is a vasodilator. NO is synthesized from the amino acid L-arginine. Nitric oxide allows the outer lining (endothelium) of blood vessels to relax their musculature. NO is thus one of the most powerful regulatory molecules of the cardiovascular system. When uric acid reacts with nitric oxide, the production of nitric acid will be depleted.
>
>
> Reducing Uric Acid
>
>
>
> High levels of uric acid is a symptom and reflects dysregulation. Many clinicians believe that serum uric acid levels greater than 5 mg/dL is considered elevated. Elevated uric acid levels are somewhat of a conundrum. For some individuals, purine-rich foods like organ meats, red meats and sardines can cause an elevation in uric acid. Although this is not the case for all people.
>
>
>
> Recent studies certainly indicate the relationship between fructose and elevated uric acid.
>
>
>
> What is apparent is that there are no "one size fits all" approaches for reducing uric acid. I would argue that elevated uric acid is the 'tip of the iceberg', which may be reflective of a number of de-regulatory processes in the body.

This is good stuff.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Noticed something bizarre last night after CT!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis

### jmathisen — 2012-07-02T19:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-35071>

So I've been faithfully CT'ing for several weeks, 40 degree or so water, 1 hour. Along with LR, lost 37 lbs.

Was standing in the bathroom after getting out last night and looking at myself in the mirror and noticing some loose skin from the weight loss on my thighs and abdomen, and what was tres bizarre, was that the line of loose skin just about exactly followed the line where my body is submerged in the cold water in the tub.

Kinda freaky. Now I gotta get a stock tank, can't have weird lines.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-37115>

> **msclman;37061 wrote:** an hour at 40 degrees!!!!!! i cant even get my tub to 40 and i put 30 pounds of ice in it! how bad do you shiver?

What's weird is no shivering during CT. Few seconds of icy shock when getting in, and then I spend a few seconds getting my headphones and my book and getting the ice bag on my abdomen, and by the time I'm done with that, I've adjusted.

\*But\* once I get out, I get about 2-3 minutes to dry off and pick up my stuff, and then the whole body shivers start. I've never had my butt actually shiver and I'm from montana originally, but since CT, that thing looks like one of those ladies with the belts around their middle hooked up to the machine getting vibrated... And that lasts a good 10-15 minutes, I have to really concentrate to not shiver.

Then it all settles down and I'm just cold, but not frozen.

So far, benefits outweighing the annoyances.

### msclman — 2012-07-12T23:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-37271>

# Thread: Compounding bio-identical hormones: CW
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compounding-bio-identical-hormones-cw.2161/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-07-02T20:31:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compounding-bio-identical-hormones-cw.2161/post-35088>

An excerpt from my HMO about where they stand. Sad.

4/29/2009

**Bioidentical and Compounded Hormones

Furthermore, hormone levels measured at a moment in time are not a good measure of long term hormone levels because of the natural fluctuations throughout the day. Perimenopausal women (by definition!) may have low estrogen levels one moment, and then normal or high levels the next. Providers at Kaiser Permanente encourage you to rely on your individual symptoms and not blood or saliva levels to determine the effectiveness of any hormone replacement regimen, compounded or not. **Kaiser Permanente does not cover hormone testing for the purpose of monitoring hormone replacement therapy.**

Where can I read more about this?

The leading experts on womenâ€™s health have developed their own responses to this issue. Below is a summary of their positions along with a link to their full fact sheet on the topic:

â€¢

The American College of Obstetrician and Gynecologists (ACOG): â€œMost compounded products have not undergone rigorous clinical testing for safety and efficacy, and issues regarding purity, potency and quality are a concern. Compounded hormone products have the same safety issues as those associated with hormone therapy agents that are approved by the FDA and may have additional risks intrinsic to compounding.â€ (View press release by searching â€œbioidenticalsâ€ at [www.acog.org](http://http://www.acog.org) )

â€¢

North American Menopause Society (NAMS): â€œNAMS does not recommend custom-compounded products over well-tested, government-approved products for the majority of women- and does not recommend saliva testing to determine hormone levels.â€ (View full fact sheet at [www.menopause.org/bioidentical\_NAMS.aspx](http://http://www.menopause.org/bioidentical_NAMS.aspx))

â€¢

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration: â€œThe FDA is concerned that claimsâ€¦(that bioidenticals are safer, natural, can correct hormonal imbalances)â€¦ mislead women and health care professionals, giving them a false sense of assurance about using potentially dangerous hormone products.â€ (View the full fact sheet at â€œBioidenticals: Sorting Myth from Factâ€ at [www.fda.gov/consumer/updates/bioidenticals040808.html](http://http://www.fda.gov/consumer/updates/bioidenticals040808.html) )

What should I do now?**Many women feel confused about hormone therapy, especially if they thought compounded bioidentical hormones to be a more natural or safer option. The decision to use, or not use hormone therapy should be based on your own personal medical history and your own health goals. There is no â€œrightâ€ answer for every woman.

Consider the following examples:

For some women, the benefits of hormone therapy outweigh any potential risks. Their quality of life (whether that be a better night sleep, a better sex drive, or a better sense of well-being) are greatly enhanced on hormone therapy. Hormone users should consider: why am I using them, what are the benefits, and what are the potential risks for me? These questions should be covered with their healthcare provider at least every year. Many women will decrease their dose of hormones over time and then stop completely after several years. If a patient is considering bioidentical hormones, we urge her to start with those that are FDA-approved and have been investigated for purity, safety and efficacy.

For some women with bothersome perimenopausal and menopausal symptoms, the potential side-effects and potential risks of hormone therapy are not acceptable, and they choose not to take them at all. And, they may have a medical condition such as blood clots or heart disease, which makes the risks higher.

Some women have very minor or no symptoms of menopause. There is no recommendation to use hormones in this situation. Any claims for health benefits in the absence of symptoms should be viewed with great skepticism at this time.

Please talk to your healthcare provider about this information. We want to help you achieve your health goals, now and in the future. Please revisit your choices on hormone therapy at least every year, and help us advocate for reliable and responsible research in this area. There are multiple options to help individualize hormone replacement therapy if you decide this is the right treatment for you.

# Thread: candida and arachidonic acid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-arachidonic-acid.2162/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-02T21:55:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-arachidonic-acid.2162/post-35100>

> 1.7) Antifungal drug resistance
>
> Resistance is defined as the competency of a microorganism to grow in the
>
> presence of a high level of an antimicrobial agent (Lewis, 2001), and clinical
>
> resistance is the perseverance of clinical lesions even though the prescribed
>
> dose, known to be effective, is used for at least seven days, and this is often
>
> associated with biofilm formation (Al-Mohsen & Hughes, 1998). Since both C.
>
> albicans and C. dubliniensis have the ability to form biofilms, these infections
>
> may exhibit increased resistance to antifungal treatment and host immune
>
> defences (Jabra-Rizk et al., 2004; Ramage et al., 2001b). Antifungal
>
> resistance of biofilms may be attributed to a restriction in penetration of the
>
> drug, due to the negatively charged EXM (Lewis, 2001). Another factor is a
>
> decrease in the growth rate of the cells in a biofilm, because most
>
> antimicrobials are active on fast growing organisms. The expression of
>
> resistance genes, such as those that encode the proteins for the multidrug
>
> resistance pumps which transport the antimicrobials across the cell membrane
>
> might also be involved in resistance. Presently, there are two main groups of
>
> drugs used for the treatment of antifungal infections, namely drugs produced
>
> by organisms, e.g. the polyene drugs, amphotericin B and nystatin, and the
>
> synthetically produced drugs, e.g. flucytosine and the azole drugs (Al-Mohsen
>
> & Hughes, 1998). The polyene antifungal, amphotericin B, which is fungicidal
>
> and has the broadest spectrum of antifungal activity, has become the â€œgold
>
> standardâ€￾ for the treatment of mycoses (Perfect & Wright, 1994;
>
> Mahmoudabadi & Drucker, 2006).

page 37

[http://etd.uovs.ac.za/ETD-db/theses/available/etd-10122009-095911/unrestricted/EllsR.pdf](http://http://etd.uovs.ac.za/ETD-db/theses/available/etd-10122009-095911/unrestricted/EllsR.pdf)

### chocolate — 2012-07-02T21:58:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-arachidonic-acid.2162/post-35101>

[http://jac.oxfordjournals.org/content/63/1/124.full](http://http://jac.oxfordjournals.org/content/63/1/124.full)

**Objectives DurinArachidonic acid increases antifungal susceptibility of Candida albicans and Candida dubliniensis

Abstract

> During Candida albicans infection, arachidonic acid (AA) is released from phospholipids of infected host cell membranes and used by C. albicans as the sole carbon source and for production of eicosanoids. AA can be incorporated into the phospholipids of yeasts, influencing the saturation level and fluidity of yeast cell membranes. It is suggested that the effectiveness of polyene (e.g. amphotericin B) and imidazole (e.g. clotrimazole) antifungals may depend upon the level of unsaturation and ergosterol in the membrane. Therefore, the aim of this study was to evaluate the effect of AA on the cell membrane and susceptibility of C. albicans and Candida dubliniensis biofilms towards amphotericin B and clotrimazole.
>
>
>
> Methods Both yeasts were grown in the presence and absence of AA and the effect of amphotericin B and clotrimazole was examined by confocal laser scanning microscopy, determination of mitochondrial metabolism, unsaturation index of the phospholipid fractions and ergosterol content of the membranes.
>
>
>
> Results AA had no effect on the viability of the cells in the biofilm; however, there was an increase in ergosterol levels as well as antifungal susceptibility of biofilms grown in the presence of AA.
>
>
>
> Conclusions AA influences phospholipid unsaturation and ergosterol content of both yeasts C. albicans and C. dublininensis, increasing susceptibility towards the antifungals. Pretreatment of biofilms with polyunsaturated fatty acids may result in the reduction in antifungal dose needed to inhibit biofilms.

**

I guess this is where the seleno proteins come in, along with the histidine.

### chocolate — 2012-07-02T22:05:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-arachidonic-acid.2162/post-35103>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/entrez/21846384?dopt=Abstract&holding=f1000,f1000m,isrctn](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/entrez/21846384?dopt=Abstract&holding=f1000,f1000m,isrctn)

Increased neuroinflammatory and arachidonic acid cascade markers, and reduced synaptic proteins, in brain of HIV-1 transgenic rats.

Abstract

BACKGROUND:

> Cognitive impairment has been reported in human immune deficiency virus-1- (HIV-1-) infected patients as well as in HIV-1 transgenic (Tg) rats. This impairment has been linked to neuroinflammation, disturbed brain arachidonic acid (AA) metabolism, and synapto-dendritic injury. We recently reported upregulated brain AA metabolism in 7- to 9-month-old HIV-1 Tg rats. We hypothesized that these HIV-1 Tg rats also would show upregulated brain inflammatory and AA cascade markers and a deficit of synaptic proteins.
>
> METHODS:
>
>
>
> We measured protein and mRNA levels of markers of neuroinflammation and the AA cascade, as well as pro-apoptotic factors and synaptic proteins, in brains from 7- to 9-month-old HIV-1 Tg and control rats.
>
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> Compared with control brain, HIV-1 Tg rat brain showed immunoreactivity to glycoprotein 120 and tat HIV-1 viral proteins, and significantly higher protein and mRNA levels of (1) the inflammatory cytokines interleukin-1β and tumor necrosis factor α, (2) the activated microglial/macrophage marker CD11b, (3) AA cascade enzymes: AA-selective Ca2+-dependent cytosolic phospholipase A2 (cPLA2)-IVA, secretory sPLA2-IIA, cyclooxygenase (COX)-2, membrane prostaglandin E2 synthase, 5-lipoxygenase (LOX) and 15-LOX, cytochrome p450 epoxygenase, and (4) transcription factor NF-κBp50 DNA binding activity. HIV-1 Tg rat brain also exhibited signs of cell injury, including significantly decreased levels of brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and drebrin, a marker of post-synaptic excitatory dendritic spines. Expression of Ca2+-independent iPLA2-VIA and COX-1 was unchanged.
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> HIV-1 Tg rats show elevated brain markers of neuroinflammation and AA metabolism, with a deficit in several synaptic proteins. These changes are associated with viral proteins and may contribute to cognitive impairment. The HIV-1 Tg rat may be a useful model for understanding progression and treatment of cognitive impairment in HIV-1 patients.

### chocolate — 2012-07-03T04:02:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-arachidonic-acid.2162/post-35202>

[http://etd.uovs.ac.za/cgi-bin/ETD-browse/view\_etd?URN=etd-10122009-095911](http://http://etd.uovs.ac.za/cgi-bin/ETD-browse/view_etd?URN=etd-10122009-095911)

> Candida albicans and C. dubliniensis are two closely related pathogenic yeast species, sharing many phenotypic characteristics which make it difficult to differentiate them, especially in clinical samples. As a result of the similarities between these species, identification techniques, based on phenotypic characteristics, have been developed. In this study some of these techniques and virulence factors were used to characterise strains belonging to these species and to select phenotypically dissimilar strains for further study. This was performed to evaluate if the effect of arachidonic acid (20:4) on these strains were the same, even though they are phenotypically different. Candida albicans and C. dubliniensis can form biofilms which play an important role during infection. During C. albicans infection, 20:4, a long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA), derived from the phospholipids (PLs) of the infected host cell membrane, serves as carbon source and precursor for eicosanoid production. Conflicting results are presented in literature regarding the effect of 20:4 on morphogenesis in C. albicans. In addition, the effect of 20:4 on growth and morphology of C. dubliniensis is unknown. Microscopic examination and enzyme activity assay indicated that 1 mM 20:4 had little to no effect on growth and metabolic activity of planktonic cells and biofilms, as well as on the morphology and viability of the cells in the biofilms. The uptake of PUFAs by yeasts is necessary for utilisation as metabolic fuels, cellular building blocks and the production of signalling molecules. However, there are no definitive studies regarding the uptake and cellular metabolism of 20:4 by these pathogenic yeasts. **The uptake and incorporation of 20:4 by planktonic cells and biofilms of selected strains of C. albicans and C. dubliniensis were evaluated by subjecting residual and cellular lipids from planktonic cells and biofilms, grown in the presence and absence of 20:4, to gas chromatography and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry. Strain specific variation in 20:4 uptake and incorporation into different lipid fractions of planktonic cells and biofilms were found. In addition, eicosanoids produced by biofilms in the presence of 20:4 were extracted and it was found that biofilms of these strains were capable of producing 3-hydroxy fatty acids from 20:4. Arachidonic acid can be incorporated into the PLs of yeasts, influencing saturation in cell membranes. It is suggested that the effectiveness of antifungals may depend upon the level of unsaturation and ergosterol content of the membrane, therefore the effect of 20:4 on the cell membrane and susceptibility of C. albicans and C. dubliniensis biofilms towards amphotericin B and clotrimazole were also determined. This was performed by confocal laser scanning microscopy, determination of mitochondrial metabolism, unsaturation index of the PL fractions and ergosterol content of the membranes of biofilms grown in the presence and absence of 20:4. The results indicated that 20:4 influences PL unsaturation and ergosterol content of both C. albicans and C. dubliniensis type strains, increasing susceptibility to the antifungals. Pre-treatment of biofilms with PUFAs may result in the reduction in antifungal dose needed to inhibit biofilms**.

### chocolate — 2012-07-03T21:51:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-arachidonic-acid.2162/post-35332>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10901420](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10901420)

**Dietary cod liver oil decreases arachidonic acid in rat gastric mucosa and increases stress-induced gastric erosions.

> Science Institute, University of Iceland, Reykjavik.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The purpose of this study was to examine the influence of long-term feeding of dietary fat rich in either n-3 or n-6 fatty acids on the availability of arachidonic acid (20:4n-6) in major phospholipids of gastric mucosa in rats. Three groups of male Wistar rats were fed either a standard diet, a cod liver oil-enriched diet (10% by weight), or a corn oil-enriched diet (10% by weight) for 8 mon. Dietary cod liver oil significantly reduced the level of 20:4n-6 in phosphatidylcholine (PC) and in phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) of gastric mucosa. The loss of 20:4n-6 was compensated for by eicosapentaenoic acid (20:5n-3) in PC, whereas the decrease in 20:4n-6 in PE corresponded to the increase in three n-3 fatty acids: 20:5n-3, docosapentaenoic acid (22:5n-3), and docosahexaenoic acid (22:6n-3). The level of 20:5n-3 was higher than the level of 22:6n-3 both in PC and PE of mucosa in rats fed cod liver oil. Diets supplemented with corn oil increased the level of 18:2n-6 but decreased the monoene fatty acids 16:1 and 18:1n-7 in PC but not in PE of gastric mucosa. The 20:4n-6 levels of both PC and PE were markedly reduced by dietary cod liver oil, to about one-third of control levels. Similar changes were also observed in the stomach wall. Gastric erosions were observed in all rats exposed to restriction stress, but this form of stress induced twice the number of erosions in rats fed fish oil compared to control rats or rats fed corn oil. We conclude that a diet rich in fish oil altered the balance between n-6 and n-3 fatty acids in major gastric mucosal phospholipids, markedly reduced the availability of 20:4n-6, and increased the incidence of gastric erosions induced by restriction or emotional stress.

**

# Thread: Fruit how much is too much
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-02T22:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35113>

So I've been eating some fruit with yogurt and today I made a little smoothy with a little milk, strawberries, blueberries, raspberries, raw unfiltered honey, and maple syrup. It was probably 1/4 cup yogurt, 2 handfuls of blueberries...6 strawberries, a handful of raspberries and about a tablespoon of honey and maple syrup. Probably about 2 cups of milk.

I didn't have precise measurements....anyway my blood sugar went from 90 to 117 or so at an hour and I'm not sure if that's a ton or not.

I did do a little dumbbell lifting and walked the dog down the blog to use a bit of the sugar so it wouldn't spike a ton but nothing really intense.

Anyone have any recommendations on sticking to a certain amount of ounces or other measurement of fruits? I know blueberries are a bit higher in sugar and maybe I shouldn't have done 2 handfuls but we have tons and I don't want them to ruin.

### AKMan — 2012-07-02T22:58:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35117>

> **Raiken3712;35273 wrote:** So I've been eating some fruit with yogurt and today I made a little smoothy with a little milk, strawberries, blueberries, raspberries, raw unfiltered honey, and maple syrup. It was probably 1/4 cup yogurt, 2 handfuls of blueberries...6 strawberries, a handful of raspberries and about a tablespoon of honey and maple syrup. Probably about 2 cups of milk.
>
>
>
> I didn't have precise measurements....anyway my blood sugar went from 90 to 117 or so at an hour and I'm not sure if that's a ton or not.
>
>
>
> I did do a little dumbbell lifting and walked the dog down the blog to use a bit of the sugar so it wouldn't spike a ton but nothing really intense.
>
>
>
> Anyone have any recommendations on sticking to a certain amount of ounces or other measurement of fruits? I know blueberries are a bit higher in sugar and maybe I shouldn't have done 2 handfuls but we have tons and I don't want them to ruin.

I think that in July in Texas you can eat as much fruit as you want! Eat a whole watermelon if you can. Seriously, this is the time to enjoy fruit. Upper limit probably depends on you and your goals or problems. If you are overweight and trying to lose fat or have other metabolic derangements you should limit or eat none at all. If you are just trying to be as healthy as you can--eat up.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-03T00:12:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35139>

Actually July in Texas is late for berries, AKMan. Melon are in season, however. I had a fling with berries between spring equinox and summer solstice, and my scale sure let me know about it.

Raiken, how about the milk? That has lots of sugars in it. With the honey and syrup, berries, and milk, that smoothy probably was a significant amount of carbs.

How about freezing the blueberries? If you put them on a plate and freeze them, you can then toss them into a bag, and they will stay separate from each other. I have to measure berries; a quarter cup looks so insignificant...

### Glamazon — 2012-07-03T03:26:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35191>

My triglycerides shot up when I was eating on the Dr. Fuhrman plan last year because there is so much fruit. I backed down to an occasional splurge (more than a serving, till I was full.) once a week or two and my triglycerides started to plummet. I eat my fruit with nothing added, plain. But, you seem to have a lot of high glycemic carbs in what you are eating. I would imagine what it is you are trying to accomplish in the context of what your state of health is at this moment based on labs is what will give you the clearest actionable picture.

### Michael — 2012-07-03T10:46:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35254>

> **Raiken3712;35273 wrote:** Anyone have any recommendations on sticking to a certain amount of ounces or other measurement of fruits? I know blueberries are a bit higher in sugar and maybe I shouldn't have done 2 handfuls but we have tons and I don't want them to ruin.

A fairly standard recommendation is one to three small servings of (preferably low-sugar) fruit. Less for people who are really metabolically deranged.

For example, that's the recommendation from Dr. Terry Wahls (the medical doctor who seems to be curing herself of multiple sclerosis with a paleo diet). But, as I say, it's a fairly standard recommendation.

I wouldn't on the whole smash them up in a blender. Better to eat them whole. That way the sugar gets in the bloodstream more slowly. And probably better not to add honey or maple sugar, since that's additional sugar, nor milk (since that's more sugar: lactose (milk-sugar) ) ... let alone all three.

Sure, you can follow the _Lights Out_ notion and eat a little more fruit when it's in season, but in much of the globe there's only fruit around for 6 to 8 weeks, and most of it is not as sugar-laden as modern highly-bred varieties. And if you were really eating on a seasonal basis, you'd be avoiding it completely for most of the year.

If you're growing fruit and you've got a surplus, you could freeze it.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-03T17:55:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35292>

> **Nonchalant;35299 wrote:** Actually July in Texas is late for berries, AKMan. Melon are in season, however. I had a fling with berries between spring equinox and summer solstice, and my scale sure let me know about it.
>
>
>
> Raiken, how about the milk? That has lots of sugars in it. With the honey and syrup, berries, and milk, that smoothy probably was a significant amount of carbs.
>
>
>
> How about freezing the blueberries? If you put them on a plate and freeze them, you can then toss them into a bag, and they will stay separate from each other. I have to measure berries; a quarter cup looks so insignificant...

Yeah it was a decent amount of carbohydrates...not something I do everyday and don't plan on doing the honey/maple thing everytime...just did it on a whim. I've heard raw unfiltered honey and grade b maple syrup can be good for you in small amounts. The milk does have some sugar too I suppose just added it because I don't drink milk all that often.

Next time I"ll probably just do the fruit and a bit of yogurt with no milk, honey, or maple. I didn't think of freezing the blueberries thanks.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-03T18:06:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35293>

> **Michael;35414 wrote:** A fairly standard recommendation is one to three small servings of (preferably low-sugar) fruit. Less for people who are really metabolically deranged.
>
>
>
> For example, that's the recommendation from Dr. Terry Wahls (the medical doctor who seems to be curing herself of multiple sclerosis with a paleo diet). But, as I say, it's a fairly standard recommendation.
>
>
>
> I wouldn't on the whole smash them up in a blender. Better to eat them whole. That way the sugar gets in the bloodstream more slowly. And probably better not to add honey or maple sugar, since that's additional sugar, nor milk (since that's more sugar: lactose (milk-sugar) ) ... let alone all three.
>
>
>
> Sure, you can follow the _Lights Out_ notion and eat a little more fruit when it's in season, but in much of the globe there's only fruit around for 6 to 8 weeks, and most of it is not as sugar-laden as modern highly-bred varieties. And if you were really eating on a seasonal basis, you'd be avoiding it completely for most of the year.
>
>
>
> If you're growing fruit and you've got a surplus, you could freeze it.

Except for the blueberries the rest are low sugar fruits aren't they? I thought blackberries, raspberries, and strawberries were decently low. What is a small serving? How many ounces...is ounces the typical measurement for that kinda thing? Also is there somewhere that lists whats in season in different areas I'd love to look at that for curiosity.

No not growing fruit my mom just bought a rather large box of blueberries 2 pounds worth.

Also how does that blood sugar spike sound for that combination? I guess I should have measured at 2 hours to see if it went up or down by that point...that might make a difference.

### MartiD — 2012-07-03T19:47:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35305>

I have been adding a handful of blueberries to GY for a few months. I hate to limit fruits in Summer especially when it is so hot out. There is nothing more refreshing than cold fruit in the hot summer! I have tried to keep to the occassional blueberries and not much else but with the holiday tomorrow I need to take something to a BBQ and thought I would take a fruit bowl. I know it is something I can snack on and keep me away from the envitable chips and other starchy snacks that will be present. I would be much better off snacking on fruit than anything else!

I need to read up on fruits and which have lowest index. I have always known berries are better than bananas but wonder how the cantalopes fall? I assume higher? I also love peaches but assume those are the best either.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-03T20:17:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35309>

you can see how much sugar is in a fruit here

[http://www.fitsugar.com/Sugar-Content-Fruit-20134844](http://http://www.fitsugar.com/Sugar-Content-Fruit-20134844)

but I think it's glycemic index that is more important. For example, watermelon is low in sugar but it's basically no pulp, so it's gonna raise your blood sugar more than say an apple

[http://www.weightlossforall.com/glyemic-list-fruits-vegs.htm](http://http://www.weightlossforall.com/glyemic-list-fruits-vegs.htm)

Then there is the whole FODMAP stuff

[http://blog.katescarlata.com/wp-content/uploads/2011/04/fodmapschecklist-7.jpg](http://http://blog.katescarlata.com/wp-content/uploads/2011/04/fodmapschecklist-7.jpg)

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-03T21:34:40+0300

### Michael — 2012-07-03T23:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fruit-how-much-is-too-much.2164/post-35356>

> **Raiken3712;35453 wrote:** Except for the blueberries the rest are low sugar fruits aren't they? I thought blackberries, raspberries, and strawberries were decently low.
>
>
> Yeah, berries of all sorts are typically low, because they have plenty of water and plenty of fiber.
>
>
>
> Grapefruit isn't too bad either, nor peaches. Stuff like pineapple and watermelon is a bit higher, IIRC. You could check tables of glycemic index/load -- e.g:
>
>
>
> [http://www.ajcn.org/content/76/1/5.full.pdf](http://http://www.ajcn.org/content/76/1/5.full.pdf)
>
>
>
> But that's probably getting over the top unbless you've got problems. And it can be misleading since glycemic index is, effectively, a measure of how much glucose is in the fruit, while fructose is actually a bigger problem metabolically. So people who eat crap like agave "nectar" -- advertsing name for syrup -- congratulating themselves on its GI value being lower than, say, honey are rather like someone who should say of a armed intruder, "it's OK; he's not got a knife," when he's carrying a sawn-off shotgun.
>
>
>
>
> > What is a small serving? How many ounces...is ounces the typical measurement for that kinda thing?
> >
> >
> > Something like an equivalent in size to a small apple. In terms of ounces maybe 3 or 4. I just checked and four ounces of raspberries would contain about 13.5 grams of carbohydrate. That's going to be OK even for people on quite carb-restricted diets - though people trying to keep under 20 grams of carbs a day might find it a bit tight.
> >
> >
> >
> > You could use ounces or grams or rough measures like "as big as a small apple" or "a handful". And, of course, a lot of food tends to be measured in "cups" in the U.S. (i.e. 8 fluid ounce containers - or a U.S. half-pint), though weights are preferred in many other countries.
> >
> >
> >
> > Fitday tells me that a cup of blueberries is 21 grams of carbs and 4 ounces of blueberries about 16.4 g.
> >
> >
> >
> > Also is there somewhere that lists whats in season in different areas I'd love to look at that for curiosity.
>
>
>
> Sorry, I don't know. Anyway, while it may be good to eat seasonal produce, I don't know whether you'd really have to match it to your precise location - except insofar as what you buy locally and in season will be fresh. I mean I don't know that you have to be attuned that closely to your precise spot on the earth's surface.
>
>
>
> Here's an interesting one: Jimmy Moore recently had an academic who researches dental health - Phillipe Hugejoel - on his podcast. Anyway, he said that people born nearer to the equator tend to have thicker enamel on their teeth, and that seems to be genetic. Hugejoel suggests that that might make people whose ancestors lived in that area more resistant to the ravages of the larger percentage of fruit that would be in the diet. It sounds plausible. So, if your ancestors came from, say, Holland, and you moved near the equator would you be advised to eat lots of fruit, bearing in mind that you'd have genetically "northern" teeth? I'd think not. I guess that would be a question for Afrikaaners.
>
>
>
> Also how does that blood sugar spike sound for that combination? I guess I should have measured at 2 hours to see if it went up or down by that point...that might make a difference.

Not something I can answer. I haven't a lot of experinece looking at blood-sugar numbers.

# Thread: candida and psoriasis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-psoriasis.2166/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-02T23:33:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-psoriasis.2166/post-35124>

[http://www.drhoffman.com/page.cfm/616](http://http://www.drhoffman.com/page.cfm/616)

Candida

by Leyla Muedin, MS, RD, CDN

Candida albicans are a species of yeast that normally reside in the GI tract. It is only when overgrowth occurs, or allergy to candida develops, that trouble begins. Candida is an opportunistic fungus which feeds primarily on sugars and other yeast-containing foods (foods that are aged, pickled, dried, fermented or cured). Actually, the problem with yeast-containing foods is that they are "seen" by the immune system of a person with candida as foreign invaders, hence triggering reaction. Overgrowth of candida is also caused by stress, use of antibiotics, steroids and oral contraceptives. It is the overgrowth of candida in the gut that can lead to a myriad of symptoms, and it is more often than not the cause of the latest GI disorder on the medical map, Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS).

Symptoms of IBS can include constipation alternating with diarrhea, gas, bloating and cramping particularly in the lower abdomen with unpredictable bowel movements and urges. This can be a source of much discomfort, not to mention the unhappiness that comes with a social calendar becoming contingent upon the availability of a bathroom. T**ypically, the dysbiosis (bacterial imbalance) that occurs with IBS starts with the invasion of multiple colonies of candida in the gut, followed by toxicity. Yeasts give off many toxins, like zymosan which causes inflammation.

_**Research conducted at the University of Tennessee in Memphis found that zymosan is found to cause much of the inflammation associated with psoriasi**_**s. Another toxin called arabinitol is known to produce toxic effects on the brain, nervous system and immune system in animal studies. Numerous other toxins produced by yeasts may explain the memory retention problems, feeling "drunk", hormone disturbances, fatigue and depression experienced by so many with yeast problems.**

It is worth noting that over ninety percent of a healthy population is "allergic" to candida. How can that be if it normally resides in the body? The delayed hypersensitivity (Type 4 allergy) response by the immune system is what helps the body control yeast overgrowth. This is a normal and protective response to yeast but sometimes it gets out of hand. An immediate hypersensitivity response to candida (Type 1 allergy), which is found in approximately 10 percent of the population, can cause hives, asthma, eczema, chronic vaginitis, abdominal cramps, and diarrhea. In these cases, immunotherapy with sublingual drops of varying levels of attenuated candida extracts successfully neutralizes the immune response, abating the hypersensitivity.

Other abnormal or unusual allergic reactions to yeast may trigger autoimmune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis, thyroiditis, or celiac disease. A recent study published in the Lancet showed candida contains a protein called HWP-1 which is similar in its structure to gluten. A candida infection in the gut can cause an immune system reaction to HWP-1, which then stimulates an allergic reaction to the gluten in wheat and other grains and may trigger celiac disease in genetically-susceptible people.** Under these circumstances, a gluten-free diet alone may not do the trick in relieving symptoms in a patient with celiac disease. A yeast-free diet with antifungal supplements such as Olive Leaf Extract, Oregacillin and/or medications such as Nystatin, Diflucan, Sporanox or Lamisil may be necessary. Other studies assert that candida is a sensitizing agent. Rats raised with regular doses of antibiotics, when inoculated with live candida, were found to develop allergies to mold, dust and pollen.>

Wiping out yeast overgrowth in the gut requires starvation (of the yeast, not the patient!) and elimination with a yeast-free, sugar-free diet and antifungal supplements. During this detoxification period, re-inoculation of the gut with healthy probiotics such as Dr. Ohhira's Probiotics Original Formula, Culturelle, or Vital 10 is critical. Dysbiosis caused by candida overgrowth warrants the consistent use of these probiotics so that bacterial balance may be re-established and healthy gut ecology maintained.

While both sexes with yeast overgrowth may share conditions such as sinusitis, asthma and joint pain, several conditions are exclusive to women, particularly those between the ages of 20 to 55. Among candida-aware health practitioners, the following are considered yeast-related health problems:

PMS

Recurrent vaginal yeast infections

Vulvodynia (burning vulva)

Recurrent urinary tract infections

Endometriosis

> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zymosan](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zymosan)
>
>
> > Zymosan is a glucan with repeating glucose units connected by β-1,3-glycosidic linkages. It binds to TLR 2.\[1\]
> >
> >
> >
> > Zymosan is prepared from yeast cell wall and consists of protein-carbohydrate complexes. It is used to induce experimental sterile inflammation. In macrophages, zymosan-induced responses include the induction of proinflammatory cytokines, arachidonate mobilization, protein phosphorylation, and inositol phosphate formation. Zymosan A also raises cyclin D2 levels suggesting a role for the latter in macrophage activation besides proliferation. **It potentiates acute liver damage after galactosamine injection suggesting that certain types of nonparenchymal cells other than Kupffer cells are involved in zymosan action.\[2\]**

### chocolate — 2012-07-09T20:18:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-psoriasis.2166/post-36360>

Does Zymosan kill cancer? I know candida tries to starve cancer by eating the same foods. Who knows this stuff?

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16543948](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16543948)

> Yeast zymosan, a stimulus for TLR2 and dectin-1, induces regulatory antigen-presenting cells and immunological tolerance.
>
> Dillon S, Agrawal S, Banerjee K, Letterio J, Denning TL, Oswald-Richter K, Kasprowicz DJ, Kellar K, Pare J, van Dyke T, Ziegler S, Unutmaz D, Pulendran B.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Emory Vaccine Research Center and Department of Pathology, Emory University, Atlanta, Georgia 30329, USA.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Emerging evidence suggests critical roles for APCs in suppressing immune responses. Here, we show that zymosan, a stimulus for TLR2 and dectin-1, regulates cytokine secretion in DCs and macrophages to induce immunological tolerance. First, zymosan induces DCs to secrete abundant IL-10 but little IL-6 and IL-12(p70). Induction of IL-10 is dependent on TLR2- and dectin-1-mediated activation of ERK MAPK via a mechanism independent of the activation protein 1 (AP-1) transcription factor c-Fos. **Such DCs **stimulate antigen-specific CD4+ T cells poorly due to IL-10 and the lack of IL-6. Second, zymosan induces F4-80+ macrophages in the splenic red pulp to secrete TGF-beta. Consistent with these effects on APCs, injection of zymosan plus OVA into mice results in OVA-specific T cells that secrete little or no Th1 or Th2 cytokines, but secrete robust levels of IL-10, and are unresponsive to challenge with OVA plus adjuvant. **Finally, coinjection of zymosan with OVA plus LPS suppresses the response to OVA via a** mechanism dependent on IL-10, TGF-beta, and lack of IL-6.** Together, our data demonstrate that zymosan stimulates IL-10+ IL-12(p70)- IL-6low regulatory DCs and TGF-beta+ macrophages to induce immunological tolerance. These data suggest several targets for pharmacological modulation of immune responses in various clinical settings.**

### chocolate — 2012-07-09T20:21:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-psoriasis.2166/post-36362>

[http://www.invivogen.com/zymosan](http://http://www.invivogen.com/zymosan)

> Zymosan
>
> Cell wall preparation of S. cerevisiae
>
>
>
> Zymosan, an insoluble preparation of cell wall from Saccharomyces cerevisiae, activates macrophages via TLR2. TLR2 cooperates with TLR6 and CD14 in response to zymosan \[1\]. Zymosan is also recognized by Dectin-1, a phagocytic receptor expressed on macrophages and dendritic cells, which collaborates with TLR2 and TLR6 enhancing the immune responses triggered by the recognition of Zymosan by each receptor \[2\].

# Thread: Comparing Thyroid results

### Destiny — 2012-07-03T01:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35157>

I just found out that my family doctor ran a thyroid test on me last fall. At least, I have something to compare it with this year's one. Not sure what to make of it. The TSH result was better in 2011 but the rest was worse, no?

**Nov 2011

TSH **3.59 (0.4-4.5 mIU/L

T4, Total **7.0 (4.5-12.0 mcg/dL

T3 uptake **32 (22.35 %)

T4, Free, Calculated **2.2 (1.4-3.8 Units)

T3, Total **77 (76-181 ng/dL)

**April 2012**

TSH - 4.29** (0.27-4.20 uIU/ml)

T4 - 9.6** (4.5-12.0 ug/dl)

FT4 - 1.27** (0.83-1.62 ng/dl)

Free T4 Index 3.3** (1.5 -3.8)

T3 Total 86** (80-200ng/dl)

T3 Uptake 34.1** (24.3- 39.0 % )

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T01:18:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35165>

Oh boy, I'm not sure with these labs. If just looking at the TSH you are heading toward less thyroid function. It's too bad you don't have the FT3. But you do have FT4 which tells you how much you have that MAY convert to T3 but you don't know how much because you don't have that lab. The one value is calculated so I don't know if that is a valuable reading. Also just looking at your T3 it's way too low. I like my FT3 at the top of the range....top of the top quartile!  Hope that helps a little.

### Destiny — 2012-07-03T01:46:50+0300

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T02:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35175>

I don't want to discourage you but I have had my own personal struggle with endos. I hope yours goes better. But remember if he/she is useless just check the name off the list and try to find someone who can help. I ended up with a hormone specialist (anti-aging) for treatment of thyroid. I highly recommend an anti-aging doc as that is pretty much all they do...hormones! Sometimes an Ob-Gyn or even an Ob-Gyn NP can be helpful too. Good luck!

### Destiny — 2012-07-03T02:30:34+0300

I picked a young endo hoping that she will be more up to date. I have an OB GYN who prescribes BHRT (not some common in the Tristate area). She also told me in April that my TSH was bordeline but still OK. I'll keep searching.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T21:06:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35320>

I would definitely ask for a TPO and Thyroglobulin antibodies test to rule out Hashimotos from your endo. If she won't authorize try and get it from your ObGyn. If you cannot get it there go get it yourself from an online lab. You just want to make sure. As I'm typing this I'm thinking you may have already had those done. Maybe not???

### Destiny — 2012-07-03T22:43:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35343>

> **Patty Cakes;35481 wrote:** I would definitely ask for a TPO and Thyroglobulin antibodies test to rule out Hashimotos from your endo. If she won't authorize try and get it from your ObGyn. If you cannot get it there go get it yourself from an online lab. You just want to make sure. As I'm typing this I'm thinking you may have already had those done. Maybe not???

I had my appointment with an endo today. The endo promised to run the entire thyroid test including the antibodies. I believe it when I see it. The whole thing with her is a no go for the future.

First, she crushed me when she told me that she is not really concerned about any TSH below 10! When I told her that I am sluggish all the time, she gave me a prescription for Synthroid 25 mg per day. I am not going to take it.

The next thing was that she had no idea about what the BHRT Triest cream is. When I told her, she told me Premarin is much better than some weak cream. Also my spiky nocturnal cortisol did not concern her at all. According to her, it was a sort of in line. The last straw was that she retested me for my toxic levels of vitamin D that is at 114. She was terrified by that number - her words not mine.

In addition, she looked herself very unhealthy like she had a very hyper thyroid. It is so disappointing but at least, let's hope, I will have some test results.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T23:24:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35353>

At least you will get some tests. That is the positive to take away from the visit. One down. I have called the doc's office and asked if they script natural thyroid meds or T3 and many have said no. You could add the tri-est, bi-est question to it. It may help to screen through a lot of docs without a consultation visit.

# Thread: Frigid Lake Tahoe--A Safety Reminder
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frigid-lake-tahoe-a-safety-reminder.2171/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2012-07-03T01:41:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frigid-lake-tahoe-a-safety-reminder.2171/post-35171>

A reminder of the critical importance of understanding the conditions of both the environment and one's own body when venturing into cold, deep waters . . .

Presumably the three June 2012 drowning victims were not cold-adapted. Dive Center says Tahoe water temp today is 52-59F at the surface, though it gets much colder at depth--"Below 600-700 foot depths, the water temperature remains a constant 39 F."

A neighbor witnessed the [incident at Camp Richardson](http://http://www.tahoedailytribune.com/article/20120605/NEWS/120609966) in which a young man in apparent good health jumped off a boat, tried to swim but sank in the cold water. Friends thought he was joking and came to his aid too late. CPR could not revive him. "Authorities believe Lake Tahoe's frigid waters contributed to \[his\] inability to reach the beach. The passenger who left the boat with \[him\] remained near the vessel and showed signs of being affected by the water's temperature, Van Arnum said."

The other two have an air of mystery--[one not witnessed at all](http://http://www.tahoedailytribune.com/article/20120625/ARCHIVES01/120629955), the other a [sky-diving accident](http://http://www.tahoebonanza.com/article/20120608/NEWS/120609964) that landed the victim out in the lake. His parachute may have been a contributing factor but the shock of cold water surely didn't help.

Important to know: [Drowning doesn't look like drowning](http://http://unofficialnetworks.com/drowning-drowning-important-read-summer-98693/).

### Melinda — 2012-07-03T03:00:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frigid-lake-tahoe-a-safety-reminder.2171/post-35185>

That's a good reminder Gracelind. I was thinking of putting a warning on a few of the ocean CT threads after reading FreetheAnimal's moving memorial to his longtime friend who was super fit but drowned off the coast of Florida in a rip tide. Always have a buddy along or watchers on the beach and stay close to shore until you get a few years of experience with the ocean conditions.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-03T16:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frigid-lake-tahoe-a-safety-reminder.2171/post-35273>

If you aren't adjusted to the mammalian dive reflex, it can certainly instill a sense of panic. I was panicked the first time I put my face in a bowl of ice water - I can't imagine jumping in a deepwater lake and not expecting it. Terrible tragedy.

# Thread: No cardio ever...ever?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 19

### Eve — 2012-07-03T02:27:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-35178>

It's quite clear one should not do cardio when one is LR (e.g., why is oprah fat post)...but is cardio reasonable when one is LS? I get the impression that Dr. K doesn't recommend cardio ever aside from sprints (and walking or swimming etc). I'm getting confused after reading some other threads in this forum mentioning 5ks and 10ks.

I really miss running/jogging. I'm still LR now, so wouldn't dream of doing it...but I was hopping when I finally become LS that I could start up again...nothing crazy...maybe a jog/run 40min 2x a week max. I find it really relaxing and a lot more fun than boring walking.

### Barry — 2012-07-03T16:48:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-35274>

>
>
> I really miss running/jogging. I'm still LR now, so wouldn't dream of doing it...but I was hopping when I finally become LS that I could start up again...nothing crazy...maybe a jog/run 40min 2x a week max. I find it really relaxing and a lot more fun than boring walking.

You just answered your own question by your last sentence !! That thought process/experience is what counts, that = Epigenetics.

And if any paleo meatheads try to tell you otherwise, they haven't got a clue what they are talking about ;-)

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-03T16:53:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-35275>

move slowly is the optimal way if you want to do cardio. that said if your adrenals are ok. and you're doing it at the optimal wo time btwn 3-6pm and all other medical issues are resolved - there's no reason why you can't do a long slow run occasionally - the thing you've got to watch out for is the "high" you get - and think its ok to do more. I love the HIITs I do - but I have to be careful - when I'm stressed I'll skip them. no need to do them and raise cortisol and inflammation. you're biggest gains in fitness will come from Lifting Heavy Things, and the occasional HIIT. with bike riding (not spin class), swimming, walking or hiking.

I've gotten back into swimming - and realized that I have to make myself go slowly - not push the intensity. Its easy to push the intensity in any form of exercise.

### AKMan — 2012-07-03T20:52:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-35316>

I used to love to run. Poor diet and metabolism problems caught up with me a few years back and I had to quit running. Now, thanks to Dr. Kruse and a few others, I am in the best shape of my life at 46. I started running again this summer and it feels great! I can run 3 miles in about 25 minutes. Now that I've seen what I can do, my plan for the rest of the summer is this: Brisk walk 1/2 mile (8 minutes), run all-out 1 mile (6-8 minutes), brisk walk 1/2 mile with a few 100yd sprints thrown in (6-8 minutes).

Total time is under 25 minutes for this workout. It doesn't feel like a 3 mile run at all, and I think I get way more out of it. I'm only doing it once a week, so getting plenty of recovery time. I think this is a great alternative to the chronic cardio style running most people do.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-26T18:07:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41209>

So what's the consensus here? I usually sign up for a annual 5k in my area. It is a fun time.

I'm not a fast runner, I just try not to come in last.

Is this harming me?

### Ray — 2012-07-26T18:24:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41213>

Isn't the hypothesis here something like: more cardio -> more oxidative stress -> more cell death -> more stem cell depletion

### quelsen — 2012-07-26T19:05:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41237>

as long as one understands that

"health provides energy for movement"

and that is IS NOT

"movement provides energy for health"

the rest is up to you.

Exercise has its place. however as with anything it can become damaging to you.

if you accept that then you will find your own sweet spot.

run is you want, or not.

bear in mind Body by Science ( for women ) in normal everyday stuff.

treat 5k's and above like your 20% note your 80%

### Lyndra — 2012-07-26T19:38:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41254>

One question for me is what is considered cardio? Is it only the high-paced endurance activity, say 75% of your max? Can one follow a Maffetone-type program (pure aerobic, low heart rate) more safely? Once I can get back to exercising, my plan is to do the Maffetone approach and build endurance at a low heart rate and not worry about being "fast."

### Stephen — 2012-07-27T04:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41433>

> **Lyndra;41471 wrote:** One question for me is what is considered cardio? Is it only the high-paced endurance activity, say 75% of your max? Can one follow a Maffetone-type program (pure aerobic, low heart rate) more safely? Once I can get back to exercising, my plan is to do the Maffetone approach and build endurance at a low heart rate and not worry about being "fast."

Personally I think this is an ok idea. My personal opinion is just don't go doing it every day of the week or for extended sessions. That is unless you want to compete in something. Then recognise that in general athletic performance does not = health.

For far too many the way they approach exercise is in a way such that their highs are too low and their lows are too high. 180bpm - age is a good place to target to stay below for pure aerobic, low heart rate training. Take another 5bpm off if you're just getting back into it or you get sick more than twice a year. Depending on the person this heart rate target (energy systems training) may well require walking, especially when going up hills.

Stephen

# Thread: Risk of alcohol abuse may increase after bariatric surgery
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery.2175/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-03T03:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery.2175/post-35192>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/06/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery-12300](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/06/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery-12300)

Among patients who underwent bariatric surgery, there was a higher prevalence of alcohol use disorders in the second year after surgery, and specifically after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, compared with the years immediately before and following surgery, according to a study in the June 20 issue of JAMA. This study is being published early online to coincide with its presentation at the annual meeting of the American Society for Metabolic and Bariatric Surgery.

â€œAs the prevalence of severe obesity increases in the United States, it is becoming increasingly common for health care providers and their patients to consider bariatric surgery, which is the most effective and durable treatment for severe obesity. Although bariatric surgery may reduce long-term mortality, and it carries a low risk of short-term serious adverse outcomes, safety concerns remain. Anecdotal reports suggest that bariatric surgery may increase the risk for alcohol use disorders (AUD; i.e., alcohol abuse and dependence),â€￾ according to background information in the article.

The authors add that there is evidence that some bariatric surgical procedures (i.e., Roux-en-Y gastric bypass \[RYGB\] and sleeve gastrectomy) alter the pharmacokinetics of alcohol. â€œGiven a standardized quantity of alcohol, patients reach a higher peak alcohol level after surgery compared with case-controls or their preoperative levels.â€￾

Wendy C. King, Ph.D., of the University of Pittsburgh, and colleagues conducted a study to determine whether the prevalence of AUD changed following bariatric surgery, comparing reported AUD in the year prior to surgery with the first and second years after surgery. The prospective study included 2,458 adults who underwent bariatric surgery at 10 U.S. hospitals. Of these participants, 1,945 (78.8 percent female; 87 percent white; median \[midpoint\] age, 47 years; median body mass index, 45.8) completed preoperative and postoperative (at 1 year and/or 2 years) assessments between 2006 and 2011. The primary outcome measure for the study was past year AUD symptoms determined with the Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (AUDIT) (indication of alcohol-related harm, alcohol dependence symptoms, or score 8 or greater).

The researchers found that the prevalence of AUD symptoms did not significantly differ from 1 year before to 1 year after bariatric surgery (7.6 percent vs. 7.3 percent), but was significantly higher in the second postoperative year (9.6 percent). Frequency of alcohol consumption and AUD significantly increased in the second postoperative year compared with the year prior to surgery or the first postoperative year.

â€œMore than half (66/106; 62.3 percent) of thosereporting AUD at the preoperative assessment continued to have or had recurrent AUD within the first 2 postoperative years,â€￾ the authors write. â€œIn contrast, 7.9 percent (101/1,283) of participants not reporting AUD at the preoperative assessment had postoperative AUD. Nonetheless, more than half (101/167; 60.5 percent) of postoperative AUD was reported by those not reporting AUD at the preoperative assessmentâ€￾

The researchers also found that male sex, younger age, smoking, regular alcohol consumption, AUD, recreational drug use, lower score on a measure of a sense of belonging at the preoperative assessment and undergoing a RYGB were independently related to an increased likelihood of AUD after surgery. RYGB accounted for 70 percent of surgeries and doubled the likelihood of postoperative AUD compared with laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding.

The authors note that although the 2 percent increase (7.6 percent to 9.6 percent) in prevalence of AUD from prior to surgery to the 2-year postoperative assessment may seem small, the increase potentially represents more than 2,000 additional people with AUD in the United States each year, with accompanying personal, financial, and societal costs.

â€œThis study has important implications for the care of patients who undergo bariatric surgery. Regardless of alcohol history, patients should be educated about the potential effects of bariatric surgery, in particular RYGB, to increase the risk of AUD. In addition, alcohol screening and, if indicated, referral should be offered as part of routine preoperative and postoperative clinical care. Further research should examine the long-term effect of bariatric surgery on AUD, and the relationship of AUD to postoperative weight control.â€￾

My doctor just recommends eating as though you've had a bypass.... I have a facebook friend that lost 40 lbs in two months following the menu for bypass. She quit but she didn't gain it back. I think she quit when her clothes fit. I think being too broke for bigger clothes was her motivation.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-03T04:28:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery.2175/post-35216>

bariatric surgery carries massive depression and suicide risks.....close to 40%. Pass on this option.

# Thread: What does a little baby (about 8 months) eat when she is sick?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-does-a-little-baby-about-8-months-eat-when-she-is-sick.2176/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-07-03T03:31:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-does-a-little-baby-about-8-months-eat-when-she-is-sick.2176/post-35193>

My friend posted this about his little baby girl recently on Face book:

_Two days of very high fever with the girl. Mom and dad are suffering. Most remarkable event of the whole thing? We don't want watermelon. We don't want vegetables of any kind. We don't want juice. We don't want water. What do we want? Pureed roasted chicken legs and thighs, with the skin and fat, mixed with homemade chicken bone broth. We'll eat as much of that as they'll give us._

Kids know what makes them healthy. His parents are paleo-ish/Weston Price eaters and yoga instructors. His girl was running close to 105 degrees.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-07T02:16:13+0300

Funny! Wih I could get my 13 month olds to eat some of those foods. They'll eat a little tiny bit of sweet potato and egg. They like to gnaw on steak and spit it out. Won't have anything to do with purees and LOVE mama milk! Oy, nursing times too! Hard to believe a baby that mall eats o well!

# Thread: B's on the train to optimal : )
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bs-on-the-train-to-optimal.2177/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-07-03T03:33:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bs-on-the-train-to-optimal.2177/post-35195>

Ok, so here goes nothing! Iâ€™ve been thinking about doing a journal for a long time, but I kept resisting. I think it scares me in a way to be so open and honest about everything, but Iâ€™ve realized that not only do I need and want to be held accountable I also want to be able to paleo it forward somehow. I try to do that in my everyday life, but Iâ€™ve learned so much about my journey to optimal by reading othersâ€™ stories and posts and it occurred to me that I need to put myself and my story out there too so that maybe even one person can find something helpful in it.

**Background**

For as long as I can remember Iâ€™ve always had less energy and just generally dealt with more fatigue than others and was finally diagnosed with hypothyroidism around age 16-17 and put on synthroid. â€œIrritable bowel syndromeâ€ runs in my family and I dealt with that a bit growing up as well, but nothing really major. PMS was pretty bad for me with lots of cramping and moodiness, but overall I actually had a fairly healthy and unremarkable childhood.

I have two beautiful kids and felt the best Iâ€™d ever felt in my life while pregnant . . . both times my husband joked he was going to keep me pregnant forever! Too bad for him my libido took a major nosedive after the second one ; ) (I told this to Dr. K prior to my consult and it was a major clue for him on what was happening with me.)

Onto the good stuff: It was about 2.5 years ago that my health majorly crashed. I began eating a vegetarian diet and was doing a super intense workout program. I had never really exercised in my life; hated any form of it really. Thankfully Iâ€™d never had a weight problem so I didnâ€™t have to worry much about it, but I had a bit more trouble taking the weight off after my second baby so I actually put some effort into it ; ) For a while I felt wonderful â€“ I remember waking super early every morning and feeling like I could take on the world. Things were fantastic!

Then all of the sudden I started to develop numerous food intolerances and crazy reactions â€“ rashes, stomach pains, etc, etc. First it was soy, then quinoa, gluten, corn and then dairy. It seemed like every 6 weeks or so there was a new food intolerance creeping up. I began an elimination diet and literally felt like I was reacting to EVERYTHING! It was terrifying. I didnâ€™t know what to eat.

Long story short â€“ I ended up on an anti-candida diet and then GAPS. I definitely got worse eating the allowed grains on the anti-candida diet. I do think GAPS helped in some areas, but also set me back in some ways too. Saw some ridiculous doctors â€“ told I had early signs of MS, muscle wasting disease, all kinds of horrific things. One doc sent me away and said there was nothing he could do for me â€“ the last few years have been rough to say the least. My main problems were (and I say were because things are finally looking up!) :

â€¢ Major fatigue and muscle weakness â€“ so bad that I could barely lift my arms to wash my hair at times or even get one leg in front of the other to walk

â€¢ Memory loss â€“ this has been a gradual thing for the last 10 or so years

â€¢ Impaired cognitive function

â€¢ Gain weight anytime my carbs go above 30-40 grams/day

â€¢ Complete lack of libido â€“ I wish it were just a decrease, but I got nothinâ€™ right now

â€¢ Constipation â€“ never had this in my life until I started GAPS

â€¢ Food intolerances mentioned above, i.e. EXTREME leaky gut â€“ so bad that I couldnâ€™t tolerate hardly any foods / supplements for a very long time

â€¢ Fat malabsorption

â€¢ Oxalate issues

â€¢ Histamine reactions â€“ couldnâ€™t have bone broths, cured meats, avocados, etc.

â€¢ Rosacea â€“ I look like a tomato when Iâ€™m hot! A tomato with really bad acne.

â€¢ Bizarre facial swelling

â€¢ Havenâ€™t had TOM since Feburary and only had it once in the 10 months prior to that

â€¢ Low body temps

â€¢ Low blood pressure

â€¢ Always freezing

â€¢ All kinds of problems with my teeth after having 8 amalgams removed â€“ infections, general pain, etc, etc.

â€¢ Mold issues â€“ triggered breathing problems; eventually did some remediation in my basement that helped a good bit with this

Iâ€™m sure thereâ€™s more, but thanks to the memory loss and impaired cognitive function thatâ€™s all I can remember right now!!

So, what Iâ€™ve done:

â€¢ Started leptin rx on 1/1/12

â€¢ Started CT in February â€“ currently do 5-6 baths/week at 50 degrees

â€¢ Eat a super clean diet following the AI protocol

â€¢ Consulted with Dr. K â€“ best decision yet!

â€¢ Found a decent doctor that will actually work with me

â€¢ Started taking pregnenolone and DHEA

And now Iâ€™m well on my way to optimal!!

Improvements Iâ€™ve been noticing:

â€¢ Sleeping much better, but I still wake not feeling totally rested and very stiff/sore in my hips

â€¢ I think Iâ€™m tolerating more foods finally! My diet has been so restricted Iâ€™m ready to add a bit of variety!

â€¢ Definite upswing in energy â€“ I think itâ€™s related to the DHEA that Dr. K recommended I increase during my consult

â€¢ Muscle weakness is very mild these days, if at all

â€¢ Just had a meeting at work and I rocked it, so Iâ€™m hoping the cognitive function is coming back. The last few years Iâ€™ve totally laid low on the job because I couldnâ€™t follow half of what was happening and didnâ€™t want to show everyone just how stupid Iâ€™d become, but I had confidence today and spoke up about an issue and got major positive feedback! Canâ€™t wait to feel productive again : )

â€¢ Just a general sense of well being. I didnâ€™t realize how â€˜downâ€™ I felt all the time until lately when I just feel positive and want to share it with everybody

â€¢ Skin is much smoother and I barely break out anymore unless Iâ€™m having a rosacea flare, which unfortunately still happens frequently

â€¢ I seem to be tolerating cold temps a bit better too . . . though that may have something to do with the heat wave weâ€™ve been experiencing lately!

**My Plan to continue to get my health back:**

â€¢ Implement the things Dr. K discussed during my consult:

o I ordered T3 â€“ just need to wait for it to get here from Mexico!

o Still working on my doc to prescribe some HC

o Continuing to increase my DHEA

o Adding a Brazil nut a day for selenium

o Getting my iodine tested â€“ if needed, add it

o Just ordered Mg Threonate

â€¢ Continuing with the baths 5-6 times/week

â€¢ Getting in a little extra CT by swimming 2-3 times/week - although the water is close to 80 degrees

Yes, I wrote a book, but thatâ€™s all I got for now. Stay tuned for any updates

~ B

### Eve — 2012-07-04T03:09:14+0300
